title: Pulmonary haemorrhage and extensive arterial thrombosis with anabolic steroid abuse
summary: This case report documents severe complications from anabolic steroid abuse, demonstrating that while these drugs commonly cause thromboembolic events, they rarely trigger diffuse alveolar hemorrhage—a potentially fatal complication. A patient presented with shortness of breath and hemoptysis accompanied by bilateral patchy lung infiltrates, subsequently developing extensive arterial thrombosis affecting the aorta and bilateral lower extremities, leading to right foot ischemia. The clinical course was further complicated by progressive rhabdomyolysis that necessitated intubation and ultimately bilateral amputation. This case highlights an unusual but severe manifestation of anabolic-androgenic steroid abuse, illustrating how these agents can precipitate both pulmonary hemorrhage and widespread vascular thrombosis simultaneously. The combination of diffuse alveolar hemorrhage with extensive arterial thrombosis represents a rare and particularly dangerous adverse effect profile that clinicians should recognize in patients with anabolic steroid exposure. These findings underscore the serious vascular and pulmonary toxicity risks associated with anabolic steroid abuse beyond the more commonly reported thromboembolic complications.

title: Ultrasound and MRI-based investigation of the role of perfusion and oxygen availability in menstrual pain
journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology
summary: This ultrasound and MRI study challenges the prevailing hypothesis that dysmenorrhea results from uterine ischemia, revealing instead that pain-free controls actually experience greater reductions in uterine perfusion and oxygenation during menstruation than women with dysmenorrhea. Using R2* imaging as a marker of tissue oxygen availability, the researchers found that both dysmenorrhea patients and controls showed increased R2* values (indicating relative ischemia) in the myometrium and endometrium during menses, yet dysmenorrhea participants paradoxically maintained higher uterine oxygen availability. Naproxen sodium, an NSAID that inhibits COX-2-mediated prostaglandin synthesis, differentially modulated these hemodynamic parameters depending on pain status: it increased endometrial R2* and resistance index in dysmenorrhea patients while decreasing pulsatility and resistance indices in pain-free controls. The findings suggest that dysmenorrhea involves dysregulated uterine hemodynamics and potentially altered hypoxia-inducible factor signaling rather than simple ischemia, and that protective COX signaling in pain-free individuals may prevent pain development. These results indicate that menstrual pain pathophysiology involves complex interactions between vascular perfusion, oxygen availability, and inflammatory mediators that extend beyond traditional ischemia-based mechanisms, potentially explaining why standard NSAID treatments remain ineffective for many patients.

title: Pregnancy Rates Among Women Treated with Medication for Opioid Use Disorder
summary: This study examined how different medications for opioid use disorder (MOUD) affect pregnancy rates in women capable of conception. Methadone and buprenorphine were both associated with reduced conception rates compared to no medication treatment, with methadone showing a stronger suppressive effect (55% reduction) than buprenorphine (16% reduction). In contrast, extended-release depot naltrexone was associated with higher odds of conception, while oral naltrexone showed no significant difference in conception rates. These findings suggest that the choice of MOUD medication has variable effects on fertility outcomes, with implications for counseling women with opioid use disorder about reproductive options and family planning. The differential effects across medication types highlight the importance of individualized treatment decisions that account for reproductive goals alongside addiction management.

title: Management of Abnormal Uterine Bleeding on Anticoagulation: The Patient-Clinician Perspective
journal: Journal of thrombosis and haemostasis : JTH
summary: This article examines the management of heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) in anticoagulated patients, highlighting a critical clinical challenge where anticoagulation therapy paradoxically increases bleeding risk while patients require thromboembolism prevention. Anticoagulants, particularly rivaroxaban, significantly increase HMB incidence compared to other anticoagulants like dabigatran, which may actually reduce bleeding rates relative to warfarin and low-molecular-weight heparin. HMB-associated iron deficiency causes substantial morbidity including fatigue, cognitive impairment, and hair loss, severely compromising quality of life and potentially increasing recurrent venous thromboembolism risk up to five-fold. Combined hormonal contraceptives offer therapeutic advantages by effectively reducing HMB, dysmenorrhea, and endometriosis while preventing certain cancers, though they carry increased VTE risk—a challenging trade-off in anticoagulated populations. First-line alternatives for HMB management include tranexamic acid, long-acting reversible contraceptives like levonorgestrel intrauterine devices, and iron supplementation. The article emphasizes that clinician-patient shared decision-making is essential given the competing risks of thromboembolism recurrence versus anticoagulation-induced hemorrhage.

title: To Evaluate the Efficacy and Safety of Mifepristone in Reducing the Size & Symptoms of Uterine Leiomyoma/Fibroids
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: This prospective clinical study demonstrates that mifepristone, a progesterone receptor modulator, offers significant promise as a non-surgical pharmacological treatment for uterine fibroids (leiomyomas). The drug substantially reduced uterine volume by 75% while simultaneously alleviating the primary clinical symptoms associated with fibroids, including pelvic pain and abnormal uterine bleeding. Beyond fibroid size reduction, mifepristone improved hemoglobin levels from 9 gm/dL to 12.51 gm/dL over six months, directly addressing the anemia commonly resulting from heavy menstrual bleeding. Pain intensity, measured by Visual Analog Scale scores, showed progressive improvement over the three-month observation period. These findings collectively establish mifepristone as an effective alternative to surgical intervention for managing symptomatic uterine fibroids, with favorable effects on both structural disease burden and systemic hematologic parameters. The safety and efficacy profile positions this agent as a valuable option for women seeking conservative management of this common gynecological condition.

title: Experiences of “Antara”: The Injectable Contraceptive in Rural Indian Women Presenting to a Tertiary Care Hospital of Eastern India
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: This study evaluates the clinical experiences and acceptability of Antara (depot medroxyprogesterone acetate/DMPA), an injectable contraceptive used in rural Indian women. While DMPA is an effective contraceptive, the study found that irregular menstruation and amenorrhea were the primary reasons women discontinued the medication, highlighting significant tolerability concerns in this population. Multiple sociodemographic factors were associated with discontinuation, including lower educational status, poorer socioeconomic conditions, and initiation of Antara during the interval period rather than postpartum. The fear of side effects emerged as a major driver of treatment discontinuation among users. These findings suggest that while DMPA offers reliable contraceptive efficacy, patient counseling regarding menstrual changes and tailored counseling for disadvantaged populations may improve continuation rates in rural settings. The results underscore the importance of informed consent and expectation management when prescribing hormonal contraceptives in resource-limited healthcare contexts.

title: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors for premenstrual syndrome and premenstrual dysphoric disorder
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary: This Cochrane systematic review demonstrates that selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs)—including fluoxetine, paroxetine, sertraline, escitalopram, and citalopram—significantly reduce symptoms of both premenstrual syndrome (PMS) and premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD), conditions characterized by mood disturbances, irritability, anxiety, and depression triggered by hormonal fluctuations during the menstrual cycle. The mechanism underlying SSRI efficacy involves inhibiting serotonin reuptake, thereby enhancing serotonin availability for mood regulation. Continuous SSRI administration proves more effective than luteal phase-only dosing for symptom reduction. However, SSRIs carry multiple adverse effects including nausea, sexual dysfunction, insomnia, asthenia, tremor, sweating, gastrointestinal disturbances, and cognitive impairment. Alternative treatment options for PMS and PMDD include lifestyle interventions, hormonal contraceptives containing drospirenone, acupuncture, and herbal medicines, which may be considered when SSRIs are poorly tolerated or contraindicated.

title: Effects of Repeated Treatment with the Monoacylglycerol Lipase Inhibitor MJN110 on Pain-Related Depression of Nesting and Cannabinoid 1 Receptor Function in Male and Female Mice
journal: The Journal of Pharmacology and Experimental Therapeutics
summary: MJN110, a monoacylglycerol lipase inhibitor, increases endogenous 2-arachidonoylglycerol levels, which acts as a high-efficacy agonist at cannabinoid 1 and 2 receptors to produce analgesic effects. In studies of pain-related behavioral depression using an intraperitoneal acid-induced nesting model, acute MJN110 treatment provided partial but significant pain relief. However, repeated MJN110 treatment revealed sex-dependent differences in efficacy, producing sustained but weak antinociception in male mice while inducing tolerance development in females. The analgesic effects were associated with modest cannabinoid 1 receptor desensitization that varied by brain region and sex, suggesting that receptor adaptation limits the sustained therapeutic potential of this approach. These findings indicate that while MAGL inhibition offers promise for pain management, the emergence of receptor desensitization and sex-dependent tolerance may constrain long-term therapeutic utility in treating pain-related behavioral dysfunction.

title: Bradykinin Receptors Play a Critical Role in the Chronic Post-ischaemia Pain Model
summary: This study demonstrates that bradykinin receptors (B1 and B2) play a critical role in chronic post-ischemic pain development and maintenance through inflammatory signaling pathways. Bradykinin antagonists effectively mitigated mechanical allodynia and paw edema when administered through multiple routes, though they did not affect cold allodynia, suggesting selective involvement in specific pain modalities. Conversely, bradykinin receptor agonists enhanced pain responses, and captopril blocked the anti-allodynic effects of receptor antagonists, indicating that bradykinin signaling through the renin-angiotensin system is essential for pain perpetuation. Gene expression inhibition of bradykinin receptors in the spinal cord reduced nociceptive behavior, establishing the receptor pathway as a therapeutic target. These findings suggest that bradykinin receptors contribute to the pathogenesis of complex regional pain syndrome type I (CRPS-I) and represent a potential pharmacological target for managing chronic pain conditions through selective receptor antagonism.

title: Beyond Birth Control: The Neuroscience of Hormonal Contraceptives
summary: Hormonal contraceptives, commonly prescribed for endometriosis management, exert significant effects on the brain through multiple neurobiological pathways that remain incompletely characterized. These medications are associated with mood disorders and dysregulate the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, thereby altering stress response mechanisms. The adolescent brain demonstrates particular sensitivity to hormonal contraceptives, undergoing significant structural changes during treatment with these agents. Additionally, hormonal contraceptives may potentiate ischemic brain damage, especially when combined with other risk factors such as smoking-derived nicotine exposure. These findings highlight that the neurological impact of hormonal contraceptives extends beyond their intended reproductive effects and warrants further investigation to optimize their use in endometriosis treatment while minimizing adverse neuropsychiatric outcomes.

title: A Prospective Study of the Relationship of COVID-19 Vaccination to Menstrual Cycle Characteristics in Adolescent Girls
journal: The Journal of adolescent health : official publication of the Society for Adolescent Medicine
summary:
# Summary

This prospective study examined menstrual cycle changes in adolescent girls following COVID-19 booster vaccination, finding that recipients experienced shorter menstrual cycles averaging 5.35 days shorter in the post-booster period compared to pre-booster cycles. The timing of booster administration within the menstrual cycle phase significantly influenced outcomes, with vaccination during the follicular phase associated with the most substantial cycle shortening, and the effect being most pronounced in the second post-booster cycle. Psychological stress emerged as an independent factor associated with both shorter cycles and increased menstrual symptoms, with higher stress scores predicting greater symptom severity regardless of vaccination status. The study suggests that disruption of follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) regulation through the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, combined with intense immune and inflammatory responses to the mRNA vaccine and psychological stress, may mechanistically explain the observed menstrual disturbances. While menstrual flow and pain were not significantly altered by vaccination, the findings indicate that vaccine administration timing and baseline stress levels are important modulators of menstrual cycle changes in adolescent populations.

title: Menstruation-Related Angina—The Wee Hours
journal: The International Journal of Angiology : Official Publication of the International College of Angiology, Inc
summary: This article examines menstruation-related angina and cardiac syndrome X, two conditions characterized by chest pain that stem from decreased estrogen levels affecting cardiovascular tissues. Estrogen acts through genomic and nongenomic pathways on cardiomyocytes, endothelial cells, and smooth muscle cells; when estrogen levels decline during menstruation or perimenopause, this protective signaling is disrupted, resulting in catamenial angina and cardiac syndrome X, respectively. Catamenial angina presents as recurrent nonexertional left-sided chest pain during menstruation, accompanied by diaphoresis, hot flushes, and persistent lethargy, while cardiac syndrome X occurs in perimenopausal and postmenopausal women and involves anginal-type retrosternal chest pain with normal coronary angiography findings. Pharmacological management of both conditions relies on nitrates, beta-blockers, and analgesics to control symptoms and provide hemodynamic relief. These findings highlight the critical role of estrogen in maintaining cardiovascular homeostasis and suggest that estrogen-related variations throughout the menstrual cycle and menopause significantly influence anginal symptom expression in susceptible women.

title: Breast Cancer in Users of Levonorgestrel-Releasing Intrauterine Systems
summary:
# Summary

This Danish registry-based study examined breast cancer risk in users of levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems (LNG-IUS), devices commonly used for contraception and endometriosis management. The research found a significant association between continuous levonorgestrel-IUS use and increased breast cancer risk. The study leveraged comprehensive nationwide registry data to establish this epidemiological relationship with high confidence. These findings are relevant to clinical decision-making regarding endometriosis treatment options, as LNG-IUS devices are frequently prescribed for this condition due to their local hormonal effects. The association warrants careful consideration of individual patient risk factors when selecting among available pharmacological management strategies for endometriosis.

title: Recent Trends in Medical Management of Endometriosis
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary:
# Summary

Modern medical management of endometriosis employs a multi-tiered pharmacological approach targeting pain and disease progression through distinct mechanisms. First-line treatments include NSAIDs combined with hormonal agents such as progestogens and combined oral contraceptive pills to reduce pain symptoms. Advanced therapies include GnRH agonists and antagonists like leuprolide acetate, as well as newer oral agents like elagolix, which provide symptom relief through gonadotropin-releasing hormone pathway modulation. Selective progestin receptor modulators (mifepristone and ulipristal acetate), progestin-receptor-specific compounds like dienogest, and aromatase inhibitors represent additional therapeutic options that suppress estradiol-mediated disease while improving quality of life and reducing endometriosis-associated pelvic pain. Selective estrogen receptor modulators (tamoxifen, raloxifene, and bacidoxifen) demonstrate anti-proliferative effects on endometriotic implants, while the levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system offers a non-systemic option for patients not seeking conception. Collectively, these pharmacological strategies reflect the shift toward personalized medicine in endometriosis management.

title: Uterine-Conserving Treatment Options for Atypical Endometrial Hyperplasia and Early Endometrial Cancer
summary:
# Summary

Atypical endometrial hyperplasia (AEH) is a precursor lesion with significant potential to progress to endometrioid endometrial cancer, with multiple risk factors including obesity, unopposed estrogen exposure, polycystic ovary syndrome, tamoxifen exposure, and hyperinsulinemia. Progesterone therapy is the cornerstone of uterine-conserving treatment, with megestrol acetate and medroxyprogesterone acetate as first-line oral progestins, while levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine devices offer localized delivery with improved tolerability. Metformin enhances progesterone sensitivity and improves insulin resistance through reduced gluconeogenesis and circulating insulin levels, making it a valuable adjunct to hormonal therapy, while gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists and aromatase inhibitors such as letrozole and anastrozole provide alternative treatment options. Progesterone receptor expression correlates with treatment response and prognosis, whereas overexpression of growth factors including transforming growth factor alpha and epidermal growth factor receptor—along with activation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway—contribute to progestin resistance. Weight loss exceeding ten percent of body weight substantially improves progestin therapy response, while obesity and polycystic ovary syndrome paradoxically increase recurrence risk despite treatment, underscoring the importance of metabolic management alongside pharmacological intervention.

title: Bullous pemphigoid treated with baricitinib as steroid-sparing therapy for a patient with uncontrolled diabetes
summary: Bullous pemphigoid (BP) is an autoimmune blistering disease driven by autoantibodies (BP180 and BP230) targeting the basement membrane and triggering type 2 inflammation, which promotes Th17 cell differentiation and IL-17 production. This inflammatory cascade activates neutrophils to release elastase, contributing to dermal-epidermal junction degradation and subepidermal blister formation. Baricitinib, a JAK 1/2 inhibitor, effectively treats BP by blocking Th17 differentiation and downstream inflammatory signaling, achieving clinical remission with rapid improvement in pruritus and skin lesions within one week. A notable advantage of baricitinib as steroid-sparing therapy is its ability to improve glycemic control, making it particularly beneficial for BP patients with uncontrolled diabetes who cannot tolerate the hyperglycemic effects of high-dose corticosteroids. Other JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, upadacitinib) and alternative agents including dupilumab, immunosuppressants, and tetracycline antibiotics provide additional treatment options, though JAK inhibition represents a mechanistically targeted approach addressing the Th17-mediated pathogenesis of BP.

title: Non-inferiority study to compare the efficacy of relugolix with dienogest for endometriosis-associated pain and usefulness of administering relugolix prior to dienogest (READY study): study protocol for a multicenter randomized controlled study
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent disease characterized by ectopic endometrial growth that causes dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. This non-inferiority trial (READY study) compares relugolix, a non-peptide GnRH antagonist, with dienogest, a selective progestin, for treating endometriosis-associated pain. Relugolix directly antagonizes GnRH receptors to suppress luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone secretion, thereby reducing estradiol and progesterone levels without the initial symptom flare-up or bone loss associated with GnRH agonists. Dienogest, the current first-line therapy, acts selectively on progesterone receptors to suppress ovarian function and endometrial proliferation but frequently causes abnormal uterine bleeding through endometrial decidualization. The study investigates whether sequential administration of relugolix prior to dienogest could thin the endometrium and reduce abnormal bleeding complications while maintaining efficacy for pain reduction. Both agents show distinct advantages and adverse event profiles that may influence treatment selection and sequencing strategies for endometriosis management.

title: Therapeutic selection for abnormal uterine bleeding with ovulatory dysfunction: a cross-sectional study in Chinese women
summary:
# Summary

Abnormal uterine bleeding with ovulatory dysfunction (AUB-O) is a common condition in Chinese women affecting approximately 50% of abnormal uterine bleeding cases, characterized by irregular menstruation, dysmenorrhea, and heavy menstrual bleeding that can lead to anemia and infertility. This cross-sectional study identified multiple therapeutic approaches, with combined oral contraceptives (COCs)—particularly newer formulations like Yasmin I and II containing drospirenone and ethinyl-estradiol—demonstrating high efficacy and patient acceptance across reproductive and senior age groups. COCs work through estrogen-mediated endometrial repair and suppression of hyperplasia, while progestins and synthetic progesterone replicate the luteal phase hormonal environment to transform the endometrium and promote coordinated shedding. Alternative treatments including levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems and GnRH agonists are reserved for refractory cases or patients without fertility requirements, while curettage therapy is increasingly selected with advancing age, particularly in women over 45 with long-term irregular bleeding and endometrial cancer risk factors. The study highlights that drospirenone-containing COCs offer distinct advantages through anti-mineralocorticoid and anti-androgen activity compared to older testosterone-derived progestins, though careful patient selection is essential given contraindications including cardiovascular disease, thrombogenic conditions, and hepatic disease.

title: Tumor Necrosis Factor-Alpha Inhibitor Use and Malignancy Risk: A Systematic Review and Patient Level Meta-Analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis examined malignancy risk associated with TNF-α inhibitors, a class of immunosuppressive medications widely used to treat chronic inflammatory disorders. TNF-α inhibitors work by blocking the binding of soluble TNF-α to its receptor, which suppresses downstream production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, chemokines, and adhesion molecules while reducing peripheral blood mononuclear cell activation. The analysis of pooled patient data from multiple studies found that overall TNF-α inhibitor exposure was not significantly associated with increased cancer risk, though non-melanoma skin cancers emerged as the most robustly documented malignancy type. Individual TNF-α inhibitors—including adalimumab, infliximab, etanercept, certolizumab pegol, and golimumab—showed variable malignancy rate ratios with overlapping confidence intervals, indicating no definitive individual drug signal. While 146 malignancies were documented over 15,120 patient-years of exposure, representing diverse cancer types including lymphomas, lung, breast, and colorectal cancers, the authors note that chronic immunosuppression theoretically increases malignancy risk through decreased immune surveillance. The findings suggest TNF-α inhibitors maintain a favorable safety profile for treating inflammatory conditions, though continued monitoring for non-melanoma skin cancer remains warranted.

title: Stroke and myocardial infarction with contemporary hormonal contraception: real-world, nationwide, prospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This nationwide prospective cohort study examined the cardiovascular risk associated with contemporary hormonal contraception methods, finding that most formulations significantly increase the risk of arterial thrombotic events. Combined oral contraceptives containing ethinyl estradiol and progestin were associated with elevated rates of both ischemic stroke and myocardial infarction, with dose-dependent effects observed at lower estrogen doses. Non-oral combined products, particularly the vaginal ring and hormonal patch, demonstrated even higher thrombotic risks, likely due to higher plasma estrogen levels achieved through transdermal or vaginal delivery. Progestin-only methods (pills, implants) also showed increased arterial thrombotic risk in a dose-dependent manner, suggesting progestin-associated effects beyond estrogen-mediated mechanisms. Notably, the levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device showed no increased risk for either ischemic stroke or myocardial infarction, making it a safer alternative for women with thrombotic risk factors. These findings underscore the importance of individualizing hormonal contraceptive selection based on baseline cardiovascular risk profiles.

title: Effects of Biased Analogues of the Kappa Opioid Receptor Agonist, U50,488, in Preclinical Models of Pain and Side Effects
summary:
# Summary

Kappa opioid receptor (KOR) agonists effectively treat multiple pain conditions, including paclitaxel-induced neuropathic pain, acute thermal and mechanical pain, and cancer-related pain through G-protein signaling pathways and modulation of neuroinflammation. While traditional KOR agonists like U50,488 produce potent antinociceptive effects, they cause significant adverse side effects including sedation, anxiety, dysphoria, motor incoordination, and conditioned place aversion, limiting their clinical utility compared to mu opioid agonists. This study identified Compound 3, a biased KOR agonist analog of U50,488, which reversed paclitaxel-induced neuropathic pain and mechanical/thermal allodynia without inducing any notable side effects, sedation, anxiety-like behavior, motor impairment, or place aversion. Surprisingly, signaling bias toward G-protein pathways did not correlate with either antinociceptive efficacy or side effect profiles, and knockout studies revealed that β-arrestin2 recruitment is not responsible for U50,488-induced adverse effects. These findings demonstrate that Compound 3 represents a promising therapeutic candidate for chronic pain management with a superior safety profile, though the mechanisms underlying its selective lack of side effects remain to be clarified.

title: The role of different LNG-IUS therapies in the management of adenomyosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis examines the efficacy of levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) therapies for adenomyosis management. The LNG-IUS treats adenomyosis through sustained local release of progesterone, which inhibits endometrial growth by downregulating estrogen and progesterone receptors, thereby reducing menstrual bleeding and pelvic pain. LNG-IUS demonstrates superior efficacy compared to alternative progestin therapies like etonogestrel, achieving greater reductions in uterine volume and fewer adverse effects such as weight gain. Combination therapies enhance treatment outcomes substantially: GnRH-agonist pretreatment reduces LNG-IUS expulsion risk and improves symptom resolution for dysmenorrhea and abnormal bleeding; combined surgical excision or focused ultrasound ablation with LNG-IUS prevents adenomyosis recurrence more effectively than surgical intervention alone; and GnRH-agonist plus LNG-IUS significantly reduces lesion volume, uterine dimensions, and endometrial thickness. Additionally, LNG-IUS shows promise for improving menstrual patterns, pregnancy rates, and implantation outcomes in adenomyosis patients, making it a cornerstone therapy either as monotherapy or in combination with other surgical or pharmacological interventions.

title: A systematic review and meta-analysis comparing the use of elagolix therapy alone or in combination with add-back therapy to treat women with uterine fibroid associated heavy menstrual bleeding
summary:
# Summary

Elagolix, a nonpeptide GnRH receptor antagonist, effectively treats heavy menstrual bleeding associated with uterine fibroids by competitively inhibiting GnRH signaling, thereby suppressing luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone and reducing estradiol and progesterone production. Meta-analysis demonstrates that elagolix alone or combined with add-back therapy significantly reduces menstrual blood loss, improves hemoglobin levels, induces amenorrhea, and enhances quality of life compared to placebo. However, the therapeutic benefit must be weighed against substantial adverse effects, particularly elagolix monotherapy inducing dose-dependent bone mineral density loss across multiple skeletal sites—lumbar spine, total hip, and femoral neck—a hypoestrogenic effect that is reversible upon discontinuation. Combination therapy with estradiol and norethindrone acetate add-back substantially mitigates bone density reduction while maintaining efficacy in symptom control and improving health-related quality of life. Both treatment strategies carry increased risks of vasomotor symptoms (hot flushes), though these are more pronounced with elagolix monotherapy, yet clinically significant endometrial abnormalities do not occur with either regimen. These findings support elagolix with add-back therapy as the preferred approach for managing uterine fibroid-related heavy menstrual bleeding, balancing symptom control with bone health preservation.

title: Seeking help for menstrual pain during adolescence: A mixed-methods retrospective survey of treatment experiences and preferences
summary: Menstrual pain affects approximately 93% of adolescent females, with 43% experiencing moderate-to-severe dysmenorrhea that significantly impairs school attendance, academic performance, participation in extracurricular activities, and psychological health. Untreated menstrual pain during adolescence is associated with higher rates of chronic pain persisting into adulthood, affecting roughly three-quarters of affected adolescents. While NSAIDs and acetaminophen serve as first-line pharmacological treatments and hormonal contraceptives are prescribed when initial therapies fail, many adolescents experience limiting side effects including gastrointestinal distress, nausea, vomiting, and headaches from NSAIDs, and mood changes, weight gain, fatigue, and migraines from oral contraceptives. Non-pharmacological approaches such as topical heat, exercise, and dietary modifications represent alternative or complementary options that some adolescents prefer. Critically, involving adolescent patients in treatment decision-making processes significantly improves treatment compliance, patient satisfaction, and overall outcomes, suggesting that individualized, preference-informed treatment strategies are essential for effective menstrual pain management in this population.

title: Hormonal Contraceptive Use and Affective Disorders: An Updated Review
summary:
# Summary

Hormonal contraceptives alter endogenous sex steroid hormone levels and their signaling pathways, producing complex effects on women's mental health through multiple biological mechanisms. The evidence indicates that hormonal contraceptive use is associated with both increased depression risk—particularly in adolescents during the first two years of use and in women with ADHD—and improved mood in specific populations such as those with premenstrual depression. The neurobiological mechanisms underlying these effects include dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, altered amygdala structure and function with increased vigilance to negative emotional stimuli, disrupted diurnal cortisol rhythms, and elevated chronic inflammation. Estradiol, the primary endogenous estrogen, influences mood through heightened threat perception and upregulation of inflammatory processes, while synthetic progestins in contraceptives bind multiple steroid hormone receptors (progesterone, androgen, glucocorticoid, and mineralocorticoid receptors) with affinities diverging from natural progesterone. Age at contraceptive initiation and pre-existing mental health history emerge as critical risk factors predicting depression, with protective effects observed when hormonal formulations reduce natural hormone fluctuation. These findings underscore that hormonal contraceptive effects on mood vary substantially by individual characteristics and contraceptive formulation, requiring personalized clinical consideration.

title: The effect of dienogest and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist on pelvic pain after laparoscopic surgery for endometriosis: An RCT
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition that causes chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dyschezia, with potential long-term complications including infertility, fibrosis, and adhesions. This randomized controlled trial compared two leading medical therapies—gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonists and dienogest—in patients undergoing laparoscopic surgery for endometriosis management. Both treatments significantly reduced pelvic pain and related symptoms (p < 0.0001), with GnRH agonists suppressing gonadotropin secretion and estrogen production to induce medical menopause, while dienogest moderately inhibits the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and reduces estrogen levels through antiproliferative, anti-inflammatory, and anti-angiogenic mechanisms. However, GnRH agonists produced more severe adverse effects including hot flashes (>60%), bone loss (20–60%), joint pain, and vaginal dryness, while dienogest was associated with decreased libido but demonstrated better tolerability due to its anti-androgenic profile. These findings suggest that while both agents effectively manage endometriosis-related pain postoperatively, dienogest may offer a more favorable safety profile for long-term use compared to GnRH agonists.

title: Galactocele Following Aesthetic Breast Augmentation: Diagnosis, Management, and Prevention
summary:
# Summary

Galactocele formation following aesthetic breast augmentation involves multiple interconnected risk factors related to surgical technique, hormonal medications, and patient lactation history. Periareolar incisions and retroareolar glandular disk removal carry particularly high risks for ductal obstruction and galactocele development, while inframammary fold incisions are implicated in a smaller subset of cases. Hormonal factors substantially contribute to pathogenesis, including oral contraceptives and norethisterone increasing galactocele risk, postoperative hormonal injections, and medications such as metoclopramide, antihypertensives (verapamil, methyldopa, reserpine), and antidepressants that promote galactorrhea through elevated prolactin levels. Recent breastfeeding history and higher parity increase susceptibility to these complications. Diagnosis employs breast ultrasound, SUDAN IV staining to confirm milk presence, and serum prolactin measurement with brain MRI to exclude prolactin-secreting tumors. Treatment focuses on dopamine agonists, particularly bromocriptine and cabergoline, which effectively suppress lactation in patients with elevated prolactin levels, while preventive strategies include careful surgical planning and patient education regarding medication selection and lactation timing relative to surgery.

title: Long-Term Outcomes of Implanon in Managing Adenomyosis: A 3-Year Prospective Study
journal: Medical Science Monitor: International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
summary: This three-year prospective study demonstrates that the Implanon subdermal implant, which releases the progestin etonogestrel, is effective for treating adenomyosis while providing reliable contraception. Adenomyosis causes chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and heavy menstrual bleeding; etonogestrel suppresses these symptoms by inhibiting the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, increasing cervical mucus viscosity, and reducing endometrial thickness, thereby inhibiting adenomyosis development. Among 124 subjects, Implanon achieved 100% contraceptive efficacy with no unintended pregnancies, and patients with adenomyosis showed markedly higher continuation rates (88.6% at three years) compared to those without adenomyosis (41.8%), with lower removal risk and greater satisfaction. While early adverse effects included bleeding abnormalities (62.9% at three months), amenorrhea, and local irritation, these complications declined substantially over time, with bleeding-related problems decreasing to 10.84% by 24 months. Older age (≥35 years) and parity were associated with improved continuation rates, suggesting Implanon is particularly suited for adenomyosis management in this demographic. The study establishes etonogestrel implants as a superior long-acting option for adenomyosis treatment, offering durable symptom relief with high patient satisfaction despite initial tolerability concerns.

title: Etonogestrel Subdermal Implant in Adolescents: Everything We Should Know to Conduct Proper Counseling, a Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

The etonogestrel (ENG) subdermal implant is a long-acting reversible contraceptive that operates through multiple mechanisms: it suppresses ovulation by inhibiting gonadotropin secretion and follicular maturation, while also thickening cervical mucus and altering the endometrium to create an inhospitable environment for implantation. As a LARC method, the ENG implant is approximately 40 times more effective than oral contraceptives at preventing unintended pregnancies in adolescents and substantially reduces maternal and neonatal mortality and unsafe abortions. Beyond contraception, the implant provides endometrial protection through suppression of proliferation, thinning of the endometrial lining, and reduction of estrogen receptor activity, making it valuable for adolescents with conditions like PCOS. Common side effects include abnormal uterine bleeding (particularly in younger, lower BMI individuals with elevated etonogestrel serum levels), mood disturbances, headaches, breast pain, and acne worsening, though the implant does not affect future fertility. Comprehensive contraceptive counseling is essential to facilitate informed decision-making and improve sexual and reproductive health outcomes among adolescents, while also addressing concerns about insertion-related anxiety and potential complications.

title: Central Serous Chorioretinopathy in Endometriosis Treatment with Progestogen: A Metabolic Understanding
summary:
# Summary

Dienogest, a progestin widely used for endometriosis treatment, works by binding progesterone receptors and creating a hypoestrogenic, hypergestagenic environment that inhibits endometrial-like tissue proliferation and angiogenesis. While highly effective at reducing endometriosis-related pelvic pain and dysmenorrhea, this article presents an unusual case of central serous chorioretinopathy (CSC) developing after 21 months of estradiol valerate/dienogest therapy, manifesting as visual disturbances including diplopia and metamorphopsia. The mechanism underlying this ocular complication involves dysregulation of hormone receptor signaling in the retina and choroid, where progesterone, estrogen, and androgen receptors are normally present and regulate vascular permeability and inflammation. Elevated prolactin levels and hormonal imbalance from chronic steroid exposure disrupted normal vascular homeostasis, leading to serous neuroretinal detachment detected on optical coherence tomography. Notably, cessation of dienogest therapy resulted in complete regression of CSC within 15 days, establishing a direct causal relationship. This case highlights an underrecognized metabolic consequence of progestin-based endometriosis treatment and suggests clinicians should monitor for ocular symptoms in patients receiving long-term dienogest therapy.

title: Real-World Effectiveness, Safety, and Tolerability of Facilitated Subcutaneous Immunoglobulin 10% in Secondary Immunodeficiency Disease: A Systematic Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Facilitated subcutaneous immunoglobulin 10% (fSCIG 10%) is an effective treatment for secondary immunodeficiency disease (SID), a condition arising from extrinsic factors such as hematological malignancies, transplantation, immunosuppressive agents, and protein loss. Secondary immunodeficiency manifests as hypogammaglobulinemia and low serum immunoglobulin G levels, predisposing patients to severe, recurrent infections. The fSCIG 10% formulation utilizes recombinant human hyaluronidase to increase subcutaneous tissue permeability, enabling administration of larger immunoglobulin volumes and producing more stable serum IgG levels with prolonged intravascular absorption compared to intravenous immunoglobulin. Clinical evidence demonstrates that fSCIG 10% significantly reduces acute serious bacterial infections, increases serum IgG concentrations, and improves patient quality of life and treatment satisfaction, with the majority of patients reporting better efficacy against infections and enhanced overall outcomes. The treatment demonstrates excellent safety and tolerability, with rare and typically mild adverse reactions including low-grade fever, headache, local edema, and infusion site inflammation, making it a favorable option for immunodeficiency management across diverse patient populations.

title: Assessment of Women’s Awareness of the Possible Risk of Stroke Associated with the Use of Oral Contraceptives Pills in Saudi Arabia: A Cross-Sectional Study
summary:
# Summary

Oral contraceptive pills (OCPs), which contain estrogen and progestins, work by suppressing follicle-stimulating hormone to inhibit ovulation, while offering therapeutic benefits including treatment of dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, and endometriosis-associated pain, as well as protection against endometrial and ovarian cancers. However, OCPs carry significant cardiovascular risks, particularly estrogen-induced elevation of coagulation factors (II, VIII, fibrinogen) that promote thrombus formation and increase the risk of both ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke through cerebral thrombosis. Estrogen activates nuclear receptors and downstream signaling pathways (MAPK and IP3 kinase) that enhance gene transcription and blood coagulability, mechanisms underlying the documented association between OCP use and venous thromboembolism, cardiovascular events, and breast and cervical cancer risk. Despite these well-established risks, the study found that over two-thirds of Saudi Arabian women were unaware that OCP use increases stroke risk, with weight gain being the most commonly recognized side effect. The findings emphasize the importance of patient awareness regarding appropriate contraceptive selection, routine blood pressure monitoring, and lifestyle modifications as strategies to mitigate stroke risk among OCP users.

title: A Pelvic Mass in a Young Patient With Crohn's Disease
summary: This case report describes a rare coexistence of Crohn's disease and unicentric Castleman disease in a young patient with endometriosis, presenting with a left pelvic sidewall mass. Both conditions share a common pathogenic mechanism centered on interleukin-6 (IL-6), a proinflammatory cytokine involved in immune dysregulation, suggesting IL-6 as a potential therapeutic target for both diseases. The patient's Crohn's disease was managed with conventional therapies including mesalamine, adalimumab, and methotrexate, while the Castleman disease diagnosis prompted consideration of IL-6-targeted immunotherapy. The report highlights that anti-IL-6 monoclonal antibodies, particularly siltuximab, represent first-line therapy for idiopathic multicentric Castleman disease, and experimental IL-6 inhibitors like tocilizumab and olamkicept show efficacy in Crohn's disease management, though IL-6 inhibition carries risks of gastrointestinal perforation and profound immunosuppression. This case underscores the clinical importance of recognizing IL-6-mediated pathways in distinguishing and potentially unifying treatment approaches for these overlapping inflammatory conditions.

title: Hydroxychloroquine: A double‑edged sword (Review)
summary: Hydroxychloroquine functions as an immunomodulatory agent with broad therapeutic potential in autoimmune and inflammatory conditions through multiple mechanistic pathways. The drug exerts its effects primarily by inhibiting toll-like receptors 7 and 9, suppressing B and T cell activation, and blocking the classical complement pathway, thereby reducing production of complement fragments C3a and C5a and multiple proinflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IFN-γ, IL-1, IL-6, and IL-17. These mechanisms translate into clinical efficacy across rheumatologic diseases, with documented benefits in systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, antiphospholipid syndrome, and Sjögren's syndrome, resulting in reduced disease activity, improved organ function, and decreased mortality. Notably, hydroxychloroquine reduces endometriosis risk in systemic lupus erythematosus patients and provides cardiovascular and renal protective effects by inhibiting platelet aggregation, neutrophil extracellular trap formation, and vascular inflammation. However, the drug's clinical utility is constrained by significant adverse effects including gastrointestinal complications and serious cardiotoxicity manifesting as conduction disorders and structural heart disease with prolonged use, necessitating careful risk-benefit assessment and monitoring.

title: Association of fluoroquinolones with the risk of spontaneous pneumothorax: nationwide case–time–control study
summary:
# Summary

This nationwide case-time-control study demonstrates that fluoroquinolone use is associated with an increased risk of spontaneous pneumothorax, with an adjusted odds ratio of 1.59 compared to control periods. The proposed mechanism involves fluoroquinolone-induced collagen disruption of the lung connective tissue, wherein these antibiotics reduce synthesis of collagen types I and III by upregulating matrix metalloproteinases, potentially compromising the structural integrity of pulmonary tissues that normally contain these critical collagen components. The study identifies a broader collagen toxicity profile for fluoroquinolones affecting multiple tissues, with documented associations to tendon rupture, aortic aneurysm, aortic dissection, and retinal detachment. However, the findings also suggest that the infection itself—rather than the antibiotic alone—may be the primary driver of pneumothorax risk, as amoxicillin use showed an even higher association (odds ratio 2.25), pointing to infections requiring antibiotic intervention as the key predisposing factor. Pre-existing lung abnormalities including blebs, bullae, and emphysema-like changes, combined with smoking status, appear to interact with these effects to promote spontaneous pneumothorax occurrence. These results indicate that fluoroquinolones should be prescribed judiciously given their potential to compromise pulmonary connective tissue integrity in susceptible patients.

title: Hereditary Angioedema with Normal C1 Inhibitor: an Updated International Consensus Paper on Diagnosis, Pathophysiology, and Treatment
summary:
# Summary

Hereditary angioedema with normal C1 inhibitor (HAE-nC1INH) comprises a heterogeneous group of disorders caused by pathogenic variants in genes including F12, PLG, ANGPT1, KNG1, MYOF, HS3ST6, CPN1, and DAB2IP that dysregulate the kallikrein-kinin system, leading to excessive bradykinin production and increased vascular endothelial permeability. Unlike classical C1INH-deficient HAE, patients with HAE-nC1INH characteristically show no response to conventional therapies such as antihistamines, corticosteroids, or epinephrine, but respond to targeted treatments including C1INH concentrates, bradykinin B2 receptor antagonists, plasma kallikrein inhibitors, and fibrinolytic agents like tranexamic acid. HAE-nC1INH displays distinct clinical features including lower disease penetrance, later symptom onset, female predominance, slower attack progression, and frequent abdominal or airway involvement. Estrogen exposure significantly triggers attacks in multiple HAE-nC1INH subtypes, particularly HAE-FXII and HAE-HS3ST6, with estrogen-containing contraceptives and pregnancy identified as important clinical risk factors. The expanded recognition of HAE-nC1INH genetic variants and their bradykinin-mediated pathophysiology has transformed understanding of angioedema diagnosis and enabled targeted therapeutic approaches superior to traditional allergy-based treatments.

title: Long-term clinical outcomes of dienogest for perimenopausal women with symptomatic adenomyosis
summary: Dienogest, a progestin with high selectivity for progesterone receptors, effectively treats dysmenorrhea and menorrhagia in perimenopausal women with symptomatic adenomyosis, with 56% of patients achieving excellent pain control. However, long-term treatment success is significantly limited by baseline clinical characteristics: older age, elevated pre-treatment CA-125 levels (≥98.8 U/mL), and larger uterine volumes (>352.7 cm³) predict treatment failure and discontinuation. The most common reasons for stopping dienogest include uncontrolled bleeding, which increases with larger uterine size and severe pre-treatment anemia, as well as side effects such as headaches and depression that are more frequent in older women. Adenomyosis causes invasion of endometrium into myometrium and is frequently associated with coexisting myomas and ovarian cysts, complicating management in the perimenopausal period when fluctuating estrogen levels promote hormonal complications. Younger patients with smaller baseline uteri and lower CA-125 levels demonstrate superior long-term outcomes and better symptom control with dienogest therapy, while older women more readily transition to surgical intervention when medical management fails.

title: Development of a Long-Acting Follicle-Stimulating Hormone Using Serum Albumin Fab-Associated Technology for Female Infertility
summary:
# Summary

This study describes the development and evaluation of SAFA-FSH, a long-acting follicle-stimulating hormone formulation using serum albumin Fab-associated technology designed to treat female infertility. SAFA-FSH effectively activates the FSH receptor pathway by inducing cAMP synthesis in ovarian granulosa cells and upregulating key steroidogenic enzymes (aromatase/hCYP19α1 and hSTAR), leading to robust dose-dependent increases in estradiol production comparable to recombinant human FSH (rhFSH). In animal studies, SAFA-FSH demonstrated superior pharmacokinetics with a 2.7-fold longer serum half-life than rhFSH, resulting in greater increases in serum estradiol levels, ovarian weight, and follicle recruitment with less frequent dosing. Although SAFA-FSH showed slightly lower in vitro potency than rhFSH, its prolonged half-life and extended duration of action make it a promising alternative for treating ovulatory disorders and endometriosis-related infertility, potentially reducing treatment burden and injection frequency compared to current therapies.

title: Effects of Opioids in Cancer Pain: An Interplay Among Genetic Factors, Immune Response, and Clinical Outcomes—A Scoping Review
summary:
# Summary

This scoping review examines the complex interplay between opioid pharmacotherapy, genetic factors, and immune responses in cancer pain management. While adequate pain control improves quality of life and clinical outcomes, prolonged opioid exposure presents significant immunological trade-offs: opioids inhibit T cell receptor signaling, reduce CD8+ T cell and natural killer cell activity, and impair macrophage and neutrophil function, thereby increasing infection risk including herpes simplex virus reactivation and hepatitis C. Genetic polymorphisms in the OPRM1 gene and other opioid receptors influence individual pain perception and analgesic efficacy, with μ-opioid receptor activation potentially promoting tumor proliferation and immunosuppression through central sensitization and microglial activation. The review identifies nociceptin/orphanin FQ (NOP) receptor agonists and bifunctional ligands combining NOP and μ-opioid pathways as promising alternatives, offering enhanced analgesia while potentially mitigating immune suppression and providing anxiolytic benefits without the severe immunological complications associated with conventional opioid therapy in cancer patients.

title: A New Hope for Woman with Vasomotor Symptoms: Neurokinin B Antagonists
summary:
# Summary

This article examines neurokinin B (NKB) antagonists as a novel pharmacological approach to treat vasomotor symptoms in menopausal women. KNDy neurons in the hypothalamus—which comprise kisspeptin, neurokinin B, and dynorphin—play a critical role in both thermoregulation and reproductive hormone release. During menopause, estrogen withdrawal leads to dysregulation and overactivation of these neurons, triggering excessive NKB signaling that manifests as hot flashes and night sweats. NK3 receptor antagonists, particularly fezolinetant (FDA-approved in 2023) and elinzanetant, selectively block NKB signaling to restore normal KNDy neuron activity and re-establish thermoregulatory stability. These agents effectively reduce vasomotor symptom frequency and severity in clinical trials, with elinzanetant additionally improving sleep disturbances through dual NK1/NK3 receptor inhibition. The mechanistic understanding of the KNDy-NKB pathway represents a significant advance in endocrine therapy, opening new therapeutic avenues beyond traditional hormone replacement approaches.

title: Nanomaterials for liver cancer targeting: research progress and future prospects
summary:
# Summary

This article reviews nanomaterial-based approaches for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) treatment, a disease with multifactorial etiology including hepatitis B and C virus infections, aflatoxin exposure, alcoholic cirrhosis, and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease. Current therapeutic strategies employ tyrosine kinase inhibitors (sorafenib, lenvatinib, regorafenib, cabozantinib) and immune checkpoint inhibitors (atezolizumab, nivolumab, pembrolizumab) that target tumor proliferation and angiogenesis through inhibition of signaling pathways and growth factor receptors, though these agents cause significant adverse effects including hypertension and elevated transaminase levels. A major clinical challenge is drug resistance driven by hypoxia and adipose tissue accumulation, which modulate hypoxia-inducible factors and glucose transporters. Nanomaterial delivery systems—particularly liposomes, silica nanoparticles, and graphene nanomaterials—show promise in overcoming resistance by improving drug bioavailability and tumor accumulation via the enhanced permeability and retention effect. Paclitaxel-loaded liposomes exemplify this approach, demonstrating anti-angiogenic efficacy through inhibition of endothelial proliferation and tumor growth. FDA-approved polymeric nanoparticles such as polylactide and poly(lactic acid-glycolic acid) provide additional platforms for sustained drug delivery while preventing therapeutic loss.

title: Population characteristics of intrauterine device users in real-world clinical practice across Europe – insights from the EURAS-LCS12 study
summary:
# Summary

The EURAS-LCS12 study characterizes European intrauterine device (IUD) users across five device types, revealing distinct demographic and clinical patterns. Levonorgestrel-releasing IUDs (LNG-IUDs) across all dose formulations—LNG-IUD 8, 12, and 20 µg/day—effectively treat contraception and heavy menstrual bleeding, with LNG-IUD 20 additionally providing endometrial protection during hormone therapy. User populations differ significantly by device type: younger, nulligravid, single women predominantly select lower-dose LNG-IUD 8, while older women with completed family planning and higher income preferentially use the higher-dose LNG-IUD 20. LNG-IUD 20 users show higher associations with endometriosis and benign uterine tumors, while all IUD formulations demonstrate comparable safety profiles. These real-world prescription patterns align with clinical assumptions about tailoring IUD selection to individual reproductive goals and medical histories, establishing evidence-based guidance for device selection in diverse patient populations.

title: Moderate Associations Between the Use of Levonorgestrel-releasing Intrauterine Device and Metabolomics Profile
journal: The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism
summary:
# Summary

This metabolomic study demonstrates that levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device (LNG-IUD) use is associated with a cardioprotective metabolic profile characterized by lower concentrations of multiple lipid species, including fatty acids, cholesterol, triglycerides, and lipoprotein particles, particularly in larger very-low-density lipoprotein subclasses that carry high cardiovascular risk. LNG-IUD users also exhibited reduced levels of glycoprotein acetyls, an inflammation marker predictive of diabetes and cardiovascular disease, suggesting a protective metabolic effect. Notably, longitudinal analysis over 11 years revealed no metabolic changes attributable to LNG-IUD use, and short-term and long-term users showed distinct patterns—with short-term use associated with decreased HDL cholesterol and long-term use with unchanged or increased HDL levels. The findings indicate that LNG-IUD use presents a benign or potentially beneficial metabolic safety profile without adverse cardiometabolic consequences, although the clinical significance of these relatively moderate associations warrants further investigation.

title: Lasmiditan and Different Triptans in Menstrual Migraine: A Bayesian Network Meta-analysis
summary: Menstrual migraine is a prevalent neurological condition affecting women, triggered by estrogen decline and elevated prostaglandins during the luteal phase and menstruation, resulting in longer duration and greater disability compared to non-menstrual migraine. A Bayesian network meta-analysis comparing acute pharmacological treatments found that lasmiditan, a selective 5-HT1F receptor agonist, demonstrated superior efficacy over traditional triptans in achieving sustained pain-free rates and 2-hour pain-free outcomes, while avoiding activation of the 5-HT1B receptor that causes triptan-associated vasoconstriction. Although triptans such as sumatriptan, almotriptan, frovatriptan, and naratriptan effectively treat menstrual migraine through 5-HT1B/1D receptor activation and CGRP inhibition, they carry higher adverse event risks, particularly rizatriptan. Almotriptan demonstrated the most favorable safety profile among conventional options, while lasmiditan offers a particularly advantageous alternative for patients with cardiovascular contraindications to triptans, though it may cause central nervous system side effects. Overall, the receptor selectivity of these compounds—with lasmiditan's exclusive 5-HT1F targeting versus triptans' broader serotonergic activity—explains their differential efficacy and safety profiles in managing menstrual migraine.

title: Three cases with chronic obsessive compulsive disorder report gains in wellbeing and function following rituximab treatment
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes three patients with chronic, treatment-resistant obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) who showed substantial clinical improvement following rituximab treatment, suggesting an underlying autoimmune etiology. All three cases demonstrated evidence of intrathecal antibody production and brain-reactive autoantibodies detected in cerebrospinal fluid, including elevated IgG indices, oligoclonal bands, and novel anti-neuronal antibodies that bound to mouse thalamic neurons. The patients exhibited distinct autoimmune profiles: Case 1 showed elevated calcium/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II activity; Case 2 had dopamine receptor D1 and beta-tubulin antibodies consistent with PANDAS (pediatric autoimmune neuropsychiatric disorders associated with streptococcal infection); and Case 3 had a family history of autoimmune conditions including endometriosis alongside elevated kinase activity. Rituximab, a B-cell depleting monoclonal antibody, produced sustained symptom reduction of 44-100% on standardized psychiatric rating scales over 2.5 years, accompanied by normalization of intrathecal antibody production and improvements in daily functioning and school/work attendance. These findings support the concept that a subset of treatment-resistant OCD cases represent autoimmune encephalitis amenable to immunotherapy, and suggest that specific clinical and biological markers may identify patients likely to respond to B-cell targeted therapy.

title: Efficacy of GnRH antagonists in the treatment of uterine fibroids: a meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis demonstrates that GnRH antagonists, including relugolix, elagolix, linzagolix, and cetrorelix, effectively treat uterine fibroids by rapidly inhibiting the pituitary-ovarian axis and reducing estrogen levels, resulting in clinically significant reductions in both fibroid volume (mean difference of −27.36%) and heavy menstrual bleeding compared to placebo. Unlike GnRH agonists, which cause an initial flare-up effect, GnRH antagonists provide rapid symptomatic relief and exhibit a more favorable side effect profile with reduced bone mineral density loss. Uterine fibroids, benign tumors originating from smooth muscle cells and fibroblasts, commonly cause abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, urinary symptoms, and infertility in approximately 30% and 27% of affected women respectively. While GnRH antagonists do induce hypoestrogenism as a limiting factor, they represent an important therapeutic option alongside other approaches including GnRH agonists, ulipristal acetate, and various surgical interventions such as myomectomy and hysterectomy. The evidence supports GnRH antagonists as an efficacious pharmacological treatment for uterine fibroids with favorable clinical outcomes.

title: Long-term follow-up of the effect of oral dienogest and dienogest/ethinylestradiol treatment on cell-free DNA levels in patients with deep endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic systemic inflammatory disease associated with elevated circulating cell-free DNA (cfDNA), which originates from neutrophil extracellular traps and cellular apoptosis. Both dienogest and dienogest/ethinylestradiol are effective first-line hormonal treatments that significantly reduce symptoms of deep endometriosis, including dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain, through multiple mechanisms including induction of endometrial apoptosis, suppression of cell proliferation, and modulation of inflammatory mediators. During long-term follow-up over 12 months, both treatments paradoxically increased cfDNA levels, likely reflecting treatment-induced apoptosis in endometrial lesions rather than disease progression. Beyond their direct pro-apoptotic effects, dienogest and estroprogestins reduce inflammation and angiogenesis by modulating prostaglandins, interleukins, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, and vascular endothelial growth factor, while blocking the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovary axis and decreasing aromatase expression. These findings support the clinical efficacy of these hormonal therapies while suggesting that elevated cfDNA during treatment reflects a beneficial therapeutic mechanism rather than worsening disease status.

title: Development of pharmaceutically scalable inhaled anti-cancer nanotherapy – Repurposing amodiaquine for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC)
journal: Materials science & engineering. C, Materials for biological applications
summary: This study demonstrates the repurposing of amodiaquine, an antimalarial drug, as a nanotherapeutic agent for non-small cell lung cancer treatment. Amodiaquine-loaded nanoparticles were optimized by incorporating polyethylenimine to enhance drug loading efficiency and controlling probe homogenization speed to achieve smaller particle sizes, which improved tumor penetration into spheroid cores. The nanoparticle formulation exhibited potent anticancer activity in NSCLC cell lines, significantly reducing IC50 values and inhibiting both cell migration and colony growth compared to free drug. The mechanism of action involves inhibition of autophagy (demonstrated by increased LC3B-II levels) and induction of apoptosis (elevated Caspase-3 levels), with nanoparticle encapsulation substantially amplifying these effects. These findings indicate that pharmaceutical nanoformulation of amodiaquine provides a scalable inhaled delivery strategy with enhanced efficacy for NSCLC management through multiple antiproliferative pathways.

title: Effectiveness of A Levonorgestrel-Releasing Intrauterine System Versus Hysteroscopic Treatment for Abnormal Uterine Bleeding in Women with Cesarean Scar Defects: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

Cesarean scar defects, which occur in 56–78% of women with prior cesarean delivery, are associated with abnormal uterine bleeding, postmenstrual spotting, secondary infertility, and chronic pelvic pain through incomplete healing and inflammatory processes at the surgical site. The levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device (LNG-IUD) treats these bleeding complications by inhibiting endometrial development and reducing vascularity through localized hormone release, resulting in significantly fewer total bleeding days and spotting days compared to hysteroscopic resection at both 6 and 12 months. By 12 months, the LNG-IUD demonstrated superior overall effectiveness with a threefold higher odds ratio, and approximately 50% of users experienced amenorrhea. Oral contraceptives offer an alternative pharmacological approach, effectively reducing abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic pain, and postmenstrual spotting through modulation of endometrial vascularization and hormonal regulation. The LNG-IUD is recommended for patients with cesarean scar defect-associated abnormal uterine bleeding who do not plan conception, though users should be informed of the high likelihood of amenorrhea development within 12 months.

title: Bone Health for Gynaecologists
summary:
# Summary

Osteoporosis, characterized by reduced bone mineral density and increased fracture risk, represents a significant health concern with multiple etiological pathways relevant to gynecological practice. Oestrogen deficiency drives postmenopausal and early-onset osteoporosis, with early menopause nearly doubling fracture risk compared to standard menopause, while surgical interventions such as bilateral oophorectomy cause rapid bone loss exceeding six percent annually. Several endometriosis treatments, notably dienogest and combined oral contraceptives, reduce bone mineral density, as do depomedroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA) and numerous systemic medications including corticosteroids, proton pump inhibitors, and anticonvulsants. Non-modifiable risk factors including low body mass index, early menopause, and genetic polymorphisms in the vitamin D3 receptor and oestrogen receptor genes contribute to osteoporosis susceptibility, while modifiable factors such as nutritional deficiencies, eating disorders, excessive alcohol and tobacco use, and sedentary behavior substantially increase risk. Bone health assessment employs trabecular bone score and quantitative ultrasonography alongside dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry, with adequate calcium and vitamin D supplementation forming the foundation of preventive management for gynecological patients at risk of accelerated bone loss.

title: Effectiveness of intrapartum azithromycin to prevent infections in planned vaginal births in low-income and middle-income countries: a post-hoc analysis of data from a multicentre, randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

This post-hoc analysis of the A-PLUS trial demonstrates that intrapartum azithromycin significantly reduces maternal infections in women planning vaginal delivery in low- and middle-income countries. The antibiotic reduced the incidence of endometritis, wound infection, pyelonephritis, and composite outcomes of infection with or without sepsis, with a relative risk reduction of approximately 42-78% across these specific conditions. However, azithromycin did not reduce maternal sepsis occurring in isolation without concurrent infection, nor did it prevent neonatal infections overall, though it did lower rates of neonatal skin infections. The prophylactic benefit was consistent across subgroups regardless of delivery type, labor characteristics, or prior antibiotic use, with stronger effects observed in African populations compared to Asian settings. Given that maternal infections account for approximately 11% of global maternal deaths, these findings support the use of single-dose intrapartum azithromycin as a cost-effective intervention to prevent postpartum morbidity in resource-limited settings.

title: Sequential therapy of dienogest following relugolix for adenomyosis and impact on symptoms and serum CA125 levels: a case series
summary:
# Summary

This case series demonstrates that sequential therapy combining relugolix followed by dienogest effectively manages adenomyosis and its associated symptoms. Adenomyosis causes severe pelvic pain, menorrhagia, and dysmenorrhea through mechanisms involving elevated estrogen levels and intrauterine inflammation. Relugolix, a GnRH antagonist, rapidly reduces estrogen levels and provides immediate symptom relief while normalizing elevated serum CA125 levels, a biomarker reflecting disease activity. Subsequent transition to dienogest, an oral progestin, maintains long-term symptom control and stabilizes CA125 levels. Imaging studies confirmed that relugolix reduces uterine volume and junctional zone thickness, indicating structural improvement in adenomyotic lesions. While relugolix causes transient menopausal side effects including hot flushes and insomnia, the sequential approach provides a novel therapeutic strategy addressing both acute symptom management and sustained disease control with fewer long-term adverse effects than GnRH agonist monotherapy.

title: Changes in Canadian contraceptive choices: results of a national survey on hormonal contraceptive use
summary:
# Summary

This Canadian national survey demonstrates that hormonal contraceptives serve multiple therapeutic roles beyond pregnancy prevention, effectively treating endometriosis, polycystic ovarian syndrome, menstrual disorders, and acne. The study reveals a significant shift in contraceptive preferences among Canadian women, with increased adoption of long-acting reversible contraceptive (LARC) methods, particularly intrauterine devices, alongside declining use of oral contraceptive pills. Hormonal contraceptive use is strongly associated with sociodemographic factors, including age, education level, household income, employment status, healthcare coverage, and ethnicity, suggesting that access and utilization patterns are influenced by socioeconomic determinants. Additionally, lifestyle factors such as exercise status correlate with contraceptive method selection, with physically active women showing greater preference for LARC methods. The findings highlight that oral contraceptive pill users experience elevated blood pressure compared to users of non-oral hormonal methods, and approximately 58% of users report employing these medications for non-contraceptive therapeutic purposes. This comprehensive analysis underscores the complex interplay between medical efficacy, socioeconomic access, and personal health behaviors in shaping contraceptive and endometriosis management strategies in Canada.

title: Efficacy and safety of hormone therapies for treating adenomyosis-associated pelvic pain: a systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
summary: Adenomyosis is an estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition of the uterus that causes chronic pelvic pain, abnormal uterine bleeding, and reproductive complications including infertility and recurrent pregnancy loss, significantly impairing quality of life. This network meta-analysis evaluated hormone therapies for adenomyosis-associated pelvic pain, including progestins, combined oral contraceptives (COCs), levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems (LNG-IUS), and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists and antagonists. Dienogest demonstrated superior efficacy for pain reduction at six months compared to combined oral contraceptives and LNG-IUS, though it was associated with higher adverse effects including irregular uterine bleeding and hot flashes, limiting its tolerability. Combined oral contraceptives emerged as a favorable alternative, offering effective pain reduction with the lowest incidence of adverse effects, while LNG-IUS devices provided good efficacy with intermediate safety profiles and improved tolerability through locally high hormone concentrations. The results indicate that hormone therapies effectively treat adenomyosis-associated pain, with dienogest offering the best efficacy-to-symptom-relief ratio but at the cost of tolerability, making COCs the preferred option when balancing efficacy and safety across patient populations.

title: Long-term outcomes of pharmacotherapy in patients with persistent postural-perceptual dizziness
summary:
# Summary

Persistent postural-perceptual dizziness (PPPD) is characterized by hypersensitivity to sensory input and involves maladaptive sensory reweighting, often accompanied by anxiety and neuroticism. Serotonergic antidepressants, including venlafaxine, escitalopram, vortioxetine, mirtazapine, and paroxetine, represent a major pharmacological treatment option for PPPD and produce significant improvements in vestibular symptoms, dizziness severity, and mental health measures (depression and anxiety) by three months, with benefits sustained long-term. However, antidepressants do not reduce somatosensory hypersensitivity, as evidenced by unchanged head-tilt perception gain measurements after treatment, suggesting this underlying sensory processing abnormality persists despite clinical symptom improvement. Patients with higher pretreatment dizziness handicap scores show better treatment response to antidepressants, though initial adverse effects including nausea and abdominal distension occur in roughly one-quarter of patients during the first two weeks. For non-responders to pharmacotherapy, cognitive-behavioral therapy represents an alternative therapeutic approach, highlighting the multimodal treatment strategy needed to address the complex symptomatology of PPPD.

title: Effective long‐term prophylaxis with lanadelumab in adolescents with hereditary angioedema: EMPOWER/ENABLE
summary:
# Summary

Hereditary angioedema with C1 inhibitor deficiency (HAE-C1INH) results from mutations in the SERPING1 gene that lead to reduced or dysfunctional C1INH protein, which normally inhibits plasma kallikrein activity. This deficiency triggers dysregulation of plasma kallikrein, excess bradykinin production, and subsequent activation of bradykinin B2 receptors, causing localized increases in vascular permeability and the characteristic edema of skin, abdominal, and potentially life-threatening laryngeal sites. Lanadelumab, a monoclonal antibody that inhibits plasma kallikrein, was evaluated in the EMPOWER and ENABLE studies for long-term prophylaxis in adolescents with HAE. Pooled analysis demonstrated that lanadelumab reduced monthly attack rates by 86.7% in newly treated patients (from 3.8 to 0.65 attacks/month) and maintained rates of 0.04 attacks/month in established patients, with sustained prevention achieving 71.1-94.7% reductions in different study phases. Treatment was well-tolerated with mostly mild-to-moderate adverse events, no injection site reactions, and no discontinuations due to safety concerns, establishing lanadelumab as an effective long-term prophylactic option for adolescents with HAE.

title: Clomiphene citrate reduces premature LH surge in obese women during controlled ovarian stimulation: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Obesity impairs hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian function, resulting in diminished basal levels of luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), reduced estrogen and progesterone production, and impaired ovarian responsiveness to gonadotropin stimulation during controlled ovarian stimulation. Clomiphene citrate, a selective estrogen receptor antagonist, counteracts these obesity-related deficits by suppressing estrogen's negative feedback on the hypothalamus, thereby stimulating pituitary gonadotrophin secretion and enhancing ovarian sensitivity. In obese women undergoing controlled ovarian stimulation, clomiphene citrate effectively controlled LH levels and prevented premature LH surges—a complication typically associated with the drug's ability to elevate LH concentrations—while requiring less gonadotropin exposure compared to standard protocols.
