The underlying mechanisms of obesity-induced hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction involve insulin resistance, elevated inflammatory factors, increased leptin levels, and decreased kisspeptin secretion, all of which clomiphene citrate's enhancement of pituitary sensitivity can partially compensate for. These findings indicate that clomiphene citrate represents a safer and more effective therapeutic approach for managing infertility in obese populations by addressing their specific neuroendocrine deficiencies.

title: Clinical efficacy of etonogestrel implants on relieving dysmenorrhea in endometriosis and adenomyosis women for up to 3 years
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis and adenomyosis are hormone-dependent conditions that cause dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility through mechanisms involving abnormal uterine contractions, nerve fiber proliferation, and localized inflammation. Etonogestrel implants, a progestin-based contraceptive, effectively treat both conditions by inhibiting ovulation and inducing endometrial atrophy, resulting in significant symptom relief with dysmenorrhea severity decreasing from moderate-severe in 50% to 16% of patients over three years. The implants reduce menstrual bleeding and induce amenorrhea while serum CA125 levels—an elevated biomarker in adenomyosis produced by ectopic endometrial tissue—decrease following implantation, confirming therapeutic efficacy. Common adverse effects include weight gain (21%), acne (13%), and mood changes, with irregular bleeding and spotting occurring early after placement. These findings demonstrate etonogestrel implants as a viable long-term pharmacological option for endometriosis and adenomyosis patients who decline surgery or have persistent symptoms despite other treatments.

title: Online Misleading Information About Women’s Reproductive Health: A Narrative Review
summary: This narrative review examines widespread online misinformation about women's reproductive health, with particular relevance to endometriosis management and related gynecological conditions. The article identifies pervasive false claims linking hormonal contraception to weight gain, mental health issues, and infertility—despite evidence supporting contraceptive safety and efficacy for conditions like endometriosis and premenstrual dysphoric disorder. Beyond contraception, the review documents misleading narratives about vaccines, abortion, intrauterine devices, and unproven alternative treatments for endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and premature ovarian insufficiency, such as herbs and dietary interventions lacking scientific support. Notably, the review reveals that both lay online content and artificial intelligence systems like ChatGPT frequently exaggerate risks or promote medically unsupported procedures while simultaneously underrepresenting evidence-based pharmacological treatments. These findings underscore how online misinformation undermines patient trust in healthcare and may discourage evidence-based management of endometriosis and other reproductive conditions that benefit from hormonal and pharmacological therapies.

title: Preoperative medical therapy before surgery for uterine fibroids
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary:
# Preoperative Medical Therapy for Uterine Fibroids: Summary of Key Findings

Uterine fibroids are non-cancerous growths affecting up to 50% of women, causing heavy menstrual bleeding, anemia, pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and reduced fertility. Gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues (GnRHa) suppress pituitary-ovarian function to induce a low-estrogen state, effectively reducing uterine and fibroid volume by approximately 47%, while improving preoperative hemoglobin levels and reducing postoperative bleeding complications and morbidity. However, GnRHa treatment is limited to six months due to adverse effects including menopausal symptoms, hot flushes, and bone loss. Selective progesterone receptor modulators (SPRMs) represent an alternative preoperative therapy, substantially reducing uterine volume and increasing hemoglobin levels, though ulipristal acetate use is currently suspended due to rare but serious associations with liver failure. Both GnRHa and SPRM pretreatment reduce operative time and blood transfusion requirements, supporting their use as bridging therapies before surgical intervention, though patient tolerance of medication side effects must be carefully weighed against surgical benefits.

title: Suppression of an accessory and cavitated uterine mass with norethindrone: a case report
summary: Accessory and cavitated uterine masses (ACUM) are rare myometrial lesions that typically present in adolescents and nulliparous individuals with severe dysmenorrhea and chronic pelvic pain. While surgical resection remains the definitive treatment with high rates of symptom resolution, norethindrone acetate represents an effective non-surgical option for managing ACUM-related pain through menstrual suppression, achieving amenorrhea in 76% of patients at a 5 mg daily dose with a favorable side effect profile. In the case presented, norethindrone acetate treatment not only suppressed menses and eliminated pain but also led to imaging-documented reduction in ACUM size over one year, suggesting a potential direct effect on lesion progression. Initial medical management typically includes nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and oral contraceptive pills before diagnosis, though estrogen-containing products carry increased breakthrough bleeding risk. Pelvic ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging are essential for establishing an early diagnosis of ACUM, enabling timely intervention. For patients with ACUM seeking both symptom relief and contraception, continuous combination oral contraceptive pills can be considered as an alternative to progestin-only therapy.

title: Progestin‐only pills for contraception
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary: This Cochrane systematic review examines the comparative efficacy and safety of various progestin-only pill formulations for contraception. Among the progestin-only agents evaluated, levonorgestrel 30 μg demonstrated superior efficacy compared to alternatives such as norethisterone 350 μg and megestrol acetate, which showed lower contraceptive effectiveness. Desogestrel progestin-only pills were associated with increased bleeding problems relative to levonorgestrel formulations, though this difference did not reach statistical significance, while ethynodiol diacetate caused high rates of irregular menstrual cycles in all users. Low-dose mifepristone achieved pregnancy rates comparable to levonorgestrel progestin-only pills, suggesting comparable efficacy between these agents. Overall, the evidence indicates substantial variation in both efficacy and tolerability across different progestin-only contraceptive formulations, with levonorgestrel-containing pills emerging as a more reliable option regarding pregnancy prevention, though selection must consider individual tolerance for bleeding irregularities and cycle disturbances.

title: Emergency surgery for fibroid expulsion complicated by pelvic organ prolapse: a case report
summary: This case report describes a rare complication arising from pharmacological management of submucosal fibroids using relugolix, a GnRH antagonist. Submucosal fibroids cause abnormal uterine bleeding by protruding into the uterine cavity, and relugolix was administered to reduce fibroid size and control menstrual bleeding by suppressing estrogen and progesterone without inducing flare-up effects typical of GnRH agonists. However, despite its therapeutic benefits, the patient experienced severe uncontrollable vaginal bleeding on day 33 of relugolix treatment, requiring emergency surgery. During the surgical intervention, fibroid expulsion occurred, which was further complicated by the concurrent development of pelvic organ prolapse—a rare coincidence where weakened pelvic support structures, influenced by genetic predisposition, neuropathy, myopathy, childbirth, obesity, smoking, pulmonary disease, and constipation, led to descent of pelvic organs. This case highlights an important clinical consideration: while GnRH antagonists like relugolix are effective for treating uterine fibroids and controlling menstrual bleeding, their use in submucosal fibroids can paradoxically be associated with severe abnormal uterine bleeding requiring urgent intervention, and practitioners must remain vigilant for potential complications during medical management.

title: Comparative analysis of the clinical efficacy of conservative treatment for adenomyosis using traditional Chinese medicine and Western medicine
journal: Journal of Health, Population, and Nutrition
summary: Adenomyosis is an estrogen-dependent disease characterized by infiltration of endometrial glands and stroma into the myometrium, resulting in elevated uterine volume and elevated serum CA125 levels. The condition causes significant morbidity in women of childbearing age, presenting with dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, pelvic pain, and infertility. This comparative analysis evaluated three treatment approaches: the traditional Chinese medicine Dan'e Fukang Decoction, the progestin dienogest, and the combination of goserelin plus levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device (Mirena). Dan'e Fukang Decoction promoted blood circulation and demonstrated marked efficacy in reducing pelvic pain and uterine volume while substantially downregulating CA125 levels, with the added benefit of minimal adverse reactions. Both dienogest and goserelin plus Mirena effectively reduced menorrhagia through estradiol downregulation, though goserelin plus Mirena produced a significantly higher incidence of adverse effects including nausea, breast tenderness, headache, and gastrointestinal reactions, with additional risks of intrauterine device displacement. These findings suggest that traditional Chinese medicine offers a therapeutic alternative with comparable symptom relief and superior tolerability compared to conventional Western pharmaceutical approaches for adenomyosis management.

title: Effects of a Monophasic Hormonal Contraceptive With Norgestimate+Ethinyl Estradiol on Menstrual Bleeding: Protocol and Design of a Multicenter, Prospective, Open-Label, Noncomparative Study in Italy
summary: This study evaluates the menstrual bleeding profile and clinical outcomes of a monophasic combined oral contraceptive containing norgestimate (NGM) 0.25 mg and ethinyl estradiol (EE) 0.035 mg in Italian women. Norgestimate is a third-generation progestin with negligible androgenic activity that is rapidly metabolized to norelgestromin and levonorgestrel, maintaining progesterone-like activity while increasing sex hormone–binding globulin and reducing free testosterone levels. Large-scale European studies demonstrate that this monophasic NGM/EE formulation significantly reduces breakthrough bleeding and intermenstrual spotting while maintaining extremely high contraceptive efficacy, likely due to the dose optimization and the progestin's poor affinity for androgen receptors compared to earlier-generation progestins. Beyond menstrual regulation, monophasic NGM/EE may improve acne, mood changes, headaches, and pelvic pain, while carrying a lower venous thromboembolism risk than some alternative progestins. The formulation's negligible androgenicity distinguishes it from first- and second-generation progestins, which historically caused androgenic adverse effects such as acne, hirsutism, and oily skin. This protocol-based study aims to prospectively confirm these benefits in an Italian cohort while establishing the clinical utility of NGM/EE for managing menstrual disorders and associated symptoms.

title: Towards a more comprehensive neuroscience of hormonal contraceptives
summary:
# Summary

Sex steroid hormones exert complex regulatory effects on mammalian brain structure and function, and hormonal contraceptives correspondingly produce measurable neurobiological changes that vary substantially among individuals. Oral contraceptive use reduces grey matter volumes in the medial temporal lobe, specifically within the amygdala, parahippocampal cortex, and hippocampal CA2/3 subfield, and is associated with altered cortical thickness and task-related brain activation in some users. Beyond neuroscience effects, hormonal contraceptives effectively treat endometriosis, dysmenorrhea, and heavy menstrual bleeding while reducing risks of ovarian and endometrial cancer, though they carry cardiovascular risks including venous thromboembolism and may trigger mood disturbances, anxiety, depression, and other side effects in susceptible individuals. Contraceptive formulation influences specific cognitive domains, with effects on verbal memory and visuospatial abilities differing between users and non-users. These neurological and systemic effects occur within the context of normal brain plasticity induced by hormonal fluctuations during the menstrual cycle, pregnancy, and stress, underscoring the need for comprehensive understanding of individual variation in hormonal contraceptive response.

title: PREDICTing Post-Embolization Syndrome after uterine fibroid embolization: the PREDICT-PES study
summary: The PREDICT-PES study investigates post-embolization syndrome (PES), a clinically significant complication following uterine artery embolization for symptomatic fibroids, characterized by pelvic pain, nausea, fever, and malaise. The underlying mechanism involves ischemia-induced release of pro-inflammatory cytokines, particularly interleukin-6 and tumor necrosis factor-alpha, with larger embolic agent volumes and greater tissue ischemia correlating with increased pain severity. Nulliparity emerged as a strong independent predictor, with nulliparous patients experiencing 5.5-fold increased odds of clinically significant PES compared to parous women. Pharmacological management relies on NSAIDs as first-line agents due to their anti-inflammatory properties, supplemented by acetaminophen, opioid analgesics (morphine, oxycodone, tapentadol), and antiemetics (metoclopramide, ondansetron, dexamethasone) for symptom control. Emerging adjunctive approaches including glucocorticoids, muscle relaxants, superior hypogastric nerve blockade, and lidocaine show promise in reducing PES burden, though standardized protocols remain to be established. This research provides evidence-based guidance for predicting and managing post-embolization syndrome to improve patient outcomes following uterine fibroid treatment.

title: Metabolic and transcriptional effects of bazedoxifene/conjugated estrogens in a model of obesity-associated breast cancer risk
summary:
# Summary

Bazedoxifene/conjugated estrogens (BZA/CE) represents a promising therapeutic approach for managing obesity-associated breast cancer risk through multiple integrated mechanisms. This selective estrogen receptor modulator combined with conjugated estrogens significantly reduced body weight, adiposity, and visceral fat accumulation while simultaneously improving metabolic parameters including fasting glucose, plasma cholesterol, and insulin resistance in obese animal models. BZA/CE treatment favorably altered the gut microbiome by increasing beneficial metabolic bacteria (Faecalbaculum rodentium and Odoribacter laneus) while reducing potentially harmful species, with the enriched microbes correlating with reduced leptin levels and body fat. At the tissue level, BZA/CE reversed obesity-induced enrichment of mammary proliferation pathways and reduced estrogen receptor expression and cell proliferation in breast tissue, while promoting smaller, insulin-sensitive adipocytes. Unlike tamoxifen, which induces vasomotor symptoms and metabolic dysfunction, BZA/CE effectively relieved vasomotor symptoms while providing additional benefits through favorable modulation of circulating lipids, adipokines, and gene expression patterns associated with cancer risk reduction.

title: Reproductive Tract Bleeding in Adolescent and Young Adult Females with Inherited Bleeding Disorders: An Underappreciated Problem
journal: Journal of pediatric and adolescent gynecology
summary:
# Summary

Inherited bleeding disorders (IBDs) are significantly underappreciated causes of heavy menstrual bleeding in adolescents and young adults, affecting approximately 50% of women with menorrhagia and 20% of those with postpartum hemorrhage. These disorders, including von Willebrand disease, platelet dysfunction, and fibrinogen deficiencies, cause substantial morbidity through complications such as anemia, hypovolemic shock, and disseminated intravascular coagulation, with compounded risks during pregnancy including antepartum and postpartum hemorrhage. The endometrial coagulation system is tightly regulated by sex hormones—progesterone upregulates tissue factor and plasminogen activator inhibitor to promote hemostasis during fertile cycles, while progesterone withdrawal and estrogen-mediated increases in tissue plasminogen activator enhance fibrinolysis during menstruation, creating a critical imbalance in those with bleeding disorders. Diagnostic tools such as the ISTH-BAT score (≥6 in adult females) and PBAC score (>100) help identify at-risk patients, while treatment options range from first-line combined hormonal contraceptives and antifibrinolytics (particularly tranexamic acid, which reduces bleeding by ~50%) to specific factor replacements and intranasal desmopressin for von Willebrand disease. Pregnancy management in IBD patients requires careful monitoring and often prophylactic treatment with tranexamic acid and factor concentrates to prevent hemorrhagic complications, as coagulation factor levels change dramatically throughout pregnancy in unpredictable ways depending on the specific disorder.

title: Hormonal Treatment of Endometriosis: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary: Hormonal Treatment of Endometriosis

Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent benign condition causing a spectrum of debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, fatigue, and sexual dysfunction, affecting up to 30–50% of women with infertility. Progestins represent the cornerstone of medical management, exerting their therapeutic effects through multiple mechanisms: inducing decidualization and endometrial atrophy, suppressing gonadotropin release to create a hypoestrogenic state, and reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines and prostaglandins. Dienogest, a particularly effective progestin monotherapy, demonstrates superior long-term efficacy for pain reduction, shrinking endometriomas, and treating deep infiltrating endometriosis and adenomyosis while maintaining favorable bone density and improving quality of life over extended use. Combined oral contraceptives remain first-line therapy, especially in extended or continuous cycles for suppressing ovulation and reducing prostaglandin production, though they show limited efficacy for non-menstrual pain symptoms. Alternative hormonal approaches include the levonorgestrel intrauterine system for adenomyosis and abnormal bleeding, depot medroxyprogesterone acetate for ovulation suppression, and GnRH agonists or antagonists reserved for second-line use due to hypoestrogenism side effects, with surgical intervention integrated into comprehensive treatment plans.

title: Real-world effects of hyoscine butylbromide combined with paracetamol in women with dysmenorrhea: a patient survey
summary:
# Summary

Dysmenorrhea, a common condition frequently associated with endometriosis, involves a pathophysiological mechanism where increased prostaglandin formation leads to vasoconstriction, releasing anaerobic metabolites that stimulate pain receptors. The condition manifests with diverse symptoms including abdominal cramps, nausea, vomiting, headaches, and fatigue, significantly impairing quality of life and work productivity. This patient survey demonstrated that a fixed-dose combination of hyoscine butylbromide (10 mg) plus paracetamol (500 mg) effectively treats dysmenorrhea, with symptom relief achieved within 60 minutes in 85% of women and pain intensity reduced from 7.45 to 2.86 on a 0-10 scale. Beyond pain reduction, the combination therapy improved work/daily activities by 65%, leisure activities by 62%, and sleep quality by 70%, while also reducing concomitant headaches, palpitations, diarrhea, and breast pain. These real-world findings support the efficacy of this pharmacological combination as a practical treatment option for dysmenorrhea management, with benefits extending beyond pain relief to functional quality of life improvements.

title: Menstrual Cycle Length Changes Following Vaccination Against Influenza Alone or With COVID-19
summary:
# Summary

This cohort study of 1,501 participants with regular menstrual cycles examined associations between influenza and COVID-19 vaccinations and temporary menstrual cycle length changes. Both influenza vaccination alone and concurrent influenza-COVID-19 vaccination were associated with small but statistically significant increases in cycle length (0.40 and 0.49 days, respectively), with stronger effects observed when vaccination occurred during the follicular phase (0.82 and 0.99 days) compared to the luteal phase, where no meaningful changes occurred. The researchers propose that vaccine-triggered immune responses temporarily impact the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, the key neuroendocrine system regulating menstrual function. While these changes are biologically temporary and modest in magnitude, the study highlights that perceived menstrual disturbances following vaccination, even when small, contribute meaningfully to vaccine hesitancy among some individuals. The findings support close interaction between the immune and reproductive systems and suggest timing of vaccination relative to menstrual cycle phase influences the magnitude of cycle disruption.

title: Metformin in gynecological disorders: pathogenic insights and therapeutic implications
summary:
# Summary

Metformin, a widely used antidiabetic agent, demonstrates multifaceted therapeutic potential for endometriosis and other gynecological disorders through its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The drug operates via multiple molecular mechanisms, including inhibition of mitochondrial respiratory chain complex 1 and activation of AMPK and PI3K/mTOR signaling pathways, which together enhance insulin sensitivity, reduce reactive oxygen species, and improve mitochondrial function. In addition to treating endometriosis, metformin effectively addresses polycystic ovarian syndrome by reducing androgen levels through increased sex hormone-binding globulin production and direct suppression of ovarian steroidogenic enzyme activities. The drug also modulates inflammatory markers such as interleukin-6 and tumor necrosis factor-alpha, both of which are elevated in endometriosis, while reducing pregnancy complications including miscarriage, preeclampsia, and gestational diabetes mellitus. Furthermore, metformin exhibits anti-proliferative and anti-angiogenic properties alongside cardiovascular benefits through improved lipid metabolism and endothelial function, positioning it as a promising therapeutic agent for multiple gynecological pathologies characterized by metabolic dysfunction and chronic inflammation.

title: Systemic exogenous progestins with or without estrogens are associated with decreased rates of venous procedures for varicose veins
journal: Journal of Vascular Surgery: Venous and Lymphatic Disorders
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how systemic exogenous progestins, with or without estrogens, affect varicose vein disease and the need for surgical intervention. Both combined estrogen-progestin (CEP) therapy and progestin-only therapy significantly reduced rates of venous procedures for varicose veins, with progestin-only therapy showing the most dramatic reductions in stab phlebectomy, endovenous ablation, and sclerotherapy. The protective effect against varicose vein procedures contrasts with known risk factors for the condition, including female sex, pregnancy, obesity, and high estrogen states. While these hormonal therapies are often prescribed for endometriosis and menopausal symptoms, both demonstrated associations with deep vein thrombosis, highlighting a balance between symptom management and thromboembolic risk. The findings suggest that progestin-based therapies may offer clinical benefits for varicose vein management, though careful patient selection and monitoring remain essential.

title: The efficacy of progestins in managing pain associated with endometriosis, fibroids and pre-menstrual syndrome: a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review demonstrates that progestins are effective analgesics for endometriosis-associated pain through multiple complementary mechanisms. Progestins bind to progesterone receptors and suppress gonadotropin-releasing hormone, thereby suppressing ovulation and reducing menstrual cyclicity. Additionally, progestins exert direct anti-inflammatory effects by inhibiting cyclooxygenase-2 and downstream pro-inflammatory mediators including prostaglandins, interleukin-6, interleukin-8, and monocyte chemotactic protein-1, while also modulating nuclear factor-kappa B signaling. Across 18 of 19 endometriosis studies reviewed, progestins significantly reduced pain, with specific agents such as dienogest, levonorgestrel intrauterine systems, and depot medroxyprogesterone acetate demonstrating particular efficacy in chronic pelvic pain and dysmenorrhea. Importantly, progestin-only formulations offer a safety advantage over combined oral contraceptives by avoiding increased thrombosis risk while inducing endometrial tissue atrophy. These findings establish progestins as first-line medical therapy for endometriosis-related pain management, with efficacy extending to fibroid-related and premenstrual syndrome-related symptoms.

title: Intraoperative Clonidine in Spine Surgery: A Randomised Controlled Trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial evaluated intraoperative clonidine as an adjunct for postoperative pain management in spine surgery patients. Clonidine exerts analgesic effects by binding to alpha-2 adrenergic receptors at spinal and supraspinal levels, though the studied dose of 3 μg/kg did not significantly reduce postoperative pain intensity or opioid consumption compared to placebo. However, conflicting evidence from other studies demonstrates dose-dependent analgesic benefits with higher clonidine doses or continuous infusion regimens, suggesting efficacy is dependent on dosing strategy and route of administration. The primary limitation of clonidine in this trial was a significantly higher incidence of treatment-requiring hypotension in the clonidine group, along with other alpha-2 agonist side effects including bradycardia and sedation. These findings suggest that while clonidine has theoretical promise in multimodal pain management strategies, the risk-benefit profile and optimal dosing require further investigation to establish its role in routine perioperative analgesia for spine surgery.

title: Revisiting the role of steroidal therapeutics in the 21st century: an update on FDA approved steroidal drugs (2000–2024)
summary: This comprehensive review examines the therapeutic applications of FDA-approved steroidal drugs from 2000 to 2024, highlighting steroids as essential regulatory molecules with broad clinical utility. Steroids demonstrate high efficacy in treating multiple disease states, including cancer, inflammation, asthma, and microbial infections, underscoring their continued importance in modern pharmacotherapy. The review emphasizes the successful repurposing of specific steroid agents, particularly dexamethasone and hydrocortisone, which emerged as critical treatments during the COVID-19 pandemic. These findings demonstrate that despite their long clinical history, steroids remain among the most versatile and necessary therapeutic agents in contemporary medicine. The article provides an updated perspective on how steroid pharmacology continues to evolve and adapt to address emerging health challenges in the 21st century.

title: Promethazine for nausea and vomiting prevention after gynaecological laparoscopic surgery: A randomized controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial demonstrated that preoperative prophylactic promethazine administration significantly reduces postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) in women undergoing gynecological laparoscopic surgery, a procedure associated with PONV rates of 40–80%. Promethazine exerts its antiemetic effects through multiple mechanisms, including inhibition of emetic chemoreceptors and interactions with dopamine receptors and the vomiting center via its anticholinergic properties. Patients receiving promethazine showed substantially lower nausea and vomiting incidence at 6, 24, and 48 hours postoperatively, with reduced symptom severity and improved patient satisfaction compared to controls. The treatment is particularly valuable given that female patients, non-smokers, those with prior PONV history, and those receiving postoperative opioids face heightened risk of PONV, which can lead to serious complications including aspiration, alkalosis, increased intra-abdominal pressure, and potential wound rupture or bleeding. Promethazine demonstrated a favorable safety profile with minimal adverse effects, making it an effective prophylactic option for this high-risk patient population undergoing gynecological procedures including endometriosis treatment.

title: Psychopharmacological interventions among people who use Assisted Reproductive Technology (ART) — a scoping review
summary: This scoping review examines psychopharmacological interventions in individuals undergoing assisted reproductive technology (ART), addressing the significant psychiatric burden associated with infertility treatment. Anxiety and depression are widespread among ART patients, with infertility itself strongly associated with major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, and acute stress, particularly in women of reproductive age. Psychiatric medications were predominantly administered to treat depression (41% of studies) and anxiety disorders, with serotonin reuptake inhibitors (fluoxetine, sertraline, paroxetine) being the most frequently studied antidepressants, while GABA-positive allosteric modulators like midazolam and benzodiazepines were employed for anxiety management. Emotional distress emerges as a critical factor, with approximately 30% of couples discontinuing ART prematurely and low IVF success rates (around 30%) contributing substantially to psychological strain. The review highlights ongoing controversy regarding psychopharmacology use during infertility treatment due to concerns about potential effects on ART outcomes, underscoring the need for careful consideration of psychiatric medication management in this vulnerable population.

title: Effect of dexmedetomidine on the median effective concentration of ropivacaine for postoperative analgesia in transversus abdominis plane block: an up-down sequential allocation study
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated dexmedetomidine as an adjuvant to ropivacaine in transversus abdominis plane (TAP) blocks for postoperative pain management following laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Dexmedetomidine, a selective α2 adrenergic receptor agonist, significantly reduced the median effective concentration (EC50) and EC95 of ropivacaine required for analgesia, allowing lower local anesthetic doses while maintaining superior analgesic efficacy. The mechanism involves dexmedetomidine's inhibition of neuronal excitability through blockade of ion channels (HCN channels, K+ and Na+ currents), suppression of peripheral norepinephrine release, and anti-inflammatory effects including downregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines and upregulation of brain-derived neurotrophic factor. Addition of dexmedetomidine shortened the onset time, prolonged sensory and motor block duration, and reduced postoperative pain scores at 12, 24, and 48 hours while decreasing opioid requirements and improving overall quality of recovery. However, doses exceeding 1 μg/kg were associated with increased adverse effects including bradycardia and excessive sedation, establishing an optimal dose-response relationship for clinical application.

title: Effects of dienogest treatment on endometrioma-related clinical symptoms and endometrioma size: retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic estrogen-dependent disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue that causes significant clinical burden through chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. Dienogest, a fourth-generation selective progestin, treats endometriosis through multiple mechanisms including strong progestogenic activity, estrogen inhibition, anti-inflammatory effects, and antiproliferative and antiangiogenic properties that effectively reduce endometriotic lesion growth. This retrospective cohort study demonstrated that dienogest at 2 mg daily significantly reduced endometrioma size and associated symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and nonmenstrual pelvic pain within six months, with sustained or improved benefits continuing through long-term treatment beyond twelve months. Long-term postoperative dienogest treatment effectively prevented endometrioma recurrence, which occurs in up to 89.6% of cases after surgery alone. Dienogest showed comparable efficacy to combined oral contraceptives and GnRH agonists in pain relief while avoiding the bone density loss and hypoestrogenic symptoms associated with GnRHa therapy; however, common adverse effects included spotting, weight gain, and reduced libido, particularly with extended use. These findings establish dienogest as an effective first-line medical management option for endometriosis-related symptoms and lesion reduction.

title: Nanoemulsions Based Therapeutic Strategies: Enhancing Targeted Drug Delivery against Breast Cancer Cells
summary:
# Summary

Nanoemulsions represent a promising advancement in breast cancer therapeutics, addressing critical limitations of conventional treatments through enhanced drug delivery. These colloidal systems of nanoscale droplets (~100 nm) function by encapsulating therapeutic agents, significantly improving drug solubility, bioavailability, and absorption while simultaneously reducing systemic toxicity and off-target effects. Nanoemulsions induce apoptosis and inhibit angiogenesis in tumor microenvironments, with particular efficacy against drug-resistant cancers through mechanisms including the enhanced permeability and retention effect and ligand-functionalized active targeting. Multiple fabrication methods—including ultrasonication, phase inversion temperature, spontaneous emulsification, and high-pressure homogenization—enable precise control over droplet size and formulation properties. By protecting encapsulated drugs from enzymatic degradation and pH fluctuations, nanoemulsions overcome the bioavailability, cellular uptake, and acquired resistance challenges that plague conventional chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and surgery. These non-toxic, stable formulations represent a transformative strategy for treating all breast cancer subtypes, including triple-negative variants with otherwise poor prognoses.

title: Association between Extract Rheum rhaponticum 731 (ERr 731) prescription and subsequent breast cancer
summary: ERr 731, a specialized extract from rhapontic rhubarb roots, effectively treats menopausal symptoms including hot flashes and night sweats while demonstrating a favorable safety profile superior to traditional hormone replacement therapy. The extract functions as a selective agonist of estrogen receptor beta (ERβ) rather than the proliferation-promoting estrogen receptor alpha (ERα), and importantly, it does not increase circulating estrogen or progesterone levels nor cause endometrial changes. Large epidemiological studies found no association between ERr 731 prescription and increased breast cancer risk, with breast cancer cell proliferation studies confirming that ERr 731 does not stimulate growth of MCF-7 or MDA-MB-231 cancer cells despite its estrogenic activity. Beyond menopausal symptom relief, ERr 731 also reduces blood glucose levels and prevents serious adverse events, positioning it as a safer alternative to conventional HRT for postmenopausal women seeking symptom management without the breast and endometrial cancer risks associated with hormone-based therapies.

title: Vaginal Royal Jelly for Vulvovaginal Candidiasis Treatment: A Randomized Clinical Trial
summary: Vulvovaginal candidiasis, caused by *Candida albicans*, presents with genital itching, vaginal irritation, thick discharge, and dysuria. This randomized clinical trial compared vaginal royal jelly treatment to the standard topical antifungal clotrimazole, evaluating efficacy in reducing candidal infection and associated symptoms. Royal jelly, containing bioactive compounds with antimicrobial, antifungal, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and immune-modulating properties, effectively inhibited *Candida albicans* and significantly reduced itching, vaginal discharge, and burning symptoms over ten days of treatment. Both royal jelly and clotrimazole demonstrated comparable therapeutic efficacy in decreasing culture-positive results and alleviating vaginal symptoms, with royal jelly showing the additional benefit of no reported adverse effects, whereas clotrimazole was associated with side effects including itching, dysuria, and dermatitis. These findings support royal jelly as a potentially safer alternative to conventional antifungal therapy for treating vulvovaginal candidiasis while maintaining symptom relief and improving quality of life.

title: Efficacy and exploratory analysis of potential mechanisms of stellate ganglion block in alleviating sleep disturbance in generalized anxiety disorder: a randomized controlled trial excluding comorbid depression
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial demonstrates that stellate ganglion block (SGB), a neuroregulation procedure using local anesthetic injection, effectively treats generalized anxiety disorder and associated sleep disturbances more effectively than pharmacological treatment with estazolam and psychological intervention alone. The mechanism involves SGB suppressing sympathetic nervous system hyperactivity and modulating key neurotransmitter pathways—reducing norepinephrine while increasing serotonin and neuropeptide Y levels. Following four weeks of treatment, the SGB group showed superior clinical outcomes with 98.4% efficacy, greater reductions in anxiety and depression severity scores, and significant improvements in sleep quality metrics including Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index scores, total sleep time, and sleep efficiency. Beyond symptomatic relief, SGB reduced patient medication dependence while potentially influencing emotional regulation brain regions. These findings suggest that SGB offers a non-pharmacological alternative for managing anxiety-related sleep disturbances by restoring autonomic nervous system balance and regulating circadian rhythms.

title: Exploring the Potential of Pyridine Carboxylic Acid Isomers to Discover New Enzyme Inhibitors
summary:
# Summary

This article demonstrates that pyridine carboxylic acid isomers—particularly picolinic acid, nicotinic acid, and isonicotinic acid—represent a versatile scaffold for drug design, with pyridine being the second most common nitrogen heterocycle in FDA-approved pharmaceuticals. The pyridine core structure enables the development of therapeutics across diverse disease areas, including infectious diseases (tuberculosis, HIV, bacterial infections), oncology (cancer, leukemia), metabolic disorders (diabetes, pellagra), and inflammatory conditions. Notable examples include isoniazid and ethionamide for tuberculosis treatment, sorafenib and regorafenib for various cancers, nevirapine and saquinavir for HIV, and lasmiditan and ubrogepant for migraine management, among numerous others. Additionally, pyridine derivatives exhibit anti-inflammatory properties through CGRP antagonism and COX inhibition, suggesting potential applications in conditions characterized by inflammation. The article underscores how systematic exploration of pyridine isomers continues to yield novel enzyme inhibitors and therapeutically relevant compounds, positioning this chemical class as a cornerstone in contemporary drug discovery and development.

title: Safety and Tolerability of Relugolix in Combination with Abiraterone or Apalutamide for Treatment of Patients with Advanced Prostate Cancer: Data from a 52-Week Clinical Trial
summary:
# Summary

This 52-week clinical trial evaluated the safety and tolerability of relugolix, a small-molecule GnRH receptor antagonist, combined with either abiraterone or apalutamide for treating advanced prostate cancer. Relugolix works by competitively binding the GnRH receptor to suppress luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone, resulting in rapid testosterone suppression without the initial testosterone surge seen with GnRH agonists like leuprolide. When combined with abiraterone (which inhibits CYP17A-mediated testosterone biosynthesis) or apalutamide (an androgen receptor inhibitor), relugolix maintained mean testosterone concentrations below castrate levels and achieved median prostate-specific antigen concentrations of 0.04 ng/mL by week 12 in both treatment cohorts. Adverse events were predominantly grade 1-2, with hypertension and rash being the most frequently reported, while prednisone/methylprednisolone successfully mitigated mineralocorticoid-related side effects of abiraterone combination therapy. Notably, relugolix demonstrated superior efficacy and safety advantages over leuprolide, including higher castration rates, lower cardiovascular event incidence, rapid testosterone recovery after treatment cessation, and excellent patient adherence rates exceeding 97%.

title: Salsalate Negatively Impacts Microvascular Function in Women with Endometriosis
journal: American journal of physiology. Heart and circulatory physiology
summary: Endometriosis, a systemic inflammatory disease affecting approximately 10% of women, is an independent risk factor for cardiovascular disease and is associated with endothelial dysfunction characterized by decreased nitric oxide-mediated vasodilation. While statins have demonstrated success in improving endothelial function through their anti-inflammatory effects, this study revealed that salsalate, an oral nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug that inhibits nuclear factor κB, paradoxically impaired microvascular endothelial function in women with endometriosis despite reducing systemic inflammation. Specifically, salsalate attenuated cutaneous microvascular responses to acetylcholine and endothelium-dependent vasodilation through non-nitric oxide-dependent mechanisms, possibly by upregulating thromboxane-mediated vasoconstriction, while leaving macrovascular function and flow-mediated dilation unchanged. The findings suggest that salsalate-sensitive systemic inflammation does not directly drive microvascular endothelial dysfunction in this population, highlighting a disconnect between systemic inflammatory reduction and improvements in vascular function. These results indicate that pharmacological approaches targeting systemic inflammation in endometriosis may not uniformly benefit microvascular endothelial function and suggest that alternative mechanisms beyond NFκB inhibition may be more effective for treating endometriosis-associated cardiovascular complications.

title: Safety, Tolerability, and Pharmacokinetics of Filapixant, a Highly Selective P2X3 Receptor Antagonist, in an Ascending-Single-Dose First-in-Human Study
summary: Filapixant is a novel, highly selective antagonist of the P2X3 receptor, a key mediator of nociception that is activated by ATP to trigger action potentials in nerve fibers. P2X3 receptor blockade represents a promising therapeutic strategy for treating pain-related conditions including endometriosis, neuropathic pain, overactive bladder, and refractory chronic cough, as demonstrated by earlier P2X3 antagonists like gefapixant. This first-in-human study found that filapixant exhibited dose-proportional pharmacokinetics with a half-life of 10-15 hours supporting once-daily dosing, rapid absorption, and minimal renal excretion, with food intake having only modest effects on bioavailability. Although filapixant was generally well tolerated with no serious adverse events across all dose levels, taste-related side effects emerged as a significant concern, occurring in 37.7% of subjects and increasing substantially at higher doses (500-1250 mg), manifesting as dysgeusia, hypogeusia, and dose-dependent reductions in taste perception for bitter, salty, sour, and sweet qualities. These taste disturbances reflect P2X3 antagonism in taste bud P2X2/3 heteromers on the tongue and represent a critical consideration for clinical development, though the favorable safety and pharmacokinetic profile support further evaluation of filapixant at lower therapeutic doses for pain-driven indications like endometriosis.

title: Safety Profile of Gestrinone: A Systematic Review
summary: Gestrinone is a synthetic 19-nortestosterone derivative that treats mild to moderate endometriosis through multiple complementary mechanisms. The compound exhibits strong antiestrogenic and anti-inflammatory properties by binding with highest affinity to androgen receptors, moderate affinity to progesterone and estrogen receptors, and inhibiting aromatase to reduce circulating estrogen levels while suppressing the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. Gestrinone effectively reduces endometriotic lesion growth and associated symptoms including pelvic pain and abnormal bleeding, with additional applications demonstrated for uterine fibroids. However, the systematic review identified significant adverse effects including amenorrhea (41.4%), androgenic effects such as acne and seborrhea (42.7%), weight gain averaging 0.9-8 kg, reduced libido (26.5%), and bone mineral density abnormalities related to hormone deficiencies. Dose-dependent effects were observed, with the 2.5 mg regimen showing improved bone outcomes compared to lower doses, though higher doses (5.0 mg) increased the incidence of hot flushes (25.8%). Overall, while gestrinone remains clinically effective for endometriosis management, its safety profile warrants careful patient selection and monitoring for metabolic and hormonal side effects.

title: Advancing Brain Targeting: Cost-Effective Surface-Modified Nanoparticles for Faster Market Entry
summary:
# Summary

Surface-modified nanoparticles represent a promising approach to overcome the blood-brain barrier (BBB), which normally prevents drug passage through tight junctions between endothelial cells. The article demonstrates that nanoparticles conjugated with specific ligands—such as transferrin, Angiopep-2, or RGD peptides—promote active transport into the brain via receptor-mediated transcytosis and endocytosis, significantly improving drug bioavailability to CNS tissues. Multiple FDA-approved formulations including Lupron Depot (for endometriosis and prostate cancer) and metallic nanoparticles like NanoTherm (for glioblastoma) exemplify how polymer-based systems such as PLGA and lipid carriers enable controlled drug release while reducing systemic toxicity. Recent innovations including pH-sensitive and enzyme-responsive nanoparticles, exosome-coated carriers, and surface modifications with polyethylene glycol or zwitterionic coatings further enhance BBB penetration and reduce immunogenicity. Emerging techniques such as focused ultrasound, magnetic guidance, and tumor-treating fields offer complementary strategies to facilitate nanoparticle accumulation in brain tissues, positioning surface-modified nanoparticles as cost-effective platforms for accelerating development of therapeutics for brain disorders.

title: Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of 48-Week Low-Dose Dienogest Administration in Patients With Dysmenorrhea Caused by Endometriosis: Protocol for a Randomized, Open-Label, Parallel-Group Trial
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent disease that causes dysmenorrhea and significantly reduces quality of life, with increased incidence linked to delayed childbirth and nulliparity, while higher parity and lactation duration decrease risk. This randomized trial evaluates low-dose dienogest (1 mg/day versus 2 mg/day) for treating dysmenorrhea in patients with ovarian endometriosis over 48 weeks, measuring pain reduction via visual analog scale as the primary endpoint. Dienogest represents an advance over GnRH agonists and estrogen-progestin therapies by downregulating estrogen levels, enhancing progesterone receptor selectivity, and inhibiting endometrial cell proliferation while avoiding the thrombotic risks associated with combined hormonal contraceptives, making it suitable for patients over 40 years old. However, the dose-dependent reduction in bone mineral density with 2 mg/day dienogest—correlating with estrogen suppression—requires careful monitoring alongside potential vasomotor and mood-related adverse effects. Long-term hormone therapy with dienogest is essential for preventing endometriosis recurrence following surgical intervention, as resumed menstrual cycling substantially increases recurrence risk.

title: Motherhood and medicinal cannabis
summary:
# Summary

This review examines medicinal cannabis use among mothers, with particular attention to its therapeutic applications for endometriosis, pain management, and mental health conditions. Mothers reported that medicinal cannabis effectively treats endometriosis alongside anxiety, depression, post-traumatic stress disorder, and pre-menstrual dysphoric disorder, while also reducing physical symptoms including cramps, aching, and spasms. Real-world evidence demonstrates that cannabinoids reduce symptoms across multiple conditions including chronic pain, neuropathic pain, chemotherapy-induced nausea, and multiple sclerosis, with cannabidiol showing particular efficacy for seizure reduction in childhood epilepsy syndromes. Users report that medicinal cannabis improves emotional regulation, general functioning, sleep quality, and parental stress tolerance, with mothers noting enhanced parent-child relationships and better capacity to manage parenting demands. However, the review identifies significant concerns regarding cannabis use during pregnancy, which is associated with poor fetal neurodevelopment and low birth weight. Overall, while mothers experience substantial symptomatic relief and functional improvements with medicinal cannabis, particularly for endometriosis-related pain and mood disorders, careful consideration of reproductive risks remains essential in clinical practice.

title: Comparative study of cabergoline and hydroxychloroquine to prevent ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) in PCOS patients: a pilot randomized clinical trial
summary: This pilot randomized clinical trial compared two pharmacological approaches for preventing ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) in PCOS patients undergoing assisted reproductive technology. Cabergoline, a dopamine receptor agonist, treats OHSS by inhibiting VEGFR2 phosphorylation to control vascular permeability, while hydroxychloroquine reduces OHSS through immunomodulation by suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IFN-γ, IL-6, IL-8), inhibiting T follicular helper cells and toll-like receptor activation, and blocking endothelin-1. Multiple cytokines including IL-2, IL-6, IL-8, IL-10, IL-18, TNF-α, and VEGF are implicated in OHSS pathophysiology, with human chorionic gonadotropin triggering the condition characterized by ovarian hypertrophy and fluid shifts. Both treatments demonstrated comparable efficacy with similar laboratory parameters three to five days post-oocyte retrieval, though cabergoline showed a higher incidence of mild OHSS while hydroxychloroquine showed more moderate cases; notably, no cabergoline-treated patients required hospitalization compared to one in the hydroxychloroquine group. These findings suggest both agents offer viable preventive strategies, though cabergoline may have a slight clinical advantage in reducing severe OHSS requiring intervention in PCOS patients.

title: Attribution of colorectal cancer symptoms to medications for pre-existing chronic conditions: a secondary analysis of a vignette study in England
summary:
# Summary

This secondary analysis examines how patients with pre-existing chronic conditions attribute gastrointestinal symptoms to medications rather than recognizing them as potential cancer warning signs. Patients with type 2 diabetes, obesity, and arthritis were significantly more likely to attribute changes in bowel habit to their medications—particularly metformin, glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (used for diabetes and weight loss), and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (used for arthritis)—rather than to underlying colorectal cancer. This misattribution is clinically concerning because bowel habit changes, rectal bleeding, unexplained weight loss, and fatigue are established red flag symptoms present in 70-95% of colorectal cancer diagnoses. The findings suggest that patients with multiple chronic conditions face particular diagnostic complexity, as medication side effects can plausibly explain cancer symptoms, potentially delaying critical symptom recognition and clinical evaluation. Notably, diabetic patients showed lower likelihood of attributing symptoms to cancer itself, indicating that the burden of managing pre-existing conditions may obscure awareness of new malignancy signals.

title: Pituitary Apoplexy Following Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone Agonist Therapy: A Rare and Life-Threatening Complication
summary:
# Summary

Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonists, including leuprolide, are widely used to treat hormone-responsive conditions such as endometriosis, prostate cancer, uterine leiomyomas, and breast cancer. These medications bind to GnRH receptors on pituitary gonadotroph cells, initially stimulating the release of luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone, followed by receptor downregulation and gonadal suppression. However, GnRH agonist therapy carries a rare but life-threatening risk of pituitary apoplexy, an acute hemorrhagic event characterized by sudden headache, visual disturbances, hormonal deficiencies, and potentially adrenal insufficiency, which can develop within minutes to hours of drug administration. The mechanism underlying pituitary apoplexy remains incompletely understood but may involve acute cellular degranulation and shrinkage in the immediate phase, or stimulation of pre-existing gonadotroph adenomas leading to tumor expansion and hemorrhage in the subacute phase. Treatment of pituitary apoplexy includes urgent administration of corticosteroids such as dexamethasone and, when indicated, transsphenoidal surgery to relieve pituitary compression and prevent severe neurological sequelae including visual field defects, ocular palsies, and cardiovascular collapse.

title: Methaemoglobinaemia as an Alternative Diagnosis for Shortness of Breath: A Case Report
summary: This case report describes dapsone-induced methaemoglobinaemia presenting as unexplained shortness of breath and cyanosis in a patient. Dapsone, an antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory agent used to treat leprosy and dermatological conditions, acts as an oxidizing agent that induces methaemoglobinaemia through hepatic metabolism via the cytochrome P450 pathway. Elevated methaemoglobin levels impair oxygen-binding capacity and cause tissue hypoxia, triggering symptoms including dyspnea, cyanosis, headache, and at severe elevations (50-70%), cardiac arrhythmias and seizures. The patient's symptoms resolved promptly following dapsone discontinuation, with restoration of normal oxygen saturation, highlighting the importance of recognizing methaemoglobinaemia as a differential diagnosis in cyanotic patients presenting with normal arterial oxygen tension. Treatment options for severe cases include methylene blue, methylthioninium chloride, and exchange transfusion. This case underscores that clinicians should maintain awareness of methaemoglobinaemia as an alternative diagnosis in patients on dapsone therapy presenting with unexplained respiratory symptoms and cyanosis refractory to conventional oxygen therapy.

title: Methotrexate carried in lipid core nanoparticles reduces microglial activation and induces neuroprotection after cortical stroke induced in rats
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that methotrexate encapsulated in lipid core nanoparticles (LDE-MTX) provides significant neuroprotection following cortical ischemic stroke in rats through suppression of neuroinflammation. LDE-MTX markedly reduced microglial activation and inflammatory infiltration in the ischemic lesion, resulting in a tenfold decrease in activated macrophages and substantial preservation of neuronal survival in the periinfarct region, with a 319% increase in neuronal survival compared to controls. The lipid nanoparticle delivery system enhanced drug uptake by ischemic brain tissue fivefold through an LDL receptor-mediated mechanism involving acquired apolipoprotein E, while simultaneously reducing methotrexate toxicity. Notably, LDE-MTX did not reduce the primary infarct size or affect astrocyte activation, suggesting its neuroprotective effects are specifically mediated through anti-inflammatory mechanisms rather than through limitation of initial ischemic damage. These findings support LDE-MTX as a promising therapeutic strategy for reducing post-stroke neuroinflammation and improving neuronal survival in ischemic brain tissue.

title: Predictors of successful initiation of buprenorphine in enriched enrollment randomized withdrawal clinical trials in both opioid experienced and naïve participants: a participant-level meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis of enriched enrollment randomized withdrawal trials evaluated predictors of successful buprenorphine initiation in both opioid-experienced and opioid-naïve participants with chronic pain. Buprenorphine, a partial opioid agonist, effectively treats chronic pain and opioid use disorder, offering potential benefits over full opioid agonists in reducing overdose risk. Baseline pain severity and obesity history emerged as key predictors of titration success, with higher baseline pain and worse brief pain inventory scores reducing the likelihood of successful treatment initiation, while a medical history of obesity paradoxically increased success rates. Opioid withdrawal severity also predicted outcomes, with higher withdrawal scores associated with reduced titration success. Notably, opioid-experienced participants showed slightly higher success in the maintenance phase compared to naïve participants, suggesting prior opioid exposure may facilitate treatment continuation. These findings provide a prediction model to identify patients most likely to successfully initiate buprenorphine therapy for chronic pain management.

title: Comprehensive Review on Personalized Pain Assessment and Multimodal Interventions for Postoperative Recovery Optimization
summary:
# Summary

Surgical trauma initiates a cascade of inflammatory responses characterized by tissue damage and release of pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6 and TNF-α, which activate peripheral nociceptors and transmit pain signals to the central nervous system. Uncontrolled postoperative pain can progress to central sensitization, leading to abnormal amplification of pain signals and chronic pain, while psychological factors such as preoperative anxiety and depression further aggravate pain perception. The article emphasizes a multimodal analgesic approach incorporating pharmacological agents (opioids for severe pain despite tolerance and dependence risks, NSAIDs for mild-to-moderate pain with gastrointestinal concerns, and local anesthetics like lidocaine and bupivacaine), alongside non-pharmacological interventions including mindfulness-based therapy, physical therapies, virtual reality distraction, and neuromodulation techniques such as transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation. Personalized pain assessment using biomarkers (cortisol, C-reactive protein), standardized instruments (McGill Pain Questionnaire, PainDETECT), and artificial intelligence-enhanced wearable technologies enables individualized treatment optimization grounded in a biopsychosocial model that integrates biological, psychological, and social factors including social support. This comprehensive approach aims to prevent complications of inadequately managed postoperative pain, including persistent post-surgical pain, cardiovascular and pulmonary complications, and impaired wound healing.

title: Efficacy and safety of casirivimab/imdevimab (REGEN-COV) in pregnant individuals with COVID-19: Literature review and insights from the COVID-19 International Drug Pregnancy Registry
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the efficacy and safety of casirivimab/imdevimab (REGEN-COV), a monoclonal antibody cocktail targeting SARS-CoV-2, in pregnant individuals with COVID-19. The dual monoclonal antibodies effectively neutralize the virus and prevent progression to severe COVID-19 infection in this vulnerable population. Data from the COVID-19 International Drug Pregnancy Registry indicate that casirivimab/imdevimab demonstrates favorable safety outcomes during pregnancy, with no significant adverse effects on maternal or fetal health. These findings support the therapeutic use of this antiviral treatment as a valuable pharmacological option for managing COVID-19 in pregnant patients, who face heightened disease severity risk. The evidence contributes to an expanding body of literature demonstrating that monoclonal antibody therapeutics can be safely administered during pregnancy when clinically indicated. Overall, casirivimab/imdevimab represents an effective and safe treatment choice for pregnant individuals with confirmed or suspected COVID-19.

title: Suzetrigine Approval Breaks a 25-Year Silence: A New Era in Non-Opioid Acute Pain Management
summary: Suzetrigine represents a significant breakthrough in non-opioid acute pain management, offering the first FDA-approved selective inhibitor of the NaV1.8 sodium channel in 25 years. By selectively blocking NaV1.8 channels, which play a central role in peripheral pain pathways, suzetrigine effectively reduces moderate-to-severe acute pain symptoms while avoiding the addiction risks associated with traditional opioid analgesics. This mechanism of action—targeting peripheral nociception rather than central nervous system pathways—enables substantial pain relief with a more favorable safety profile. The approval of suzetrigine marks a paradigm shift in acute pain management, providing clinicians with a novel pharmacological option that addresses the critical need for effective non-opioid alternatives in patients with acute pain conditions.

title: Oral contraceptives with progestogens desogestrel or levonorgestrel and risk of intracranial meningioma: national case-control study
summary: This national case-control study demonstrates that current use of desogestrel 75 µg is associated with an increased risk of intracranial meningioma, particularly after five years of continuous use, with risk increasing cumulatively with duration of exposure. In contrast, levonorgestrel alone or combined with estrogen showed no significant association with meningioma risk. Meningiomas associated with desogestrel use show distinct characteristics including preferential location at the anterior and middle skull base, higher expression of progesterone receptors, and a high rate of PIK3CA mutations—a genetic feature also implicated in breast and endometrial cancers. The excess risk was greatest in women aged 45-54 years and in those with previous exposure to other progestogens, and notably decreased to background levels one year after discontinuation of desogestrel. Other progestogens including cyproterone acetate, medroxyprogesterone acetate, and nomegestrol acetate have also been recognized as risk factors for intracranial meningioma with prolonged use. These findings establish a progestogen-specific and dose-dependent relationship with meningioma development, raising important safety considerations for hormonal contraceptive and endometriosis treatment choices.

title: A Transcriptomics-Based Computational Drug Repositioning Pipeline Identifies Simvastatin And Primaquine As Novel Therapeutics For Endometriosis Pain
summary:
# Summary

This computational drug repositioning study identified simvastatin and primaquine as novel therapeutic candidates for endometriosis-associated pain. Both drugs significantly alleviated vaginal hyperalgesia and reversed the endometriosis disease signature in a rat model, while downregulating key pathways including cytokine-cytokine receptor interactions, osteoclast differentiation, and disease-associated genes (FOS, FOSB, NR4A1, LY75). The mechanisms of action involve suppression of inflammatory signaling cascades and stromal cell proliferation; simvastatin additionally inhibits endometrial stromal cell growth and invasiveness, properties supported by clinical evidence showing benefits for dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and pelvic pain. These findings suggest that repurposed drugs targeting distinct molecular pathways—the cholesterol-lowering statin and antimalarial agent—offer promising alternatives to standard hormonal and NSAID treatments for managing endometriosis pain.

title: Exploration of a Novel Catalytic Approach for Synthesizing Glycolide and ε-Caprolactone Copolymers and Their Application as Carriers for Paclitaxel
summary:
# Summary

This study describes the synthesis of glycolide/ε-caprolactone (PGCL) copolymers using a novel diethylzinc/propyl gallate catalytic system and evaluates their potential as drug delivery carriers for paclitaxel. The catalytic system achieved superior polymerization efficiency compared to conventional tin(II) 2-ethylhexanoate, producing high-molecular-weight (28–63 kDa), highly random copolymers with melting points between 43–51°C. PGCL copolymers demonstrated advantageous degradation properties, degrading more slowly than PLGA while generating the weakly acidic 6-hydroxyhexanoic acid rather than the acidic products of PLGA, potentially reducing inflammatory responses. When loaded with paclitaxel—a chemotherapeutic agent that stabilizes microtubules and treats various cancers including those of the ovary—the PGCL matrices showed non-cytotoxic profiles, minimal hemolysis, and compatibility with intravenous administration. Paclitaxel release from these carriers followed Fickian diffusion kinetics consistent with the Higuchi model, indicating sustained and controllable drug delivery. These findings suggest that PGCL copolymers synthesized via this novel catalytic approach represent a promising biodegradable platform for delivering chemotherapeutic agents with improved biocompatibility and controlled release profiles.

title: ‘In the weeds’: navigating the complex concerns, challenges and choices associated with medicinal cannabis consumption for endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis patients commonly turn to cannabis as a self-management strategy due to inadequate pain control from standard pharmaceutical treatments and intolerable medication side effects, with 99% of users reporting intent to continue cannabis use for symptom management. Cannabis consumption among people with endometriosis frequently results in reduced opioid use—a notable substitution effect given that endometriosis patients face four-fold increased risk of chronic opioid dependency compared to the general population. However, cannabis use presents significant clinical and practical challenges, including potential drug interactions with medications like warfarin and benzodiazepines, risks of cannabis use disorder, cannabis hyperemesis syndrome, and the danger of unsupervised medication reduction that could worsen symptoms or cause fatal withdrawals. Legal barriers, including concerns about workplace drug testing, driving restrictions, and law enforcement, combined with societal stigma and fear of medical judgment, discourage disclosure of cannabis use to healthcare providers, limiting clinical oversight and informed management. The perception of cannabis as a "natural" and safer alternative despite limited long-term safety data underscores a critical gap between patient preferences and evidence-based recommendations in endometriosis pain management.

title: Simvastatin-loaded liposomal nanoparticles as treatment for adenomyosis in a patient-derived xenograft mouse model: a pilot study
journal: Journal of obstetrics and gynaecology : the journal of the Institute of Obstetrics and Gynaecology
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study evaluated simvastatin-loaded liposomal nanoparticles (simvastatin-NP) as a novel treatment for adenomyosis using patient-derived xenograft mouse models. Treatment with simvastatin-NP significantly reduced both the volume and weight of adenomyosis xenografts while simultaneously downregulating Ki67, a marker of cell proliferation. The therapeutic mechanism appears multifaceted, involving inhibition of stromal cell growth through reduced Ras prenylation, suppression of matrix metalloproteinase-3 (which activates MMP-9), downregulation of vascular endothelial growth factor to limit angiogenesis, and modulation of the antioxidant Nrf2/HO-1 pathway to reduce oxidative stress. Notably, liposomal nanoparticle formulation enhanced simvastatin's bioavailability and efficacy compared to free drug, offering a promising advancement over existing adenomyosis treatments such as NSAIDs, progestins, and hormonal contraceptives.

title: Exploring Antibiotic-Mediated Disruption of Enterohepatic Circulation and Combined Oral Contraceptive Efficacy: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Combined oral contraceptives (OCPs) represent an effective pharmacological treatment for endometriosis pain and menstrual disorders, functioning through a dual mechanism wherein estrogen suppresses follicle-stimulating hormone while progestins prevent ovulation and alter uterine tissue. However, antibiotic-contraceptive interactions significantly impact OCP efficacy through two distinct pathways: enzyme-inducing antibiotics, particularly rifampicin, upregulate cytochrome P450 enzymes (especially CYP3A4) to accelerate metabolism of contraceptive hormones including levonorgestrel, etonogestrel, and desogestrel, thereby reducing their systemic exposure; additionally, broad-spectrum antibiotics disrupt enterohepatic circulation by eliminating intestinal microbiota responsible for deconjugating estrogen metabolites. Notably, most common non-enzyme-inducing antibiotics (penicillins, tetracyclines, cephalosporins, and fluoroquinolones) pose minimal clinical risk to OCP effectiveness, with continuous rifampicin usage presenting the most significant concern requiring consideration of alternative contraceptives such as levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine devices or depot medroxyprogesterone acetate. These findings underscore the importance of individualized counseling regarding antibiotic-contraceptive interactions, particularly when managing endometriosis patients requiring concurrent antimicrobial therapy.

title: The disease course in microscopic colitis may be influenced by hormonal factors
summary: This study examines how hormonal factors influence the disease course of microscopic colitis (MC), an immune-driven inflammatory bowel condition characterized by barrier dysfunction and increased intraepithelial T lymphocytes. Established risk factors include smoking, NSAIDs, proton pump inhibitors, and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, with notably, ex-smokers showing more severe disease progression. The research reveals complex hormonal influences on MC: estrogen-based hormone therapies and oral contraceptives are associated with increased disease risk and severity, while bilateral oophorectomy significantly reduces disease activity, suggesting a protective effect of ovarian hormone removal. Pregnancy and hormonal alterations generally improve outcomes by enhancing immune tolerance and mucosal barrier function, contrasting with the detrimental effects of exogenous estrogen exposure. The findings support the hypothesis that endogenous and exogenous hormonal changes substantially modulate immune-driven colitis progression, with estrogen paradoxically promoting disease when administered therapeutically despite pregnancy-related hormonal changes being protective. These results suggest that hormonal management strategies warrant consideration in personalized treatment approaches for microscopic colitis patients.

title: Neuron-Specific Enolase as a Biomarker in Ifosfamide-Induced Encephalopathy: A Case Report
summary: Ifosfamide, an alkylating agent used in osteosarcoma chemotherapy, causes encephalopathy through metabolism by cytochrome P450 enzymes—particularly CYP3A4—into neurotoxic metabolites including chloroacetaldehyde, which inhibits mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation and astrocytic gluconeogenesis. Risk factors for ifosfamide-induced encephalopathy include age over 60, female gender, concurrent cisplatin administration, and hypoalbuminemia, with clinical manifestations ranging from confusion and aphasia to psychomotor agitation. Neuron-specific enolase (NSE), a neuronal biomarker involved in glycolysis with neuroprotective functions, becomes elevated in toxic or metabolic encephalopathies and correlated strongly with encephalopathy severity and recovery trajectory. In this case, a patient with high-grade osteosarcoma developed ifosfamide-induced encephalopathy, which was successfully treated with methylene blue—an inhibitor of chloroacetaldehyde-forming enzymes—with parallel improvements in NSE levels and electroencephalographic patterns. These findings suggest NSE serves as a valuable biomarker for monitoring ifosfamide neurotoxicity, providing prognostic information alongside clinical and electrophysiological assessments during chemotherapy-induced encephalopathy management.

title: Systemic low-dose anti-fibrotic treatment attenuates ovarian aging in the mouse
summary: Ovarian aging is characterized by the development of a fibro-inflammatory microenvironment that impairs gamete quantity and quality while disrupting ovulation. This study demonstrates that low-dose pirfenidone, an anti-fibrotic agent, effectively attenuates age-related ovarian decline in mice by reducing collagen 1 (Col1a1) expression and limiting the accumulation of ovarian fibrosis. Pirfenidone treatment significantly improved multiple markers of ovarian function, including increased follicle and corpora lutea numbers, elevated anti-Müllerian hormone levels, and restored estrous cyclicity. Transcriptomic analysis revealed that pirfenidone upregulates genes related to reproductive function while simultaneously downregulating inflammatory genes, indicating that the therapeutic benefit operates through simultaneous suppression of both fibrotic and inflammatory pathways. In contrast, etanercept did not effectively block TNF expression in ovarian stromal cells, suggesting TNF-independent mechanisms drive age-related ovarian fibrosis. These findings identify pirfenidone as a promising pharmacological approach to counteract ovarian aging and support the therapeutic potential of anti-fibrotic interventions for preserving reproductive function.

title: Update on long- and short-acting contraceptive methods
summary:
# Summary

This Australian prescriber update reviews contraceptive methods and their clinical applications, with emphasis on long-acting reversible contraceptives (LARCs) as superior options for preventing unintended pregnancy. Long-acting methods including intrauterine devices and contraceptive implants achieve higher effectiveness and continuation rates (approximately 70% at three years) compared to short-acting methods like combined oral contraceptive pills. Beyond contraception, various formulations offer non-contraceptive benefits: combined oral contraceptive pills improve dysmenorrhea and acne, while hormonal IUDs effectively treat heavy menstrual bleeding. The review highlights critical safety considerations, including the association between estrogen-containing contraceptives and venous thromboembolism risk, which varies by progestogen type and estrogen dose, and absolute contraindications in patients with migraine with aura or Factor V Leiden. Enzyme-inducing drugs compromise the effectiveness of hormonal contraceptives but do not affect copper or hormonal IUDs, making these non-hormonal and hormone-containing IUDs preferred options in such drug interactions. Increased LARC uptake substantially reduces unintended pregnancy rates and associated adverse maternal and fetal outcomes.

title: Contraception Considerations in Transmasculine and Gender Diverse Adolescents & Young Adults
summary:
# Summary

This article examines contraceptive and hormone management options for transmasculine and gender diverse adolescents and young adults, with particular attention to how these approaches address both pregnancy prevention and gender dysphoria. While testosterone therapy induces amenorrhea and suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, it does not reliably prevent pregnancy, as approximately one-third of users continue to ovulate and breakthrough bleeding occurs in 25–27% of cases. Progestin-only methods such as norethindrone acetate, levonorgestrel IUDs (achieving 89% amenorrhea rates), and medroxyprogesterone acetate are preferred for menstrual suppression because they avoid feminizing effects of estrogen while maintaining contraceptive efficacy; external condoms and implants are similarly favored as they provide fewer dysphoria-inducing reminders of reproductive function. GnRH agonists used for puberty suppression also do not reliably prevent pregnancy, and discontinuation of any hormonal therapy leads to menses resumption, underscoring the importance of comprehensive contraceptive counseling. The article emphasizes that long-term fertility effects of these approaches remain understudied, and treatment decisions should balance pregnancy prevention, menstrual suppression, and psychological well-being in this population.

title: Pilot Trial to Individualize the Dose of Follitropin Delta in Oocyte Donors: REKO15
summary:
# Summary

This pilot trial evaluated follitropin delta, a recombinant FSH produced using human cell lines with unique glycosylation properties that enhance biological activity and slow clearance, for ovarian stimulation in normo-responding oocyte donors undergoing progestin-primed ovarian stimulation.
