A fixed dose of 15 mcg/day of follitropin delta achieved comparable oocyte retrieval (17.8 ± 7.8 oocytes) to standard 225 IU/day follitropin alfa (18.5 ± 7.7 oocytes), with equivalent rates of metaphase II oocytes, fertilization, blastocyst formation, and implantation. Notably, follitropin delta required significantly shorter stimulation duration (9.4 versus 10.9 days) and substantially lower total gonadotropin intake (141 mcg versus 2475 IU) while maintaining low ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome incidence (2.5%) and achieving high patient satisfaction. These findings suggest that individualized dosing of follitropin delta, guided by anti-müllerian hormone and body weight biomarkers, can minimize both poor and hyper-responders, reduce overall gonadotropin exposure, and provide an effective, patient-friendly alternative for oocyte retrieval in assisted reproductive technology.

title: Evaluation of the In Vitro Permeation Parameters of Topical Diclofenac Sodium from Transdermal Pentravan® Products and Hydrogel Celugel Through Human Skin
summary:
# Summary

Diclofenac is a non-selective cyclooxygenase (COX) inhibitor that blocks both COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes, thereby reducing prostaglandin production and alleviating the inflammation, swelling, and pain associated with conditions like osteoarthritis and menstrual cramps. This study evaluated topical diclofenac formulations using Pentravan and Celugel carriers, finding that formulations enhanced with menthol and ethanol (particularly formulation F4) achieved superior transdermal penetration and skin accumulation compared to standard preparations, with F4 delivering over 10 times more diclofenac than the commercial preparation. The enhanced penetration resulted from optimized formulation rheology—gel bases demonstrated significantly better drug release than other carriers, and the addition of penetration enhancers improved bioavailability. Topical diclofenac offers a favorable safety profile by reducing systemic exposure while maintaining analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic effects, making it a preferable alternative to oral NSAIDs for managing localized pain conditions with minimized risks of gastrointestinal complications, renal dysfunction, and hypertension. Rapid skin permeation of diclofenac produces faster therapeutic effects through swift inflammation reduction in underlying tissues, supporting its clinical utility for conditions including mild to moderate osteoarthritis pain.

title: Hematopoietic effects of Fufang E’jiao Jiang revealed by microbiome, metabolome and transcriptome analyses: a multi-omics strategy
summary:
# Summary

Fufang E'jiao Jiang (FEJ), a traditional Chinese medicine syrup composed of Asini Corii Colla and four complementary herbal ingredients, effectively treats anemia through multi-level regulatory mechanisms revealed by integrated omics analysis. FEJ significantly improves hematopoietic outcomes in chemotherapy-induced myelosuppression models by elevating red blood cells, hemoglobin, and antioxidant capacity while reducing erythropoietin levels and oxidative stress markers. At the microbial level, FEJ restores dysbiotic gut microbiota by upregulating beneficial Bacteroidota and suppressing pathogenic bacteria including Turicibacter, Akkermansia, and Verrucomicrobiota, while simultaneously increasing short-chain fatty acid production including butyric and propionic acid. Transcriptomic and metabolomic analyses demonstrate that FEJ's anti-anemia effects involve activation of hematopoietic cell lineage pathways, modulation of amino acid metabolism (L-leucine, L-proline, glycine, phenylalanine), and enhancement of bone marrow hematopoietic cell density with increased burst-forming unit-erythroid and colony-forming unit granulocyte-monocyte populations. The study establishes a strong correlation between restored microbiota composition, normalized metabolic profiles, and improved hematopoietic gene expression, supporting FEJ's therapeutic potential through gut microbiota-immune-hematopoiesis axis restoration.

title: Treatment of experimental DMBA induced mammary carcinoma with Cetrorelix (SB-75): a potent antagonist of luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: Cetrorelix, a potent luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone (LH-RH) antagonist, effectively reduced tumor mass in a DMBA-induced mammary carcinoma model through suppression of mitotic activity and induction of apoptosis. The compound's mechanism involves blocking LH-RH signaling, which consequently reduced reproductive organ weights (uteri and ovaries to 40–50% of control values) and interrupted the estrus cycle, maintaining treated animals in anestrus. While the LH-RH agonist Decapeptyl also demonstrated antitumor efficacy, the combination of Cetrorelix and Decapeptyl on an overlapping schedule produced continuous antitumor responses superior to either agent alone. The therapeutic effect of Cetrorelix appeared dependent on suppression of estrogen production, as supplementation with estradiol in ovariectomized tumor-bearing rats abolished the antitumor response. These findings demonstrate that LH-RH antagonism represents a promising pharmacological strategy for hormone-dependent breast tumors by simultaneously achieving gonadal suppression and direct tumor growth inhibition.

title: The EnDA endometrial adenocarcinoma: an oestrogen-sensitive, metastasizing, in vivo tumour model of the rat
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: The EnDA rat endometrial adenocarcinoma represents an oestrogen-sensitive tumour model that expresses oestrogen receptors but notably lacks progesterone receptors, distinguishing it from typical human endometrial carcinomas that typically express both receptor types. Ovariectomy substantially reduced tumour weight by 46-64% depending on the host species, while oestradiol substitution in ovariectomized animals restored tumour growth to control levels, demonstrating the critical dependence of this tumour on oestrogen signalling. Treatment with medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA) inhibited primary tumour growth by 67%, whereas tamoxifen produced a more modest 38% inhibition, suggesting differential therapeutic efficacy. Critically, MPA and ovariectomy both significantly reduced lung metastases, while tamoxifen treatment failed to alter metastatic burden despite inhibiting primary tumour growth. These findings establish the EnDA model as a valuable system for evaluating oestrogen-dependent endometrial carcinoma progression and metastasis, with implications for understanding progesterone-based therapeutic strategies in hormone-sensitive endometrial tumours.

title: Effects of the luteinizing-hormone-releasing hormone (LHRH) antagonist ramorelix (hoe013) and the LHRH agonist buserelin on dimethylbenz[a]anthracene-induced mammary carcinoma: studies with slow-release formulations
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: This study evaluated slow-release formulations of the LHRH agonist buserelin and the LHRH antagonist ramorelix for treating dimethylbenz[a]anthracene-induced mammary carcinoma in rats. Buserelin implants demonstrated superior efficacy, achieving dramatic tumor regression within 14 days and preventing tumor progression for up to 120 days, while ramorelix microparticles showed more limited activity, inhibiting progression for only 14 days. Both drug classes suppress gonadal function and steroid production through pituitary inhibition, with a clear correlation between sustained peptide release rates and tumor inhibition efficacy. Repeated dosing regimens of buserelin implants every 56 days or microparticles prevented tumor development, whereas ramorelix microparticles similarly prevented development but with shorter duration of action. The superior tumor control with buserelin compared to ovariectomy alone suggests that continuous gonadal suppression via LHRH modulation provides additional benefit beyond simple estrogen ablation. These findings support the therapeutic potential of sustained-release LHRH agonist formulations for hormone-responsive cancers requiring long-term hormonal suppression.

title: Two-year follow-up study (PRIMROSE 3) to assess bone mineral density in subjects with uterine fibroids completing the PRIMROSE 1 and PRIMROSE 2 linzagolix trials
summary:
# Summary

The PRIMROSE 3 extension study evaluated long-term bone mineral density (BMD) changes in women with uterine fibroids treated with linzagolix, a GnRH antagonist. Linzagolix monotherapy caused dose-dependent BMD loss, with the greatest decreases observed in the lumbar spine at 200 mg dosing; however, co-administration of add-back therapy (ABT)—comprising 1 mg estradiol and 0.5 mg norethisterone—substantially mitigated these losses. Following cessation of linzagolix treatment, BMD recovered markedly in at least 50% of subjects, with the most pronounced recovery in the 200 mg linzagolix plus ABT group, demonstrating that BMD suppression was reversible and demonstrating the critical protective role of hormone add-back. The study found that BMD loss from GnRH antagonist-induced hypo-estrogenism is less severe than with traditional GnRH agonists and carries minimal fracture risk in premenopausal women, whose baseline BMD is typically higher than postmenopausal populations. These findings support linzagolix as an effective treatment for uterine fibroid symptoms with manageable BMD effects when combined with appropriate add-back hormone therapy.

title: Deeply Infiltrating Endometriosis (DIE) No More: Dienogest as a Game Changer in Rectovaginal Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Dienogest, a fourth-generation selective progesterone receptor agonist, demonstrates efficacy in treating deeply infiltrating rectovaginal endometriosis through multiple mechanisms including inhibition of gonadotropin secretion, reduction of circulating estrogen levels, and antiproliferative and antiangiogenic properties that promote atrophy of endometriotic lesions. In a documented case study, dienogest monotherapy successfully reduced rectovaginal lesion size and eliminated key symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, pelvic pain, and postcoital bleeding within three months of treatment. Meta-analytic evidence from 256 patients confirms significant symptomatic improvement across multiple endometriosis-related pain conditions with superior tolerability compared to gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists, which carry risks of hypoestrogenism and bone mineral density loss. While irregular bleeding and minor side effects such as headache, weight gain, breast tenderness, and acne are commonly reported, these adverse effects typically improve with continued treatment and demonstrate favorable overall compliance and low discontinuation rates. Importantly, continuous hormonal suppression following surgical resection is crucial to prevent recurrence, which occurs in 40–50% of patients within five years when circulating estrogens fuel endometriotic foci. Dienogest's rapid reversibility upon cessation, with ovulation resuming within 1–43 days, further positions it as a favorable treatment option for women with deeply infiltrating endometriosis seeking fertility preservation alongside symptom management.

title: Effects of sedatives on diaphragm activity monitored by ultrasound: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that sedative agents significantly impair diaphragmatic function during and after sedation, with effects quantified through ultrasound imaging. Sedatives broadly inhibit diaphragm activity by reducing diaphragmatic motion (average 2.54 mm decrease) and diaphragmatic thickening fraction, with propofol producing the most pronounced effects (3.62 mm reduction in motion). While both propofol monotherapy and propofol combined with other sedatives (dexmedetomidine, midazolam, remimazolam) suppress diaphragmatic parameters, propofol alone causes greater impairment than combination regimens. Notably, although diaphragmatic motion and thickening fraction partially recover upon awakening, they fail to return to baseline levels, indicating a persistent post-sedation deficit. These findings highlight that sedative-induced diaphragmatic dysfunction—ranging from partial weakness to functional impairment—may predispose patients to respiratory complications and sleep disorders, raising clinical concerns for procedures requiring sedation such as gastrointestinal endoscopy.

title: Study of caspase-6 activity in aggressive HCT116 cells using methotrexate-encapsulated lactoferrin-conjugated solid lipid nanoparticles via in silico and in vitro approaches
summary:
# Summary

This study developed methotrexate-loaded solid lipid nanoparticles conjugated with lactoferrin (MTX-Lf-SLNs) as an improved delivery system to overcome the poor solubility, systemic toxicity, and non-selective biodistribution associated with free methotrexate in colorectal cancer treatment. The nanoparticles achieved superior cytotoxicity (IC50: 0.51 µM) compared to unconjugated formulations by leveraging lactoferrin's binding to cancer cell receptors (Caspase-6, EGFR, LRP1, and TfR), which enabled receptor-driven absorption and caspase-6-mediated apoptosis in HCT116 cells. MTX-Lf-SLNs demonstrated multiple anti-cancer mechanisms including inhibition of tumor growth, angiogenesis, cancer cell migration and invasiveness, while simultaneously reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β) and immune activation. The pH-sensitive formulation exhibited exceptional stability with maintained crystalline structure and reduced drug leakage, while protecting mitochondrial integrity and balancing reactive oxygen species to minimize systemic toxicity. These findings suggest that MTX-Lf-SLNs represent a promising advanced drug delivery approach for enhancing colorectal cancer treatment efficacy while reducing adverse effects.

title: Dydrogesterone is an eligible tool to suppress LH surge in assisted
summary:
# Summary

Dydrogesterone effectively suppresses premature luteinizing hormone (LH) surges during assisted reproductive technology cycles, making it a viable alternative to GnRH antagonists for controlled ovarian stimulation in progestin-primed ovulation suppression (PPOS) protocols. The medication inhibits pituitary LH secretion through progesterone's modulation of hypothalamic GnRH pulses and gonadotropin secretion, achieving comparable oocyte retrieval, metaphase II oocyte counts, and embryo quality to standard GnRH antagonist approaches. Dydrogesterone demonstrates particular clinical utility for freeze-all protocols, oocyte cryopreservation, and embryo banking while reducing ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) risk, with the added advantages of oral administration, good tolerability, lower androgenic activity, and superior cost-effectiveness compared to both GnRH agonists and antagonists. While initial concerns about progestin-induced reductions in endometrial receptivity were noted, subsequent studies have confirmed dydrogesterone's effectiveness across patient populations, including those with polycystic ovary syndrome who are particularly susceptible to elevated LH and compromised oocyte quality from intrafollicular androgen excess.

title: The Safety of Drug Treatment in Patients with Neuropathic Pain: Data from Ambulatory Care in a Real-Life Setting
summary: This real-world safety analysis evaluates pharmacological and non-pharmacological treatments for neuropathic pain in ambulatory care patients. First-line medications including tricyclic antidepressants, pregabalin, gabapentin, and duloxetine effectively treat neuropathic pain by blocking neural depolarization and activating inhibitory pathways, though they carry risks of central nervous system effects and anticholinergic adverse reactions. Topical agents such as capsaicin and lidocaine patches, along with botulinum toxin injections, provide additional options for peripheral neuropathic pain management. Long-term opioid therapy was associated with significant safety concerns including constipation, somnolence, confusion, and risks of abuse and dependence, making them less favorable than non-opioid alternatives. Notably, nutraceuticals, oxygen–ozone therapy, and diamagnetic therapy demonstrated excellent safety profiles with fewer adverse drug reactions and no reported side effects, suggesting these complementary approaches may enhance treatment adherence and outcomes. Patient comorbidities, smoking status, and sex-specific disease patterns influenced both neuropathic pain presentation and adverse medication reactions, highlighting the importance of individualized treatment selection in chronic pain management.

title: Evaluation of Assisted Reproductive Technology Outcomes Using Crossover Administration of Two Recombinant Follicle-Stimulating Hormones in the Same Patients
summary: This crossover study compared two recombinant follicle-stimulating hormone preparations used in controlled ovarian stimulation for assisted reproductive technology, demonstrating that follitropin delta, which employs an AMH-based and weight-adjusted dosing algorithm, significantly reduces the incidence of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome compared to follitropin alfa derived from Chinese hamster ovary cells. Despite biochemical differences—including higher peak FSH and estradiol concentrations with follitropin delta—both preparations achieved equivalent clinical pregnancy rates and live birth outcomes, with no significant differences in blastocyst formation rates. A key advantage of follitropin delta emerged in sequential cycles, where patients using follitropin alfa in the first cycle followed by follitropin delta in the second cycle demonstrated improved oocyte yield consistency and reduced decline in retrieval rates, particularly in the group showing the greatest change (40.7% increase). The algorithmic dosing approach of follitropin delta provides enhanced objectivity and dose responsiveness across the 6-12 μg range, particularly benefiting intermediate responders with low anti-Müllerian hormone levels who have historically experienced suboptimal oocyte yields. These findings suggest that while both preparations are effective for ovarian stimulation, follitropin delta's personalized dosing strategy and superior safety profile regarding OHSS may offer clinical advantages in assisted reproductive technology cycles.

title: Pain Reduction in Linzagolix‐Treated Patients With Uterine Fibroids: A Secondary Mediation Analysis of the PRIMROSE 1 and 2 Phase 3 Trials
summary: This secondary mediation analysis of the PRIMROSE 1 and 2 trials examined how linzagolix, a GnRH antagonist, alleviates pain in women with uterine fibroids through its effects on heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) and fibroid volume reduction. Linzagolix demonstrated dose-dependent reductions in pain, HMB, and fibroid volume, with the 200 mg dose combined with add-back therapy producing the greatest effects (84.6% HMB reduction and up to 52.4% fibroid volume reduction). Mediation analysis revealed that HMB reduction was the dominant mechanism, explaining 28–51% of linzagolix's pain-relieving effect, while fibroid volume reduction explained only 2–8%, indicating that symptom burden from excessive bleeding is the primary driver of fibroid-related pain. The underlying pathophysiology of HMB in fibroids involves multiple mechanisms including increased uterine surface area, enhanced vascularity and angiogenic factors (including TGF-β3), myometrial dysfunction, and inflammatory alterations in circulating interleukins. These findings support linzagolix as an effective oral GnRH antagonist for managing symptomatic uterine fibroids, with pain relief predominantly mediated through suppression of menstrual bleeding rather than direct fibroid shrinkage.

title: Oral Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone Antagonists in the Treatment of Endometriosis: Advances in Research
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic estrogen-driven inflammatory disease causing dysmenorrhea, non-menstrual pelvic pain, infertility, and dyspareunia, for which oral gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) antagonists represent a therapeutic advance over traditional approaches. GnRH antagonists work by directly inhibiting GnRH receptor activity and suppressing the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, thereby reducing circulating estrogen levels and avoiding the initial "flare effect" characteristic of GnRH agonists. Multiple orally administered GnRH antagonists have achieved clinical approval or advanced through trials, with elagolix demonstrating the most robust efficacy—the 200 mg twice-daily dose achieved 78% response rates for dysmenorrhea and reduced opioid use by over 65%, while relugolix and linzagolix showed comparable pain relief with reduced endometriotic cyst volumes. These agents offer practical advantages over injectable alternatives and may help overcome progesterone resistance, which limits the effectiveness of first-line treatments in approximately one-third of patients. The mechanism of benefit extends beyond estrogen suppression, as GnRH antagonists also inhibit GnRH II signaling in endometrial tissues, addressing the impaired local anti-proliferative signaling observed in endometriosis.

title: A randomized controlled clinical trial to investigate the efficacy and safety of dexmedetomidine in treating postoperative acute pain
summary: This randomized controlled trial demonstrates that dexmedetomidine effectively manages postoperative acute pain through activation of α2-adrenergic receptors, which suppress norepinephrine release and inhibit excitatory neurotransmitters including substance P and glutamate in the spinal dorsal horn. The drug's analgesic and sedative properties, mediated by α2 receptor activation in the locus coeruleus and medullary vasomotor center, result in significantly lower visual analog scale pain scores, reduced vital sign parameters (mean arterial pressure, heart rate, blood pressure), and improved patient satisfaction compared to conventional pain management. Beyond analgesia, dexmedetomidine reduces postoperative nausea and hyperalgesia while maintaining respiratory stability without depression, contributing to enhanced recovery and improved cognitive function in elderly patients. Low-dose dexmedetomidine combined with multimodal analgesia avoids severe complications such as hypotension, excessive sedation, and oxygen desaturation. The study's findings position dexmedetomidine as a valuable alternative to traditional opioid-based approaches in the Enhanced Recovery After Surgery protocol, particularly for surgical patients requiring effective pain management with improved hemodynamic stability and reduced anxiety during the perioperative period.

title: A Call for Use of Lipid Fractionation Studies in Patients With Abnormal Standard Lipid Profiles
summary:
# Summary

This case report presents a patient with statin intolerance and abnormal lipid profiles who benefited from detailed lipid fractionation analysis to guide therapeutic decisions. The patient demonstrated elevated small, dense LDL (sdLDL) and apolipoprotein B (Apo B), both independent risk factors for premature coronary artery disease and myocardial infarction, alongside other cardiovascular risk factors including early menopause and family history. A triple cholesterol management regimen combining ezetimibe, the PCSK9 inhibitor evolocumab, and bempedoic acid significantly improved lipid particle profiles by reducing sdLDL, total LDL particle number, and Apo B while increasing HDL large particles and reducing the patient's chest pain and dyspnea. However, evolocumab paradoxically elevated lipoprotein(a), which was subsequently controlled by addition of bempedoic acid, an ATP citrate lyase inhibitor that upregulates LDL receptor expression. This case underscores the clinical utility of lipid fractionation assays in identifying atherogenic lipoprotein subclasses and personalizing cholesterol management in patients with standard lipid profile abnormalities and statin intolerance.

title: Preliminary Insights into Quality of Life and Dietary Intake in Patients with Breast Cancer on Adjuvant Endocrine Therapy
summary:
# Summary

Breast cancer patients receiving adjuvant endocrine therapy experience substantial quality of life impairments and altered dietary patterns that vary by treatment type. Aromatase inhibitors cause severe joint pain (95.3%), reduced libido (84.7%), osteoporosis, and sleep disturbances, while also driving consumption of energy-dense and sugary foods with concurrent deficiencies in protective micronutrients like α-carotene, biotin, and fluoride. Tamoxifen users predominantly experience vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes, night sweats at 94%), weight gain and irritability (93%), mood swings (90%), and elevated thromboembolism risk, while consuming greater amounts of pro-inflammatory omega-6 polyunsaturated fats. Both therapies suppress estrogen levels to control hormone receptor-positive breast cancer growth and reduce recurrence, yet this sustained hormonal suppression triggers menopausal symptoms, vaginal dryness, and sexual dysfunction in over 75% of patients. Adherence to healthy dietary patterns rich in fruits, vegetables, and whole grains improves cancer prognosis, whereas poor diet quality correlates with increased breast cancer-specific mortality. These findings highlight the critical need for nutritional counseling and symptom management strategies to preserve quality of life during long-term endocrine therapy.

title: Adverse event profile differences among long-acting gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogs: A real-world, pharmacovigilance study
summary:
# Summary

This pharmacovigilance study examined adverse event profiles across five long-acting gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogs (leuprolide, goserelin, histrelin, buserelin, and triptorelin) used to treat endometriosis, uterine leiomyomas, prostate cancer, breast cancer, and central precocious puberty. The drugs function by regulating the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis to suppress ovarian function and reduce hormone-dependent disease progression. The analysis revealed distinct safety signatures for each agent: buserelin showed the highest gastrointestinal disorder incidence and hospitalization rates, histrelin was associated with psychiatric disorders and cutaneous rash, goserelin demonstrated elevated mortality and spinal cord compression risk, and leuprolide was linked to urinary tract toxicity. Across all agents, reproductive system and breast disorders represented the most common adverse event category, with additional risks including bone mineral density loss, vasomotor symptoms, abnormal vaginal bleeding, and serious complications such as anaphylaxis and cardiovascular toxicities. Off-label use carried a 1.4-fold higher risk of important medical events compared to approved indications, whereas pediatric patients under 18 years experienced significantly fewer complications, suggesting age-dependent safety profiles warrant consideration in clinical practice.

title: Stress-Induced Hyperprolactinemia Mimicking Pituitary Pathology: A Diagnostic Challenge
summary: Stress-induced hyperprolactinemia represents a significant diagnostic challenge that can mimic pituitary pathology, particularly in patients with endometriosis who already exhibit elevated prolactin levels. Psychological and physiological stress activates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, disrupting dopamine's normal inhibitory control of prolactin secretion and leading to transient prolactin elevations that cause menstrual irregularities, galactorrhea, and infertility. Endometriosis is independently associated with elevated prolactin concentrations and altered gonadotropin secretion, creating a complex hormonal environment where stress-induced hyperprolactinemia may be particularly problematic. The clinical presentation—including headaches, blurred vision, and visual field defects—can incorrectly suggest pituitary adenoma pathology when the underlying cause is stress-related dopamine dysregulation. Dienogest, a progestin used to manage endometriosis, may provide dual benefit by treating the underlying disease while potentially reducing serum prolactin levels. Crucially, stress reduction and supportive care can effectively resolve stress-induced hyperprolactinemia, emphasizing the importance of distinguishing functional prolactin elevations from pathological pituitary tumors in endometriosis patients.

title: Risk factors and countermeasures for abnormal uterine bleeding during dienogest therapy for adenomyosis: a review
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis is an estrogen-dependent disorder commonly treated with dienogest (DNG), a selective progesterone receptor agonist that effectively reduces adenomyosis-related pain but causes abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) in some patients through pseudodecidual breakthrough bleeding mechanisms. Multiple clinical and imaging risk factors predict DNG treatment discontinuation, including younger age (<38 years), high pretreatment menstrual blood loss, enlarged uterine dimensions (corpus length ≥10 cm, volume >100 cm³, myometrial thickness ≥4 cm), low baseline hemoglobin levels (≤5.12 g/dl), elevated serum CA-125, and specific adenomyosis subtypes (I and IV) with anterior wall localization. Strategic interventions improve treatment adherence and efficacy, including GnRH agonist pretreatment to thin the endometrium before DNG initiation, combination with traditional Chinese medicine formulas, adjunctive tranexamic acid bridging, and integration with minimally invasive procedures such as focused ultrasound ablation or microwave endometrial ablation. Baseline hemoglobin optimization to ≥11.0 g/dl through iron supplementation or transfusion, combined with extended hematologic surveillance, is essential for maximizing treatment tolerance and long-term compliance in adenomyosis patients receiving DNG therapy.

title: Rationale and design of the GLADE study: a randomized, multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial evaluating the safety and efficacy of gestrinone subdermal bioabsorbable pellet in endometriosis-related pelvic pain
summary: The GLADE study is a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial designed to evaluate the safety and efficacy of a novel subdermal bioabsorbable gestrinone pellet for treating endometriosis-related pelvic pain. Gestrinone is a synthetic steroid that addresses endometriosis through multiple mechanisms: it suppresses folliculogenesis and inhibits release of FSH and LH via central hypothalamic-pituitary effects, thereby inducing amenorrhea and reducing hormonal stimulation of ectopic endometrial tissue. The drug also interacts with progesterone and estrogen receptors while reducing sex hormone-binding globulin, leading to elevated free testosterone levels. While oral gestrinone effectively treats endometriosis-related pain and symptoms, its androgenic side effects—including acne, hirsutism, androgenetic alopecia, clitoral hypertrophy, and voice changes—have limited clinical adoption, alongside metabolic effects such as HDL reduction and weight gain. The development of a vaginal formulation and bioabsorbable pellet represents an attempt to improve tolerability by reducing systemic androgenic adverse events while maintaining therapeutic efficacy for patients with refractory pain, particularly those with deep infiltrative endometriosis.

title: How do patients choose between obesity medications: A thematic analysis
summary:
# Summary

This thematic analysis examines how patients select obesity medications and the factors influencing their treatment decisions. Obesity is highly associated with serious comorbidities including type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and cancer, with higher BMI correlating with increased health burden across multiple conditions. Patients considered several medication options—including Orlistat, Semaglutide, Tirzepatide, and newer agents like CagriSema and Retatrutide—that work through distinct mechanisms such as reducing dietary fat absorption and controlling appetite. Beyond clinical efficacy, patient medication preferences were shaped by three critical factors: treatment efficacy, safety profiles, and quality of information from healthcare providers, with notable attention to medication-specific effects on comorbidities like diabetes and cardiovascular health. Weight loss achieved through these medications improved functional outcomes including mobility and range of motion while reducing psychological burden from depression and social isolation, though side effects such as nausea presented trade-offs in treatment tolerability.

title: Structure-based machine learning screening identifies natural product candidates as potential geroprotectors
summary:
# Summary

This study applied machine learning algorithms to identify natural product candidates with geroprotective potential—compounds that maintain organismal homeostasis and treat age-related diseases including cancer, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and neurodegeneration. The research employed Decision Tree Classifier, Support Vector Machine, and K-Nearest Neighbours models to screen molecular structures, with the Support Vector Machine achieving the highest predictive accuracy (AUC: 0.73). Known geroprotectors like rapamycin and metformin were used as reference compounds; rapamycin inhibits the insulin/IGF-1 pathway while metformin activates adenosine monophosphate-activated protein kinase to extend lifespan, and both reduce inflammation and improve metabolic function. The machine learning approach identified natural compounds including myricetin and epigallocatechin gallate as promising geroprotector candidates, alongside validation of previously reported geroprotective molecules such as 1-MNA, trigonelline, and hypotaurine. These findings suggest that natural products represent a valuable source for developing novel pharmacological interventions to enhance healthy longevity by targeting the fundamental hallmarks of aging.

title: Trends in the estimated proportion of outpatients with menstrual disorders and the number of prescribed low-dose estrogen/progestin drugs in Japan: A descriptive study
summary: This Japanese descriptive study examined trends in menstrual disorder diagnoses and low-dose estrogen/progestin (LEP) prescriptions from 2008 to 2023, revealing that endometriosis is a major cause of secondary dysmenorrhea and menstrual-associated symptoms that significantly impact quality of life and work productivity. Low-dose estrogen/progestin drugs, including formulations such as norethisterone/ethinylestradiol, drospirenone/ethinylestradiol, and levonorgestrel/ethinylestradiol, effectively treat both dysmenorrhea and endometriosis and represent the primary pharmacological approach in Japan. Following public health insurance coverage of LEPs in 2010 and the introduction of generic formulations in 2015, prescriptions increased substantially, particularly among women in their 20s, while outpatient visits for menstrual disorders peaked in the late 20s and endometriosis diagnoses were highest in the 40s age group. The expansion of insurance coverage and reduced drug costs through generic pricing lowered economic barriers and increased health-seeking behavior, suggesting that rising outpatient proportions reflect both genuine increased treatment utilization and improved access rather than increased disease prevalence. This study demonstrates that pharmaceutical management of menstruation-associated symptoms has become increasingly accessible in Japan, with LEPs serving as the cornerstone treatment for dysmenorrhea and endometriosis-related disorders.

title: The Role of Hydroxychloroquine in the Management of Rheumatic Disorders: A Comprehensive Review
summary: Hydroxychloroquine (HCQ) is a broad-spectrum immunomodulatory agent with established efficacy in treating multiple rheumatic conditions including rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, primary Sjögren's syndrome, and antiphospholipid syndrome. The drug functions through multiple mechanisms: it accumulates in acidic cellular compartments (lysosomes, endosomes, and Golgi vesicles) where it modulates lysosomal action, autophagy, and cellular signaling networks, while also interacting with key immune cells including B cells, T cells, and antigen-presenting cells. HCQ exerts potent anti-inflammatory effects by inhibiting synthesis of proinflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, IL-12, IL-15, IL-17, IL-23) and autoantibodies, and in antiphospholipid syndrome specifically, it prevents complement activation, reduces neutrophil extracellular trap release, inhibits platelet aggregation, and improves pregnancy outcomes with higher live birth rates when combined with anticoagulation. Combination therapy with agents such as methotrexate or leflunomide demonstrates superior efficacy compared to monotherapy, and HCQ can reduce corticosteroid-related side effects, making it a cornerstone agent in rheumatic disease management despite occasional adverse effects such as HCQ-related rash.

title: Opioid use after adult liver transplantation: Incidence, high-risk use, and adverse events in a large US cohort
summary:
# Summary

This study examined opioid utilization and associated complications in adult liver transplant recipients (LTRs), revealing substantial risks with this pain management approach. Pre-transplant opioid use was the strongest predictor of continued opioid consumption post-transplant, with female sex, psychiatric comorbidities, prolonged hospitalization, and surgical complications also significantly increasing both incident opioid use and high-risk prescribing patterns. Opioid use—particularly high-risk usage involving concomitant benzodiazepine therapy—was associated with significantly worse outcomes, including 2.87-fold increased hazard of complications, adverse psychiatric events, sleep disorders, chronic pain syndrome, substance use disorder, and fractures. The study advocates for multimodal pain management strategies incorporating regional anesthesia, non-opioid analgesics, physical therapy, and psychological interventions as effective alternatives to reduce opioid dependence and associated morbidity in the transplant population.

title: Dienogest vs. combined oral contraceptive: A systematic review and meta‐analysis of efficacy and side effects to inform evidence‐based guidelines
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition affecting reproductive-age women that causes dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, pelvic pain, and infertility. Dienogest, a fourth-generation synthetic progestin, effectively treats endometriosis by suppressing ovarian function, controlling uterine bleeding, and exerting potent antiproliferative effects on both eutopic and ectopic endometrial tissue independent of estrogen levels, with established long-term efficacy in reducing pain symptoms and improving quality of life. This meta-analysis comparing dienogest to combined oral contraceptives found comparable efficacy in treating endometriosis symptoms, with no significant differences in common adverse effects including vaginal bleeding, nausea, headache, and hot flushes; however, combined oral contraceptives were superior in reducing dyspareunia while dienogest showed lower bone mineral density loss compared to GnRH agonists. Both therapeutic approaches work by blocking the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis to induce amenorrhea and reduce inflammation, though dienogest demonstrates additional advantages including absence of androgenic activity and good oral bioavailability. The most frequent reasons for treatment discontinuation were irritability and weight gain, while mood changes and depression warrant further investigation as potential side effects requiring monitoring during long-term therapy.

title: Upregulation of 15-Hydroxyprostaglandin Dehydrogenase by Celecoxib to Reduce Pain After Laparoendoscopic Single-Site Surgery (POPCORN Trial): A Randomized Controlled Trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial demonstrates that celecoxib reduces postoperative pain following laparoendoscopic single-site (LESS) surgery by upregulating 15-hydroxyprostaglandin dehydrogenase (HPGD) expression in peritoneal tissue. Carbon dioxide insufflation during laparoscopy causes peritoneal stretching and inflammation that leads to postoperative pain through prostaglandin-mediated nociceptor stimulation; celecoxib, a cyclooxygenase-2 inhibitor, blocks prostanoid synthesis while paradoxically enhancing HPGD mRNA expression, which promotes prostaglandin degradation. Patients treated with celecoxib showed significantly higher rates of grade 3 HPGD expression and substantially lower numerical rating scale pain scores at 6 and 12 hours post-surgery, particularly in those exposed to prolonged CO2 insufflation (>60 minutes). These findings suggest that celecoxib's analgesic efficacy depends on sufficient inflammatory stimulus from prolonged CO2 exposure to trigger HPGD upregulation, offering a non-opioid alternative for managing post-laparoscopic pain while potentially reducing the nausea and vomiting associated with traditional opioid-based analgesia.

title: The effect of medroxyprogesterone acetate plus conjugated equine estrogens on lipoprotein(a) and apolipoprotein concentrations in postmenopausal women: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials demonstrates that medroxyprogesterone acetate plus conjugated equine estrogens (MPA/CEE) produces favorable lipid and lipoprotein profiles in postmenopausal women. The combination therapy significantly increases apolipoprotein A1 (a protective marker) while reducing apolipoprotein B, lipoprotein(a), and oxidized low-density lipoprotein—all atherogenic factors associated with cardiovascular disease. These lipid modifications are proposed to confer cardioprotective effects through estrogen's capacity to block oxidized LDL formation and activate estrogen receptors in coronary arteries. The clinical benefits vary with treatment parameters, showing greater improvements at higher doses (2.5 mg/day MPA), longer treatment duration (≥12 months), and in specific populations including older women and those with elevated BMI. Beyond cardiovascular benefits, MPA serves multiple therapeutic roles in treating endometriosis, dysmenorrhea, abnormal uterine bleeding, and amenorrhea, though the analysis also notes that progestin addition to estrogen therapy prevents unopposed estrogen-related endometrial carcinoma risk.

title: First-in-Human Safety, Tolerability, Efficacy, and Pharmacokinetics of Pegfosimer Manganese (SN132D) for Contrast-Enhanced MRI of Breast Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This first-in-human study evaluated pegfosimer manganese (SN132D), a manganese-based nanoparticle contrast agent, for contrast-enhanced MRI imaging of breast cancer and other tissues. The agent demonstrated an acceptable safety profile with clinically relevant enhancement of primary breast tumors, liver, and pancreatic structures through the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect mechanism. The most commonly reported adverse events were flushing and feeling hot (5 participants), elevated transaminase levels (4 participants), and nausea/vomiting (2 participants), with elevated transaminases likely attributable to manganese ion release from the nanoparticles. Pharmacokinetic analysis showed peak manganese plasma concentrations of 0.9145 mg/L at the end of the one-hour infusion with subsequent renal clearance. Beyond breast cancer applications, a follow-up study has been completed testing pegfosimer manganese in younger females with endometriosis, suggesting potential utility of this contrast agent in imaging pathology beyond oncology.

title: A Case of Drug-Induced Pancytopenia due to Tamoxifen
summary: Tamoxifen, a standard treatment for hormone receptor-positive breast cancer that significantly improves disease-free and overall survival, carries a well-documented adverse effect profile ranging from common side effects like hot flashes and gastrointestinal symptoms to serious but rare hematological complications. This case report documents a rare instance of drug-induced pancytopenia occurring five months after a patient initiated tamoxifen therapy following surgical resection of invasive ductal carcinoma and completion of multimodal chemotherapy and radiation. The patient developed progressive reductions in white blood cell, neutrophil, and platelet counts with accompanying anemia, which was confirmed through bone marrow biopsy that ruled out myelodysplastic syndrome. While prior chemotherapy and radiation therapy likely contributed to bone marrow suppression, tamoxifen emerged as the suspected primary causative agent, requiring intervention with granulocyte colony-stimulating factor and blood transfusions before switching to alternative hormonal therapy with exemestane and a luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone agonist. This case emphasizes that although tamoxifen-induced cytopenia is uncommon, clinicians should maintain vigilance for hematological toxicity during tamoxifen administration, particularly in patients with prior exposure to bone marrow-suppressive therapies.

title: Structural insights into GPCR signaling activated by peptide ligands: from molecular mechanism to therapeutic application
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the structural biology and therapeutic potential of peptide-binding G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs), which constitute a major drug target family. Peptide ligands—including hormonal peptides such as glucagon and ghrelin, neuropeptides like endorphins and neuropeptide Y, regulatory peptides including angiotensin and vasopressin, and chemokines—activate diverse GPCR subtypes to regulate critical physiological processes including metabolism, immune responses, pain perception, cardiovascular homeostasis, and reproductive functions. Upon peptide binding, GPCRs undergo conformational changes that promote coupling to intracellular G proteins (Gs, Gi/o, Gq, and G12/13), triggering downstream signaling cascades such as adenylyl cyclase activation, phospholipase C stimulation, and Rho GTPase regulation. The structural insights gained from high-resolution crystal structures of key receptors like the β2-adrenergic receptor have enabled rational drug design for clinically validated targets including GLP-1R (successfully treating type 2 diabetes and obesity) and receptors such as the μ-opioid receptor, parathyroid hormone receptor, and chemokine receptor 5, which are actively pursued in drug development. These studies demonstrate how understanding GPCR activation mechanisms by peptide ligands translates molecular structural knowledge into effective therapeutic interventions across multiple disease contexts.

title: Effect of uterine sound on pain perception during intrauterine device insertion: A randomized trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized trial demonstrates that uterine sounding effectively reduces pain during intrauterine device (IUD) insertion, with patients in the uterine sound group experiencing significantly lower pain scores compared to those without the procedure, including sustained pain relief at 30 minutes post-insertion. Multiple patient factors influence pain perception during IUD placement: nulliparity, history of cesarean delivery, retroverted uterus position, and severe dysmenorrhea all associate with increased pain, while prior vaginal delivery correlates with decreased pain. The study identifies several pharmacological interventions that effectively reduce procedural pain, including topical and local anesthetics (particularly 2% lidocaine gel), prostaglandins (dinoprostone and misoprostol), and oral analgesics such as ketorolac, tramadol, and naproxen, with lidocaine-prilocaine cream showing particular efficacy. Additionally, IUD type affects pain experience, as levonorgestrel IUDs cause more discomfort than copper devices, and underlying conditions including endometriosis, chronic pelvic pain, and uterine anomalies are associated with heightened pain perception. The findings suggest that multimodal pain management strategies combining mechanical methods like uterine sounding with pharmacological approaches provide optimal pain control for IUD insertion procedures.

title: Linzagolix – new perspectives in the pharmacotherapeutic management of uterine fibroids and endometriosis
summary: Linzagolix, an oral non-peptide GnRH antagonist, represents a promising advancement in treating endometriosis and uterine fibroids by suppressing ovarian function and reducing estrogen-dependent growth of these conditions. Clinical trials demonstrate that linzagolix effectively reduces heavy menstrual bleeding, dysmenorrhea, and pelvic pain while decreasing fibroid and uterine volume and improving anemia-related outcomes, with add-back therapy (low-dose estrogen-progestogen) significantly ameliorating hypoestrogenic side effects such as hot flashes and bone mineral density loss. The drug's convenient once-daily oral dosing and 15-hour half-life offer practical advantages over earlier GnRH agonists, which require intramuscular administration and are associated with initial hormone flares and greater bone loss. Treatment with linzagolix substantially improves quality of life by reducing symptom burden and analgesic use, though common adverse effects include headaches, fatigue, and vasomotor symptoms. As one of three currently available oral GnRH antagonists, linzagolix joins elagolix and relugolix as emerging therapeutic options recommended by major reproductive medicine societies, positioning GnRH antagonists as the most promising pharmacological class for managing these estrogen-dependent gynecological conditions.

title: Effects of ultrasound-guided transversus abdominis plane block with combined dexmedetomidine and dexamethasone on the stress and immune function
summary:
# Summary

Ultrasound-guided transversus abdominis plane (TAP) blocks supplemented with dexmedetomidine and dexamethasone significantly improve postoperative pain management and immune preservation in surgical patients. Both agents individually reduce postoperative pain scores, sufentanil consumption, and stress hormones (cortisol and norepinephrine), while prolonging TAP block duration; however, their combination produces superior analgesic effects with the most pronounced reduction in opioid requirements and enhancement of sedation. The combined approach most effectively suppresses stress-induced immunosuppression by preserving the CD4+/CD8+ T cell ratio and overall immune function, counteracting the immunosuppressive cascade triggered by postoperative pain and stress hormone release. These anti-inflammatory and stress-reducing properties of the drug combination facilitate early ambulation and rapid recovery while mitigating opioid-related adverse effects such as nausea, vomiting, and respiratory depression. These findings suggest that dexmedetomidine-dexamethasone adjuvants in regional anesthesia represent an effective multimodal strategy for optimizing perioperative outcomes by simultaneously controlling pain, preserving immunity, and reducing systemic inflammation.

title: The role of self-management in endometriosis pain: insights from a cross-sectional survey in Germany, Austria, and Switzerland
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth, causes a spectrum of debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, pelvic pain, and infertility that significantly impact patients' sexual function, mood, sleep, and exercise capacity. This cross-sectional survey across Germany, Austria, and Switzerland reveals that while NSAIDs (particularly ibuprofen used by 96.4% of patients) and hormonal contraceptives remain first-line pharmacological treatments, self-management strategies play a critical role in pain control and medication reduction. Cannabis emerged as the most effective self-management intervention for pain reduction, with 47.7% of users reporting over 50% reduction in endometriosis-related medication, while heat therapy was notably both widely utilized (91.1% of participants) and highly effective, making it the most practical accessible option. Additional moderately effective self-management approaches include osteopathy, yoga/Pilates, herbal medicine, stretching, and meditation, though many lack robust clinical evidence. Diagnostic delays correlate with symptom severity and pain chronification, underscoring the importance of timely diagnosis, while paradoxically disease stage (rASRM) does not correlate with pain intensity, suggesting symptom severity is driven by multifactorial mechanisms beyond anatomical burden.

title: Hepatotoxicity Associated with Adalimumab in Hidradenitis Suppurativa: A Report of Two Cases of Drug-Induced Liver Injury
summary: Adalimumab, a TNF-α inhibitor widely used to treat hidradenitis suppurativa, can paradoxically cause severe drug-induced liver injury through mechanisms involving aberrant immune activation, eosinophilic infiltration, and hepatic inflammation following TNF-α blockade. This report documents two cases of hepatotoxicity associated with adalimumab treatment, including one patient who developed markedly elevated transaminases (ALT 1595 U/L, AST 587 U/L, GGT 556 U/L), steatosis, and right upper quadrant discomfort after more than two years of therapy, and another who experienced severe epigastric pain with elevated liver enzymes after just a single dose. Discontinuation of adalimumab and administration of oral corticosteroids resulted in progressive clinical and biochemical improvement in both cases. Although adalimumab remains a cornerstone therapeutic agent for hidradenitis suppurativa, these cases underscore the critical importance of routine hepatic monitoring and prompt clinical recognition of hepatotoxicity during treatment to prevent serious liver complications.

title: The effect of dexamethasone in laparoscopic abdominal surgery - a review on inflammatory markers for stress response
summary:
# Summary

Surgical procedures trigger a systemic inflammatory response characterized by rapid elevation of inflammatory biomarkers including c-reactive protein, interleukins, serum amyloid A, and fibrinogen. Dexamethasone, a glucocorticoid with immunosuppressive properties, substantially reduces this inflammatory cascade by modulating immune cell activity and decreasing pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1 and IL-6, with c-reactive protein levels showing 38-60% reductions at 24-48 hours postoperatively at an 8 mg dose. Beyond anti-inflammatory effects, dexamethasone mitigates multiple adverse postoperative outcomes including pain, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, tissue damage, edema, and adhesion formation while potentially accelerating recovery. Importantly, meta-analytic evidence demonstrates that dexamethasone does not increase infection rates, delayed wound healing, or glucose intolerance, establishing it as a safe adjunct in laparoscopic abdominal surgery. The drug's mechanism appears to balance acute inflammation with resolution, though optimal perioperative pain management may require combination therapy with other agents such as ketamine, gabapentin, or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs.

title: Compounded hormonal pellets: a critical review of current evidence and risks
summary: Gestrinone compounded in pellet form has been explored since the 1980s as an oral treatment for endometriosis, effectively reducing pelvic pain and potentially beneficial for deep endometriosis, though its therapeutic use is significantly limited by substantial adverse effects. The drug induces amenorrhea in approximately 30% of users and causes multiple androgenic side effects including acne, hypertrichosis, and elevated liver enzymes, while also inhibiting fertility and causing oligomenorrhea, headaches, and elevated hematocrit levels. Testosterone pellets, similarly used to enhance sexual desire and treat sexual dysfunction in postmenopausal women, carry risks of androgenic effects, mood disturbances, weight gain, and cardiovascular complications due to supraphysiological hormone levels. Estradiol pellets maintain stable hormone levels but are associated with breast tenderness, skin reactions, fatigue, and gynecological complications including endometrial hyperplasia and prolonged bleeding. Nestorone pellets tested for endometriosis prevent pregnancy through ovulation inhibition but cause irregular bleeding and hypoestrogenic symptoms that recur post-treatment. Overall, over 40 medical societies oppose indiscriminate use of hormonal pellets due to documented adverse effects exceeding 200 documented complications, recommending instead established alternatives such as GnRH agonists, oral contraceptives, and hormonal intrauterine devices for endometriosis and related conditions.

title: The Rise of Clinical Decision Support Algorithms in Pain Management 2009–2024
summary:
# Summary

This article examines NarxCare, a widely-adopted clinical decision support algorithm designed to reduce opioid misuse by flagging high-risk prescribing patterns, but identifies substantial concerns about its development, implementation, and societal impact. The pharmaceutical and opioid industries have heavily influenced NarxCare's development and widespread adoption across 52 of 54 U.S. prescription drug monitoring programs, despite limited evidence of its actual effectiveness in preventing opioid misuse. The algorithm's proprietary design creates significant transparency and accountability barriers, as the criteria and weighting factors used in risk calculations are not publicly disclosed, and machine learning systems inherently carry biases that may exacerbate health disparities based on race and socioeconomic status. A critical concern is that NarxCare may shift perceived responsibility for opioid misuse away from manufacturers and distributors toward individual patients and clinicians, thus perpetuating a narrative of opioid safety rather than addressing systemic industry accountability. The article highlights how pharmaceutical industry influence extends through corporate policy activities and legislative channels to shape the regulatory landscape and clinical decision-making, raising questions about whether these tools genuinely improve patient outcomes or primarily serve industry interests. These findings underscore the need for greater transparency, independent validation, and critical examination of algorithmic decision-support tools in clinical practice.

title: A qualitative interview study exploring the experiences of pain specialists on prescribing opioids for chronic non-cancer pain
summary:
# Summary

Chronic non-cancer pain, including pain from endometriosis, significantly impairs physical functioning, quality of life, and causes psychological distress. While opioids effectively treat moderate-to-severe acute pain, they provide only modest symptom reduction for chronic non-cancer pain and are associated with serious adverse effects including overdose risk, addiction, opioid-induced hyperalgesia, and endocrinopathies. Pain specialists interviewed emphasized that chronic opioid treatment actually impedes pain rehabilitation and functional recovery. However, opioid tapering, despite initial withdrawal symptoms and temporarily increased pain intensity, ultimately improves pain levels, energy, and cognitive function when continued. Alternative medications such as buprenorphine patches and tapentadol were identified as preferred treatment options for managing chronic pain in this population, reflecting a clinical shift toward reducing long-term opioid dependence.

title: The impact of in utero exposure to cancer treatments on foetal reproductive development and future fertility: a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

In utero exposure to cancer therapies, including chemotherapy agents and hormonal treatments, significantly impairs fetal reproductive development with long-term consequences for fertility. Chemotherapy drugs such as cisplatin, carboplatin, cyclophosphamide, etoposide, and doxorubicin cross the placenta and cause substantial decreases in germ cell numbers in both developing ovaries and testes, while also inducing apoptosis and disrupting gonadal architecture. Hormone therapies like tamoxifen and letrozole similarly reduce germ cell populations and compromise Sertoli cell development in males, with tamoxifen also increasing apoptosis in fetal ovarian tissue. The vulnerability of primordial germ cells and key somatic cells during critical developmental windows means that fetal exposure to these agents can compromise the ovarian reserve and spermatogenic capacity established in utero, ultimately resulting in reduced fertility and decreased offspring numbers in cancer survivors. Notably, chemotherapy is contraindicated in the first trimester due to increased risk of birth defects, and in utero exposure is associated with additional complications including preterm birth and fetal growth restriction.

title: Comparison of quality of life in patients with endometriosis undergoing treatment with progestins, oral contraceptives pills (OCP), and GnRH agonists
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes significant morbidity through chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, substantially affecting patient quality of life and psychological well-being. Three primary pharmacological treatments—progestins, oral contraceptive pills (OCPs), and GnRH agonists—reduce ectopic endometrial tissue growth and alleviate symptoms through distinct mechanisms: progestins and OCPs reduce pain and regulate hormonal fluctuations, while GnRH agonists rapidly suppress pain but induce a hypoestrogenic state. Despite differential efficacy profiles, quality-of-life improvements were comparable across all three treatment groups (p=0.197), though each carries distinct adverse effects, including breakthrough bleeding and mood changes with progestins, nausea and headaches with OCPs, and vasomotor symptoms and bone density loss with GnRH agonists requiring monitoring. The findings suggest progestins may offer enhanced cost-effectiveness for endometriosis management, whereas GnRH agonists are reserved for more severe cases despite their rapid symptom relief. Supplementary interventions such as dietary modifications and nutritional support may further improve pain and quality of life outcomes in endometriosis patients.

title: Endocrine and Metabolic Challenges of Long-Term Lithium Therapy: Diagnostic and Management Considerations
summary:
# Summary

Lithium, while essential for treating bipolar disorder and acute mania, causes significant endocrine and metabolic complications through disruption of multiple signaling pathways. The drug inhibits glycogen synthase kinase-3 and protein kinase A while activating the Wnt signaling pathway, leading to lithium-induced hyperparathyroidism (LIH) characterized by elevated parathyroid hormone levels and hypercalcemia. Chronic lithium exposure also triggers hypothyroidism through TSH-sensitive adenylate cyclase inhibition, nephrogenic diabetes insipidus via aquaporin-2 downregulation, and secondary osteoporosis. The presented case demonstrates that parathyroidectomy frequently fails to resolve lithium-induced hypercalcemia due to underlying parathyroid hyperplasia, whereas lithium discontinuation successfully normalized serum calcium and improved bone mineral density by 8.2% in the lumbar spine. Management strategies include vitamin D supplementation, calcimimetic agents for severe hypercalcemia, and consideration of alternative psychiatric medications such as duloxetine, which effectively controlled depression and fibromyalgia while avoiding lithium's compounding endocrine toxicity.

title: Seeking the root causes of menstrual pain: A systematic review of biomarkers in menstrual effluent
summary: Dysmenorrhea affects over 40% of women and is a leading cause of work and school absenteeism, yet many patients experience incomplete pain relief with standard treatments. Multiple biomarkers in menstrual effluent have been implicated in menstrual pain pathogenesis, with elevated prostaglandins (particularly PGF2α and PGE2), along with eicosanoids including 12-HETE, leukotrienes, and thromboxanes consistently identified in women with dysmenorrhea. Beyond the classical prostaglandin pathway, emerging evidence highlights platelet-activating factor (PAF) and 12-HETE as alternative pain mediators; notably, 12-HETE potentiates TRPV receptors that are implicated in both menstrual pain and endometriosis. While NSAIDs effectively reduce prostaglandin concentrations and provide pain relief in many patients, the incomplete efficacy in others suggests additional molecular targets warrant therapeutic attention. Current pharmacological approaches under investigation include PAF receptor antagonists (rupatadine, BN52021, Lexipafant) and combination therapies targeting 5-lipoxygenase and soluble epoxide hydrolase, offering potential alternatives for NSAID-resistant dysmenorrhea. Future clinical trials should explore these multi-targeted approaches to address the heterogeneous biochemical mechanisms underlying menstrual pain.

title: Esketamine/dexmedetomidine–based opioid-free anesthesia and its association with postoperative bowel and cognitive dysfunction after total laparoscopic hysterectomy
summary:
# Summary

This study compared esketamine-dexmedetomidine-based opioid-free anesthesia (OFA) with opioid-based anesthesia (OBA) in 114 patients undergoing total laparoscopic hysterectomy for endometriosis, adenomyosis, and fibroids. The esketamine-dexmedetomidine regimen, which targets NMDA receptors and alpha-2 adrenergic receptors respectively, significantly accelerated bowel recovery and preserved postoperative cognitive function compared to conventional opioid-based approaches. Patients in the OFA group demonstrated faster return of bowel function (shorter time to first bowel movement and flatus passage), lower postoperative pain scores at 24 and 48 hours, reduced rescue opioid requirements (23% vs. 42%), and smaller declines in Montreal Cognitive Assessment scores from baseline. Additional benefits included shorter hospital stays, earlier oral intake tolerance, and lower overall postoperative opioid consumption without increased adverse events. These findings suggest that opioid-free anesthesia with esketamine and dexmedetomidine offers a safer, more effective alternative to traditional opioid-based regimens for gynecological surgery, with particular advantages in mitigating opioid-related complications and preserving cognitive function across diverse patient populations.

title: Exploring Salvia miltiorrhiza’s Therapeutic Effects on Adenomyosis by Inhibiting TNF-α/HIF-1α/IL-17-Driven Inflammatory Cascade: Mechanistic Insights from Target Prediction and Experimental Validation
summary: Salvia miltiorrhiza (Danshen), a traditional Chinese herb, demonstrates therapeutic efficacy against adenomyosis by suppressing a TNF-α/HIF-1α/IL-17-driven inflammatory cascade. Network pharmacology and molecular validation revealed that the herb's active components inhibit key inflammatory and proliferative pathways involving TNF, IL-1β, IL-17A, and HIF-1α, with binding interactions confirmed through molecular docking and dynamics simulations. Experimental studies in adenomyosis mouse models showed that Salvia miltiorrhiza significantly reduced mRNA and protein expression of TNF-α, IL-17A, IL-1β, and HIF-1α while improving pathological outcomes as assessed by histological analysis. The therapeutic mechanism involves targeting multiple nodes in the inflammatory network, including STAT3, EGFR, AKT1, and matrix metalloproteinases (MMP2/9), which are dysregulated in adenomyosis pathogenesis. These findings position Salvia miltiorrhiza as a promising botanical therapeutic candidate for managing adenomyosis-related symptoms including infertility, anemia, and heavy menstrual bleeding, offering an alternative to conventional hormone-based treatments with potentially improved tolerability.

title: Relugolix in Monotherapy and Combined Therapy for the Treatment of Uterine Diseases and Its Effects on Bones: A Systematic Review
summary: Relugolix, a selective gonadotropin-releasing hormone receptor antagonist, effectively treats both uterine fibroids and endometriosis by inhibiting follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone, thereby reducing ovarian steroid hormone production without triggering hormonal flare-up. The drug demonstrates superior efficacy compared to traditional GnRH agonists like leuprorelin, with faster onset of action and quicker recovery of menstruation post-treatment. However, relugolix monotherapy causes significant bone mineral density loss due to potent estrogen suppression, a limitation overcome through combination therapy with estradiol and norethisterone acetate (add-back therapy), which maintains bone health while effectively reducing endometriosis-related and fibroid-related symptoms including heavy menstrual bleeding, pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and dyspareunia over two years. The combination formulation also minimizes vasomotor symptoms and prevents endometrial hyperplasia without compromising efficacy. These findings establish relugolix combination therapy as a well-tolerated, long-term pharmacological option for managing uterine diseases while preserving skeletal integrity.

title: Detection of Problems Related to Hormonal Contraceptives in Community Pharmacy: Application of a Structured Questionnaire in Women of Childbearing Age
summary:
# Summary

Combined oral contraceptives (COCs) are widely used therapeutically in women of childbearing age for endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, acne, and menstrual cycle regulation, operating through antiandrogenic mechanisms. However, this study found that 47.2% of women using COCs reported adverse reactions, with psychiatric disorders being the most common (28.1%), including mood swings, anxiety, and depressive symptoms, possibly mediated through progestin effects on serotonergic neurotransmission. Additional adverse effects included headache, fatigue, decreased libido, breast tenderness, acne paradoxically, hair loss, fluid retention, and in rare cases, coagulation disorders and cardiovascular complications. Certain subpopulations face elevated risks: smoking, hypertension, obesity, migraines, and liver disorders significantly increase the risk of thromboembolism and cardiovascular events with COC use. Emergency contraceptive options like ulipristal acetate and levonorgestrel offer post-intercourse pregnancy prevention, though their efficacy varies by body mass index and time elapsed since intercourse, and both carry risks of breakthrough bleeding, weight gain, and emotional lability that frequently motivate treatment discontinuation.

title: Are We Considering All the Potential Drug–Drug Interactions in Women’s Reproductive Health? A Predictive Model Approach
summary: This predictive modeling study identified 192 women's reproductive health drugs associated with 51 different reproductive conditions, revealing extensive and previously uncharacterized drug-drug interactions (DDIs) with potential clinical consequences. Estradiol emerged as the reproductive drug with the highest interaction burden, particularly with asthma, allergy, and diabetes medications, while follitropin (FSH) showed the most DDIs among assisted reproduction drugs. The analysis uncovered critical safety concerns including twelve novel DDIs between oral contraceptives and HIV antiretroviral drugs that could compromise contraceptive efficacy, and identified synergistic interactions between commonly co-prescribed combinations such as triptorelin with levocarnitine for endometriosis treatment and heparin with antihypertensive agents for pregnancy complications. Estradiol combined with progesterone may increase liver damage risk, and drugs like cyclosporine can reduce follitropin effectiveness, highlighting the necessity for comprehensive interaction screening in reproductive medicine. The study demonstrates that current clinical practice may overlook significant DDIs in women's reproductive health, particularly in preconception, infertility, and menopause management, warranting implementation of predictive interaction models to optimize therapeutic outcomes and minimize adverse drug events.

title: Discovery and Evaluation of Novel Sulfonamide Derivatives Targeting Aromatase in ER+ Breast Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study describes the discovery and characterization of novel sulfonamide derivatives as selective aromatase inhibitors for treating estrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer. The researchers synthesized 17 sulfonamide compounds featuring an indole ring or heterocyclic scaffolds and identified compounds 1, 3, 9–10, and 13–14 as potent aromatase inhibitors with IC50 values between 30–60 nanoMolar, comparable to existing third-generation inhibitors. Molecular docking and dynamics simulations revealed that the most active compounds achieve high-affinity binding through multiple interactions: the heterocyclic core coordinates with the heme group via π-π stacking, the sulfonamide linker forms hydrogen bonds with Met374, and aromatic moieties establish π-π interactions with Trp224 within the enzyme's active site. In cellular assays using MCF7 breast cancer cells, compounds 3 and 9 demonstrated nanomolar to low micromolar potency with selectivity over normal mammary epithelial cells, and target fishing analysis suggested these compounds may also engage EGFR and PTK2B for potential multi-target therapeutic activity. These findings establish sulfonamide-based scaffolds as promising alternatives for estrogen-dependent cancer treatment, with potential applications extending to endometriosis and other estrogen-driven disorders.

title: Cross‐sectional and longitudinal metabolomics‐based profiles associated with oral contraceptive and progestin‐only pill use: A Finnish population‐based study
summary:
# Summary

This Finnish population-based metabolomics study demonstrates that combined oral contraceptives (COCs), particularly third-generation formulations containing ethinylestradiol, are associated with substantial metabolic alterations that create an unfavorable cardiometabolic profile. COC use increases levels of saturated and monounsaturated fatty acids, triglycerides, cholesterol, and inflammation markers while reducing amino acids including alanine, glutamine, glycine, tyrosine, and proline, potentially indicative of increased oxidative stress. In contrast, progestin-only pills and levonorgestrel intrauterine devices appear metabolically safe with minimal alterations in metabolic biomarkers compared to non-users. Notably, these COC-associated metabolic changes are largely reversible upon discontinuation or switching to alternative contraceptive methods, and women who switched from COCs to levonorgestrel intrauterine devices showed improvements in fatty acid composition and triglyceride levels. The progestin component modulates estrogen's metabolic effects, with anti-androgenic and nonandrogenic progestins demonstrating safer cardiometabolic profiles than androgenic variants.

title: FDA approves Veozah (Fezolinetant) for menopausal symptoms: a new nonhormonal option
summary:
# Summary

Fezolinetant (Veozah), a nonhormonal neurokinin-3 receptor antagonist approved by the FDA in May 2023, represents a novel therapeutic approach for moderate to severe menopausal vasomotor symptoms including hot flashes and night sweats. The drug works by blocking neurokinin B signaling in the hypothalamus, which normalizes the activity of KNDy neurons involved in thermoregulation; during menopause, declining estrogen levels lead to increased neurokinin B signaling that abnormally elevates KNDy neuron activity, causing vasomotor symptoms. Clinical trials (SKYLIGHT and VESTA studies) demonstrated that fezolinetant at doses of 30-45 mg significantly reduced vasomotor symptoms and improved quality of life as early as the first week of treatment. This nonhormonal option is particularly valuable for patients with endometriosis, uterine fibroids, hereditary breast cancer, migraines with aura, or cardiovascular disease—conditions that contraindicate estrogen-based hormone replacement therapy. The safety profile is generally favorable with mild to moderate adverse effects primarily including headache and fatigue, though rare severe hepatic effects warrant monitoring.

title: Triptorelin associated adverse events evaluated using FAERS pharmacovigilance data
summary:
# Summary

Triptorelin, a GnRH agonist FDA-approved for treating endometriosis, prostate cancer, and central precocious puberty, exerts its therapeutic effects by activating GnRH receptors and subsequently suppressing estrogen in females and testosterone in males. This pharmacovigilance analysis of FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS) data identified vasomotor symptoms, particularly hot flashes (>70%), as the most common adverse events, alongside expected outcomes including sexual dysfunction, bone loss, and osteoporosis with associated bone pain. Gender-specific effects include testicular atrophy in males and dyspareunia in females, while psychiatric and reproductive system disorders represented significant adverse event clusters. The study also detected unexpected statistical signals warranting further investigation, including neuropsychiatric effects (personality changes, behavioral disorders, suicidal ideation), serious conditions (pituitary apoplexy and hemorrhage, acute coronary syndrome), and growth-related abnormalities, highlighting the need for comprehensive patient monitoring during triptorelin treatment.

title: Hormonal Contraception and Endometrial Thickness in IVF/ICSI Cycles: A Multicentre Historical Cohort Study
summary: This multicenter cohort study examined how prior contraceptive use affects endometrial thickness during IVF/ICSI cycles, a critical factor for embryo implantation success. The levonorgestrel intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) distinctly reduced endometrial thickness by causing endometrial atrophy through local hormone release and cervical mucus thickening, resulting in significantly lower odds of achieving endometrial thickness ≥7 mm across all duration groups (0–3 years through >9 years of use). Conversely, combined oral contraceptive pills, progestin-only pills, and no contraceptive use were all associated with higher odds of adequate endometrial thickness (≥7 mm), with progestin-only pills and no contraception showing the highest odds ratios. The differential effects of contraceptive methods likely reflect their distinct hormonal mechanisms and patterns of endometrial suppression, with the LNG-IUS's localized progestin delivery producing prolonged endometrial thinning even after discontinuation. These findings suggest that women with prior LNG-IUS use may require additional monitoring or interventions to optimize endometrial development before embryo transfer in fertility treatments.

title: Myocardial infarction with nonobstructive coronary arteries associated with blood transfusion in a young woman: A case report
