summary: This case report describes a young woman with ovarian endometriosis who developed myocardial infarction with nonobstructive coronary arteries (MINOCA) following blood transfusion. The patient's endometriosis-related abnormal uterine hemorrhage caused moderate anemia, which was initially treated with hemocoagulase and oxytocin, followed by hormone therapy with ethinylestradiol and cyproterone acetate. Blood transfusion was administered to correct the anemia, but paradoxically triggered acute coronary syndrome four hours after the second transfusion, manifesting as severe chest pain and progressing to cardiogenic shock. The underlying mechanism was attributed to transfusion-induced microvascular spasm, a recognized type 2 MINOCA pathway involving oxygen supply-demand imbalance. Management included dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin and clopidogrel), statin therapy with rosuvastatin, vasodilation with nicorandil, and vasopressor support with vasopressin, which successfully alleviated microcirculatory dysfunction. This rare case highlights the potential iatrogenic cardiac risk of blood transfusion in anemic patients and emphasizes the importance of careful consideration of transfusion indications in endometriosis-related hemorrhage management.

title: Molecular and Immunomodulatory Mechanisms of Statins in Inflammation and Cancer Therapeutics with Emphasis on the NF-κB, NLRP3 Inflammasome, and Cytokine Regulatory Axes
summary: Statins exert potent anti-inflammatory and anti-cancer effects through multiple molecular pathways beyond their primary cholesterol-lowering mechanism. By inhibiting HMG-CoA reductase, statins reduce cholesterol synthesis and upregulate hepatic LDL receptors, thereby decreasing circulating LDL-cholesterol and suppressing synthesis of isoprenoid intermediates critical for protein prenylation. These pleiotropic effects extend to robust inhibition of NF-κB signaling and suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokine biosynthesis, evidenced by reduced levels of C-reactive protein, IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α in multiple clinical trials using rosuvastatin and atorvastatin. Specific statins demonstrate therapeutic efficacy in managing chronic inflammatory diseases including rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and diabetes, while enhancing endothelial function. Epidemiological evidence links statin exposure to reduced incidence of breast, prostate, and colorectal cancers through mechanisms including inhibition of neoplastic cell proliferation and induction of programmed cell death, with preclinical studies confirming statin-mediated suppression of tumor progression and remodeling of the tumor microenvironment. However, clinical use is tempered by significant adverse effects including myopathy, hepatotoxicity, and increased diabetes risk.

title: The Impact of Nutrition on Endometriosis Complaints in Patients Using and Not Using Hormone Therapy
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by chronic pelvic pain and substantial quality of life impairment, with pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-1, interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor alpha, and prostaglandins playing central roles in its pathogenesis. While hormone therapy reduces dysmenorrhea symptoms by suppressing prostaglandin and cyclooxygenase-2 production, patients on hormone therapy paradoxically reported higher pain levels and greater academic/work performance impairment compared to those not using hormone therapy. Dietary patterns significantly influence endometriosis-related symptoms, with high consumption of added sugars, sweetened products, and processed foods associated with increased work/school absences and concentration difficulties in hormone therapy users, whereas increased vegetable and nut consumption correlated with higher absenteeism in this group. Beyond sugary foods, consumption of refined carbohydrates, processed meats, animal fats, and sweetened beverages was associated with worsening abdominal pain, cramps, weakness, and gastrointestinal symptoms, while whole grains and vegetable fats showed complex relationships with symptom severity. These findings suggest that dietary modifications may serve as a complementary strategy to pharmacological management, with certain food categories potentially exacerbating endometriosis-related morbidity regardless of hormone therapy use.

title: IL6 genetic perturbation mimicking IL-6 inhibition is associated with lower cardiometabolic risk
summary:
# Summary

This study leveraged genetic variants in the IL6 locus as natural experiments to understand the therapeutic potential of IL-6 inhibition for cardiovascular disease. Genetic downregulation of IL-6 signaling was causally associated with substantially lower lifetime risks of coronary artery disease, peripheral artery disease, and ischemic atherosclerotic stroke, supporting IL-6's causal role in atherosclerotic disease pathogenesis. The anti-IL-6 antibodies ziltivekimab and clazakizumab, currently in phase 3 clinical trials, appear to mediate cardiometabolic benefits through reductions in inflammatory and lipid biomarkers including fibrinogen, serum amyloid A, and lipoprotein(a), while increasing protective HDL cholesterol and apolipoprotein A. Beyond cardiovascular disease, IL-6 inhibition was genetically associated with lower risks of type 2 diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, and polymyalgia rheumatica. However, safety analyses identified warning signals including increased migraine, open-angle glaucoma, and pregnancy-related maternal hemorrhage, suggesting that while IL-6 targeting shows promise for cardiometabolic risk reduction, careful clinical monitoring will be essential during phase 3 trials and post-marketing surveillance.

title: Endometrial Changes Associated with Mifepristone: A Review
summary: Mifepristone, a progesterone receptor antagonist, demonstrates therapeutic efficacy for endometriosis and uterine fibroids through multiple endometrial mechanisms while raising safety concerns regarding benign endometrial changes. The drug functions by competitively inhibiting progesterone-mediated signaling and blocking the endometrial transition from proliferative to secretory phase, while simultaneously downregulating progesterone and estrogen receptors and promoting caspase-3-mediated apoptosis at higher doses. A notable adverse effect is mifepristone-associated endometrial changes (PAECs), characterized by cystic glandular dilatation and hyperplasia that may mimic malignancy; these changes correlate with upregulation of fibroblast growth factor 18 and are more prevalent in older women and those treated during the luteal phase. Low-dose mifepristone (≤10 mg/day) administered during the early follicular phase appears safer for treating endometriosis, with reduced systemic effects on the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and minimal endometrial thickening. Importantly, PAECs and endometrial thickness changes are largely reversible upon treatment discontinuation, and intermittent dosing regimens combined with transvaginal ultrasound monitoring and subsequent levonorgestrel intrauterine system placement may mitigate these complications while preserving therapeutic benefits.

title: Menstrual disorders following COVID-19 vaccination: a review using a systematic search
journal: Frontiers in Drug Safety and Regulation
summary:
# Summary

COVID-19 vaccination is associated with transient menstrual disorders in women of reproductive age, with comprehensive evidence showing that vaccines cause changes in cycle length, amount and duration of bleeding, breakthrough bleeding, and menstrual pain, typically returning to normal within several months. Multiple studies document that the first and second vaccine doses produce measurable cycle delays of 0.5 to 2.3 days, while heavy menstrual bleeding occurred with relative risks up to 1.90 after the first dose, prompting regulatory agencies to add this adverse event to product information for Pfizer and Moderna vaccines. Risk factors for experiencing menstrual changes include advanced age, elevated body mass index, smoking, high perceived stress levels, and use of non-hormonal intrauterine devices, whereas hormonal contraception and hormonal medications reduce the risk by approximately 50%. Notably, women with pre-existing menstrual conditions including endometriosis, adenomyosis, uterine fibroids, polycystic ovary syndrome, and thyroid disorders show increased susceptibility to vaccine-related menstrual disruptions. The predominantly mild nature of these menstrual changes and their spontaneous resolution contrasts with their potential psychological impact on vaccine acceptance among reproductive-age women.

title: Analyzing real-world adverse events of spironolactone with the FAERS database
summary: This pharmacovigilance study analyzed adverse events associated with spironolactone using the FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS) database. Spironolactone, a potassium-sparing diuretic and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, is widely prescribed for cardiovascular and endocrine conditions including heart failure, hypertension, resistant hypertension, and polycystic ovary syndrome, as well as off-label for acne, hirsutism, and androgenic alopecia through its antagonism of androgen and progesterone receptors. The analysis identified hyperkalemia as the most frequent serious adverse event, particularly in patients with renal dysfunction, along with common dose-dependent menstrual irregularities and gastrointestinal symptoms. The study revealed several rare but notable adverse events including male endometriosis, 5-alpha-reductase deficiency, spinal bulbar muscular atrophy, and double-hit lymphoma, with remarkably elevated reporting odds ratios suggesting potential signal detection. Gender-specific effects were documented, with feminizing side effects such as gynecomastia and breast discomfort predominating in males, while menstrual irregularities and other endocrine effects were more prevalent in females. These findings emphasize the importance of monitoring potassium levels and sex hormone-related adverse events during spironolactone therapy, particularly in vulnerable populations.

title: Defactinib with avutometinib in patients with solid tumors: the phase 1 FRAME trial
summary:
# Summary

The FRAME trial evaluated the combination of avutometinib, a RAF-MEK clamp inhibitor, with defactinib, a FAK inhibitor, in patients with solid tumors. Avutometinib inhibits MEK and induces dominant-negative RAF-MEK complexes that reduce phosphorylation of downstream effectors p-MEK and p-ERK, while defactinib blocks FAK, which mediates integrin signaling and drives epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, proliferation, and immune escape. The combination achieved a 42.3% objective response rate in low-grade serous ovarian cancer (LGSOC), with particularly strong efficacy in KRAS-mutated LGSOC (58.3% response rate and 30.8 months median progression-free survival) compared to historical MEK inhibitor monotherapy response rates of 11-26%. The dual pathway targeting approach leveraged the rationale that combining MAPK and FAK pathway inhibition prevents compensatory resistance mechanisms seen with single-agent signal transduction inhibitors. Common adverse events included rash (90%), creatine phosphokinase elevation (56%), and diarrhea (38%), with an intermittent dosing schedule improving tolerability compared to continuous dosing.

title: How Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Medications Are Depicted in Instagram Posts Regarding Women’s Health, Nontraditional Access, and Barriers to Access: Content Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This content analysis examined Instagram posts depicting glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) medications, revealing substantial online promotion of these drugs beyond their FDA-approved indications. While GLP-1 medications including semaglutide and tirzepatide are established treatments for type 2 diabetes and obesity through mechanisms involving glycemic control improvement and appetite suppression, social media discourse increasingly associates them with off-label uses including polycystic ovarian syndrome, endometriosis, and infertility. The analysis found that over half of GLP-1-related posts promoted weight loss specifically, with polycystic ovarian syndrome being the most frequently cited health condition among posts from predominantly female users, reflecting a dramatic 588-659% increase in GLP-1 use among adolescent and young adult women between 2020 and 2023. While social media frames GLP-1 medications as enhancers of positive emotional well-being, the study documented minimal discussion of clinical side effects such as nausea, highlighting a significant gap between promoted benefits and evidence-based safety information on these platforms.

title: Preoperative Sublingual Misoprostol for Intraoperative Blood Loss Reduction in Total Abdominal Hysterectomy: A Randomized Controlled Trial
summary:
# Summary

Preoperative sublingual misoprostol 400 μg significantly reduces intraoperative hemorrhage during total abdominal hysterectomy through direct uterotonic effects that enhance myometrial contractility and promote vasoconstriction. The randomized controlled trial demonstrated that misoprostol reduced mean operative blood loss by 58.1 ml, postoperative hemoglobin drop, and transfusion requirements by 65% relative risk reduction compared to placebo. The sublingual route achieves superior bioavailability with peak plasma concentrations within 30 minutes, enabling optimal hemostatic timing, and demonstrated consistent efficacy across all age and parity subgroups. Secondary clinical benefits included reduced hospital length of stay and emerging as the strongest independent predictor of blood loss reduction. While misoprostol was associated with increased gastrointestinal adverse events including nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and uterine cramping—attributed to prostaglandin receptor activation—these were predominantly mild and reversible. The intervention demonstrates cost-effectiveness and meaningful clinical relevance for reducing transfusion requirements and postoperative morbidity in routine gynecological surgery.

title: A Gender-Affirming Approach to Contraceptive Care for Transgender and Gender-Diverse Patients
summary: This article addresses contraceptive management for transgender and gender-diverse patients, with particular relevance to menstrual symptom control. Testosterone therapy suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and inhibits ovulation in patients assigned female at birth, though breakthrough bleeding occurs in up to 25% of users and can trigger gender dysphoria. Hormonal contraceptives offer multiple therapeutic benefits for both cisgender and transgender patients, effectively treating endometriosis, heavy menstrual bleeding, polycystic ovarian syndrome, premenstrual syndrome, and premenstrual dysphoric disorder, while also providing menstrual suppression when combined with testosterone therapy. The levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device is favored for its suppressive effects on menstruation, though initial breakthrough bleeding may occur, whereas copper IUDs increase bleeding and can exacerbate gender dysphoria. For patients assigned male at birth, estrogens and antiandrogens used in gender-affirming hormone therapy inhibit spermatogenesis and reduce sperm motility and count. Clinicians must carefully consider individual preferences regarding estrogen-containing contraceptives, as these can trigger gender dysphoria, alongside managing common side effects like menstrual-like cramps and breast tenderness to optimize care for this population.

title: Antisynthetase Syndrome Masquerading As Psoriasis and Psoriatic Arthritis: A Case Report
summary: This case report describes antisynthetase syndrome (ASyS), an autoimmune condition characterized by anti-Jo-1 antibodies, presenting clinically as psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis. The cardinal features of ASyS include arthritis, myositis, interstitial lung disease, and distinctive skin findings such as mechanic's hands—hyperkeratotic, scaly plaques that can mimic psoriasis but are histologically distinguished by dyskeratotic cells, interface changes, and pseudo-checkerboard patterns. Unlike dermatomyositis, which commonly presents with photosensitivity and characteristic rashes (heliotrope, V-neck, violaceous), ASyS rarely exhibits these hallmark features, making clinical differentiation challenging. The patient showed significant improvement in both skin and joint symptoms following methotrexate therapy (20 mg/week), though narrow-band ultraviolet B phototherapy paradoxically triggered flares. This case emphasizes the diagnostic importance of recognizing ASyS as a distinct entity, as early identification is critical for appropriate management and pulmonary surveillance to prevent potentially serious interstitial lung disease complications.

title: The use of tirzepatide to successfully treat persistent genital arousal disorder/genitopelvic dysesthesia: a case report
summary: This case report presents the first documented use of tirzepatide, a dual GLP1/GIP receptor agonist, to successfully treat persistent genital arousal disorder/genitopelvic dysesthesia (PGAD/GPD), a condition associated with severe distress, depression, anxiety, and functional impairment. The mechanism underlying tirzepatide's therapeutic effect involves GLP1 and GIP receptor activation in brain reward circuits, particularly in the medial preoptic area, which modulates dopaminergic signaling involved in genital blood flow regulation. By increasing tonic dopamine signaling while suppressing phasic reward-driven dopamine firing, tirzepatide effectively reduces both PGAD/GPD symptoms and comorbid psychiatric symptoms including depression and anxiety. The case demonstrates rapid symptom resolution and concurrent improvement in addictive behaviors, suggesting that tirzepatide's broader effects on reward processing and appetitive control may address the neurobiological basis of PGAD/GPD. These findings provide a novel mechanistic framework for understanding PGAD/GPD as a disorder of aberrant dopaminergic reward signaling and support further investigation of GLP1/GIP agonists as a new treatment class for this previously intractable condition.

title: Impact of endometrioma management strategies on ovarian reserve over the follow-up period, a prospective longitudinal study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective longitudinal study evaluated how different endometrioma management strategies affect ovarian reserve, measured by anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels and antral follicle count. Endometriomas are strongly associated with diminished ovarian reserve through two proposed mechanisms: mechanical compression reducing blood flow to the ovary and inflammatory responses causing follicular damage. Surgical excision resulted in the most significant AMH decline (38%), with bilateral cystectomy causing up to 44% reduction and unilateral cystectomy approximately 30% reduction, primarily due to removal of healthy ovarian tissue and thermal damage from energy-based modalities like cauterization and laser techniques. Medical management with oral contraceptive pills (OCPs) and dienogest also reduced AMH levels at six months (26% and 21% respectively), though dienogest showed a less pronounced effect due to its more localized anti-inflammatory action on endometriotic lesions rather than systemic gonadotropin suppression. Even expectant management showed progressive AMH decline, indicating that endometriomas themselves pose a continuous threat to ovarian reserve independent of treatment choice. These findings suggest that while all management approaches impact ovarian reserve, surgical techniques using suturing instead of energy-based hemostasis and medical treatments like dienogest may minimize harm to reproductive potential.

title: Tolerability and Shared Decision-Making in the Hormonal Management of Endometriosis-Associated Pain
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory disorder characterized by ectopic endometrial-like tissue and associated with significant psychological comorbidities including depression and anxiety. The pathophysiology involves a complex hormonal-inflammatory axis where aberrant aromatase expression and deficient 17β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2 activity promote local estradiol excess, while loss of progesterone receptor isoform B mediates progesterone resistance and chronic inflammation. Multiple pharmaceutical approaches effectively reduce endometriosis-associated pain: GnRH agonists (leuprolide, goserelin) and antagonists (elagolix, relugolix) suppress ovarian estradiol synthesis, while progestins including dienogest and gestrinone antagonize estrogenic activity and modulate progesterone signaling. However, these treatments differ substantially in tolerability profiles—GnRH agonists cause significant bone loss and vasomotor symptoms, while dienogest preserves bone mineral density but carries risks of irregular bleeding and mood changes, and gestrinone preserves bone density but causes androgenic side effects including acne and hirsutism. Shared decision-making is essential, as selecting optimal hormonal therapy requires balancing pain relief efficacy against individual tolerability concerns and long-term safety considerations.

title: Letrozole at the Crossroads of Efficacy and Fetal Safety in Ovulation Induction: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Letrozole, a third-generation aromatase inhibitor, has emerged as the preferred first-line oral agent for ovulation induction, particularly in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), where it treats chronic anovulation by suppressing estrogen synthesis through inhibition of CYP19A1. This transient estrogen reduction removes negative feedback on the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, elevating GnRH and FSH secretion while intra-ovarian androgens upregulate FSH-receptor expression and insulin-like growth factor-1 to promote monofollicular ovulation. Compared to clomiphene citrate, letrozole achieves higher ovulation and live-birth rates, reduces multiple gestation risk, improves endometrial thickness, and produces fewer side effects; it is particularly effective in obese PCOS patients with roughly doubled live-birth rates. Letrozole also reduces ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk through its physiologically-mimicking FSH surge pattern and short 42-48 hour half-life, which limits embryonic exposure. Despite preclinical observations of skeletal anomalies and placental changes in animal models, extensive clinical evidence from over 4,700 pregnancies demonstrates that letrozole does not increase rates of major congenital anomalies, miscarriage, preterm delivery, or low birth weight, providing reassurance regarding fetal safety in clinical practice.

title: Cost-effectiveness of dienogest compared to gonadotropin releasing hormone agonists for the management of endometriosis in Vietnam
journal: Cost Effectiveness and Resource Allocation : C/E
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes significant morbidity through dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, subfertility, and psychological effects including depression and anxiety. While gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists (GnRH-a) such as triptorelin, leuprorelin, and goserelin effectively treat endometriosis-related pain, they produce substantial hypoestrogenic side effects including hot flushes, decreased bone mineral density, mood disturbances, and vaginal dryness. This cost-effectiveness analysis in Vietnam demonstrates that dienogest, a progestin administered as a once-daily oral tablet, achieves comparable pain relief to GnRH-a while causing fewer hormonal side effects and demonstrating superior cost-effectiveness. Dienogest dominated all GnRH-a comparisons for both dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain management, with the lowest costs ($363.3 for dysmenorrhea and $381.5 for pelvic pain) and highest quality-adjusted life years (1.74 and 1.64, respectively). Probabilistic sensitivity analysis confirmed dienogest's high probability (≥99%) of cost-effectiveness at Vietnam's willingness-to-pay threshold of one GDP per capita. The oral formulation of dienogest also offers practical advantages over injectable GnRH-a, potentially improving treatment adherence and patient convenience.

title: Management of abnormal bleeding in etonogestrel-releasing implant users: quo vadis? An exploratory study on the attitudes of a cohort of Italian gynaecologists and review of the literature
summary:
# Summary

The etonogestrel-releasing implant prevents ovulation and effectively treats dysmenorrhea, endometriosis, and adenomyosis, but its therapeutic benefits are limited by abnormal bleeding patterns and adverse effects including acne, weight gain, and decreased libido. Abnormal bleeding occurs due to long-term progestin therapy-induced changes in endometrial vasculature that reduce perfusion and create a hypoxia-reperfusion state, triggering reactive oxygen species release and alterations in angiopoietin expression that promote aberrant angiogenesis and abnormal vessel formation. This process is compounded by increased Matrix Metalloprotease expression, thrombin generation from abnormal microvasculature, and altered expression of stress-response mediators like FKBP51 and ZBTB16 that further disrupt hemostasis and endometrial integrity. Multiple pharmacological strategies address implant-related bleeding, with combined oral contraceptives being most commonly recommended as first-line therapy, while alternative approaches including tamoxifen, mifepristone, doxycycline, and ulipristal acetate demonstrate varying efficacy in reducing bleeding episodes and improving continuation rates. Patient factors such as higher body mass index correlate with more favorable bleeding patterns, offering potential for personalized management strategies.

title: Safety and pharmacology of AMY109, a long-acting anti–interleukin-8 antibody, for endometriosis: a double-blind, randomized phase 1 trial
summary:
# Summary: Safety and Pharmacology of AMY109 for Endometriosis

AMY109, a long-acting anti–interleukin-8 recycling antibody, was evaluated in a phase 1 double-blind randomized trial for treating endometriosis by targeting interleukin-8 (IL-8), a key mediator in endometriosis pathogenesis. IL-8 promotes endometrial cell adhesion, invasion, and implantation within the peritoneum, while elevated IL-8 levels and IL-8 receptor expression in endometriotic tissue are associated with dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain. AMY109 effectively suppressed free IL-8 plasma levels with sustained suppression across multiple doses, demonstrating linear pharmacokinetics and a prolonged half-life of 40 days that enables convenient once-monthly dosing. Treatment with AMY109 reduced visceral analog scale scores for dysmenorrhea and non-menstrual pelvic pain, decreased analgesic medication requirements, and reduced pain interference scores without interrupting menstrual bleeding. The most common adverse events were mild-to-moderate upper respiratory tract infections and gastrointestinal symptoms; no antidrug antibodies were associated with adverse effects, supporting AMY109's potential as a novel endometriosis therapeutic targeting IL-8-mediated pathogenic mechanisms.

title: Refractory high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to chronic paracetamol use: a case report
summary: Chronic paracetamol use at therapeutic doses causes pyroglutamic acidosis, a form of high anion gap metabolic acidosis increasingly recognized in clinical practice. The mechanism involves paracetamol's metabolite N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine depleting hepatic glutathione, which disrupts the gamma-glutamyl cycle and leads to pathological accumulation of 5-oxoproline (pyroglutamic acid). Women are particularly susceptible to this complication due to lower baseline glutathione stores and differences in glutathione transferase activity compared to men. N-acetylcysteine therapy effectively reverses paracetamol-induced pyroglutamic acidosis by directly replenishing intracellular glutathione and restoring normal feedback inhibition of the gamma-glutamyl cycle. Elevated urinary 5-oxoproline levels serve as a diagnostic marker confirming pyroglutamic acidosis, with estimates suggesting this metabolic derangement accounts for approximately 5% of cases with unexplained high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This case highlights the importance of considering paracetamol toxicity in refractory acidosis, particularly in women with concurrent risk factors for glutathione depletion.

title: Comparison of the short-term effects of dienogest and oral contraceptives on pain and quality of life in women with endometriosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility through cyclic hemorrhage and inflammatory stimulation at ectopic lesions characterized by elevated estrogen receptors and reduced progesterone receptors. Oral contraceptives suppress ovulation and reduce estrogen production via hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis inhibition, while their progestogen components induce endometrial atrophy and inhibit prostaglandin synthesis to alleviate pain. Dienogest, a selective progesterone receptor agonist, demonstrates superior efficacy in improving quality-of-life measures including emotional well-being, social support, work function, and sexual relationships compared to oral contraceptives, while directly inhibiting ectopic endometrial proliferation through COX-2 downregulation and angiogenesis inhibition. Although oral contraceptives show greater short-term pain reduction for pelvic pain and dyspareunia, dienogest produces significantly better outcomes on endometriosis-specific quality-of-life scales (EHP-5, EHP-30) and physical component scores. Both treatments carry distinct side effect profiles, with oral contraceptives associated with hand numbness and weight gain, while dienogest carries progesterone-related effects including irregular vaginal bleeding and decreased bone density.

title: Safety and efficacy of vilaprisan in Japanese women with fibroids: The Phase 3 ASTEROID 8 trial
summary: The Phase 3 ASTEROID 8 trial evaluated vilaprisan, a selective progesterone receptor modulator, as a treatment for uterine fibroids and associated heavy menstrual bleeding in Japanese women. Vilaprisan 2 mg/day demonstrated substantial efficacy, rapidly inducing amenorrhea (median 4 days) and reducing mean bleeding days to approximately 1.4 per 28-day cycle, with high response rates across both treatment arms. Common treatment-emergent adverse events included nasopharyngitis, intermenstrual bleeding, hot flushes, and headaches, though the majority were mild in intensity; notably, comprehensive endometrial monitoring revealed no cases of malignant neoplasm or endometrial hyperplasia, and liver function remained normal. While serious adverse events occurred in 3.4% of women, including rare cases of hypopituitarism and elevated aldosterone, the safety profile was generally favorable and consistent with other selective progesterone receptor modulators. These findings support vilaprisan as an effective therapeutic option for managing symptomatic uterine fibroids in East Asian populations, who experience a higher disease burden compared to other ethnic groups.

title: Clinical value of hysteroscopic levonorgestrel intrauterine system suture fixation in treating adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis causes significant clinical morbidity through dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility, with pathophysiology involving enlarged uterine cavities, abnormal myometrial contractions, and cervical laxity. The levonorgestrel intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) treats adenomyosis by delivering continuous levonorgestrel that inhibits prostaglandins and thromboxanes while enhancing apoptosis through Fas antigen upregulation and Bcl-2 reduction, resulting in reduced dysmenorrhea, menstrual bleeding, and uterine volume. However, conventional LNG-IUS placement suffers from high displacement and expulsion rates due to the device's fixed dimensions and soft fixation in enlarged uteri. Hysteroscopic LNG-IUS suture fixation substantially improves outcomes by anchoring the device to the uterine wall, reducing expulsion rates to 4.8% compared to conventional placement, while maintaining comparable symptom relief. Both approaches effectively alleviate dysmenorrhea and menorrhagia, though hysteroscopic fixation offers superior device retention and represents a superior alternative to GnRH-α agonists and hysterectomy for adenomyosis management. The primary limitation is postoperative irregular vaginal spotting occurring in approximately 38% of hysteroscopic fixation patients.

title: Efficacy of Elagolix and Relugolix for the Treatment of Pelvic Pain in Patients With Endometriosis: A Systematic Review
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory gynecological condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth, strongly associated with debilitating symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. This systematic review demonstrates that GnRH antagonists—specifically relugolix and elagolix—effectively reduce endometriosis-associated pain by reversibly blocking GnRH receptor binding and inhibiting gonadotropin release, achieving reductions of 59-84% in dysmenorrhea and non-menstrual pelvic pain while decreasing opioid use by up to 15.9%. A key therapeutic advantage of these agents over conventional GnRH agonists is their favorable safety profile; when combined with hormonal add-back therapy (estrogen and progestogens), they maintain bone mineral density loss below 1% and avoid the severe climacteric symptoms, hot flashes, mood disturbances, and vaginal dryness associated with prolonged GnRH agonist use. Evidence from major randomized trials including SPIRIT 1 and 2 confirms that both relugolix and elagolix combination therapy significantly improve quality of life and daily functioning while reducing the need for concomitant analgesic medications. These findings establish GnRH antagonists as safe and effective long-term treatment options for endometriosis-related pain, offering superior tolerability compared to existing hormonal therapies.

title: Relugolix’s impact on endometriosis-associated pain and quality of life: a meta-analysis of EHP-30 outcomes
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic gynecological condition characterized by debilitating pain and infertility risk, traditionally managed with GnRH agonists that carry significant hypoestrogenic side effects including bone mineral density loss and vasomotor symptoms. Relugolix, an oral GnRH antagonist, emerged as a promising alternative that effectively treats endometriosis-associated pain including dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia while demonstrating superiority over placebo in improving EHP-30 pain domain scores (mean difference 6.77 points), with the 40 mg dose showing superior efficacy. Beyond pain reduction, relugolix significantly enhanced multiple dimensions of health-related quality of life, including emotional well-being, social support, and self-image, with substantially increased rates of pain domain responders (odds ratio 3.245). The therapeutic benefits were most pronounced at 24-week follow-up and with combination therapy approaches, though relugolix showed numerically lower effectiveness compared to the traditional GnRH agonist leuprorelin. Common adverse events associated with GnRH antagonists include hot flushes, fatigue, and headaches, which remain important clinical considerations despite relugolix's improved side effect profile compared to earlier hormonal therapies.

title: A randomized, double blind, placebo-controlled pilot study to assess the efficacy of erenumab in individuals with temporomandibular disorder
journal: Journal of Oral & Facial Pain and Headache
summary: This randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled pilot study evaluated erenumab, a calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) receptor antagonist, as a potential treatment for temporomandibular disorder (TMD) pain. The rationale was based on the high co-occurrence of TMD and chronic migraine, both conditions involving the trigeminovascular pathway, and erenumab's established efficacy in reducing migraine frequency through CGRP receptor blockade. However, the trial found that erenumab was not efficacious in reducing TMD myalgic pain in the 30 subjects with relatively mild pain, with no significant treatment effects on pain interference, severity, jaw function limitation, or somatic symptom severity at any timepoint. Adverse events were more frequent in the erenumab group, including constipation, nausea, drowsiness, injection site irritation, and increased depression and anxiety scores on the PHQ-4 scale compared to placebo. These findings suggest that despite the shared neurobiological pathways between TMD and migraine, CGRP receptor antagonism may not translate therapeutically to TMD pain management, at least in populations with mild pain severity.

title: Genotoxicity and acute toxicity of Hyunburikyung-tang: assessing the safety of prescribing traditional Korean medicine for dysmenorrhea
summary: Hyunburikyung-tang, a traditional Korean herbal medicine clinically used to treat dysmenorrhea and menstrual disorders, exerts its therapeutic effects by suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines, prostaglandin E2, and nitric oxide—key mediators of menstrual pain. The herbal formulation contains bioactive compounds including decursin from Angelica gigas Nakai root, which inhibits NF-κB-mediated inflammatory responses, and paeoniflorin from Paeonia lactiflora, which acts as an analgesic through NMDA receptor interaction and COX-2 inhibition. Additionally, Cnidium officinale in the formulation downregulates MMP-9, a major factor implicated in endometriosis-associated pelvic pain and dysmenorrhea. Safety assessments demonstrate that Hyunburikyung-tang does not induce acute toxicity, genotoxicity, chromosomal aberrations, or organ damage at therapeutic doses, showing no mutagenic activity in bacterial and mammalian test systems. However, the formulation contains potentially problematic ingredients: peach seeds (Prunus persica) and safflower (Carthamus tinctorius) have documented teratogenic and reproductive toxicity effects in experimental models, raising safety concerns for pregnant or potentially pregnant patients. These findings support the clinical efficacy of Hyunburikyung-tang for dysmenorrhea management while highlighting the need for careful patient screening regarding reproductive status.

title: Awake craniotomy for brain tumor resection: anesthetic management and clinical experience from a high complexity hospital in Latin America
summary:
# Article Summary

This article describes anesthetic management for awake craniotomy, a neurosurgical technique used to resect brain tumors in eloquent cortical areas while preserving neurological function through real-time intraoperative monitoring. The primary pharmacological approach combined dexmedetomidine and remifentanil to provide sedation, analgesia, and anxiolysis while maintaining respiratory drive and enabling successful neurological mapping of speech, motor, and sensory functions. Dexmedetomidine demonstrated neuroprotective advantages and fewer respiratory adverse effects compared to alternative agents, though the combination caused bradycardia as the most frequent cardiovascular event. Perioperative management additionally included regional scalp blocks for comfort, seizure prophylaxis with levetiracetam and lacosamide, antiemetic prophylaxis with dexamethasone and ondansetron, and tranexamic acid for blood management. The clinical experience from this Latin American hospital demonstrates that this multimodal anesthetic approach successfully enabled safe tumor resection while preserving critical neurological functions in patients with brain tumors located in eloquent areas.

title: Is there an association between pelvic pain and gender-affirming testosterone therapy in trans masculine adolescents? An exploratory cross-sectional study
summary: This exploratory study examined pelvic pain in transgender masculine adolescents receiving gender-affirming testosterone therapy, revealing a complex relationship between hormonal treatment and pain outcomes. While testosterone therapy reduced overall pelvic pain prevalence from 85.7% in untreated individuals to 69.4% in those on testosterone, likely through anti-inflammatory effects and suppression of ovulation and menstruation, paradoxically testosterone was associated with increased orgasm-associated pelvic pain and breakthrough bleeding in some users. The cohort experienced substantial disability from pelvic pain, with nearly 70% unable to participate in extracurricular activities and 50% experiencing school or work absenteeism, with pain most commonly localized to the suprapubic region and characterized as cramping or aching. Management strategies employed included both pharmacological approaches (paracetamol, NSAIDs, oral progestins) and non-pharmacological interventions (heat therapy, physical exercise), though notably only 21.4% of participants reported improvement in preexisting pelvic pain after starting testosterone. The cohort demonstrated high rates of comorbid mental health conditions including anxiety (74%) and depression (67%), as well as nearly universal prior dysmenorrhea (96.9%), suggesting that pain management in transmasculine youth requires attention to both hormonal and psychosocial factors.

title: Perioperative Anesthetic and Hormonal Considerations in a Patient With Anti-glutamic Acid Decarboxylase Encephalitis: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a 41-year-old woman with anti-GAD (glutamic acid decarboxylase) encephalitis and endometriosis, highlighting the interplay between autoimmune neurological disease and hormonal factors. Anti-GAD antibodies depleted GAD levels, impairing GABA synthesis and creating a hyperexcitable neurological state associated with seizures, movement disorders, and cognitive decline. The patient's symptoms were significantly exacerbated by hormonal fluctuations from her menstrual cycle, endometriosis-related inflammation, and pro-inflammatory cytokine release, suggesting that estrogen-driven endometriosis directly worsened her neurological condition. Treatment with immunotherapy (corticosteroids, immunoglobulins, and rituximab) provided limited improvement, but suppression of endometriosis through bilateral oophorectomy produced marked improvement in neurological symptoms, indicating that controlling estrogen-dependent disease activity was crucial. Perioperatively, the patient required careful management with benzodiazepine prophylaxis and GABA-enhancing anesthetic agents (propofol and midazolam) to prevent hyperexcitability and seizures, with particular attention to drug-drug interactions involving aprepitant's inhibition of cytochrome P450 metabolism. This case underscores the critical relationship between endometriosis-driven hormonal dysfunction and autoimmune encephalitis progression, where definitive hormonal management achieved superior neurological outcomes compared to immunotherapy alone.

title: MON-166 Preclinical characterization of a highly potent and selective HSD17B1 inhibitor for the treatment of endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by abnormally elevated concentrations of 17β-estradiol (E2) within ectopic lesions, which is driven by upregulation of the enzyme HSD17B1 that converts 17β-estrone (E1) to the more potent E2. GenSci146 is a highly potent and selective HSD17B1 inhibitor that effectively blocks E1-to-E2 conversion in both recombinant enzyme assays and ex vivo endometriosis patient tissue samples. In a transgenic mouse model, GenSci146 dose-dependently reversed E1-stimulated uterine growth without affecting estrogen receptor signaling, demonstrating its selectivity for HSD17B1 inhibition over estrogen receptor modulation. These preclinical findings establish GenSci146 as a promising candidate for treating endometriosis by reducing local estrogen production within ectopic lesions through selective HSD17B1 inhibition.

title: SUN-124 Impact of GnRH Agonist Therapy on Bone Density in a Eunuch Individual
summary: Goserelin, a GnRH agonist widely used in endometriosis management, effectively suppresses both testosterone and estradiol levels, with one patient achieving testosterone levels below 50 ng/dL after two years of therapy. While this hormonal suppression successfully reduced dysphoria and mood instability associated with elevated testosterone, it came at a significant cost to skeletal health, resulting in an 11.8% decline in bone mineral density at the hip. This adverse effect is consistent with broader complications of GnRH agonist therapy, which include osteoporosis, depression, and metabolic syndrome. The study highlights a critical clinical trade-off: although goserelin effectively treats endometriosis and provides psychological benefit through testosterone suppression, prolonged use substantially increases osteoporosis risk, comparable to the 4.3% vertebral bone loss observed in cisgender women with endometriosis. These findings underscore the importance of monitoring bone density and considering adjunctive therapies or treatment duration limits when prescribing GnRH agonists for endometriosis management.

title: MON-196 Hormone Replacement Therapy In Menopausal Patient With Symptomatic Endometriosis And Bone Metabolism: A Case Report
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent condition that can persist into the postmenopausal years, affecting up to 4% of postmenopausal women, yet treating it remains clinically challenging due to the limitations of conventional therapies. Standard first-line oral progestins have a 33% failure rate, while GnRH agonists such as goserelin effectively suppress endometriosis but induce severe hypoestrogenic side effects including vasomotor symptoms, vaginal atrophy, and bone loss—particularly problematic in menopausal patients. This case demonstrates that a novel combination approach using the selective progestin R2323, which exhibits antiestrogenic and antiprogestogenic activity with mild androgenic properties, combined with low-dose transdermal estradiol, successfully treated both endometriotic pain and pelvic symptoms while simultaneously improving bone mineral density over two years. The androgenic potential of R2323 likely contributed to improved bone health, while the low-dose estradiol supported metabolic and cardiovascular health without significantly reactivating endometriotic lesions. This integrated hormonal strategy offers a potential alternative for menopausal women with symptomatic endometriosis who experience intolerable side effects from conventional therapies, balancing endometriosis suppression with preservation of bone integrity and quality of life.

title: Controlled Clinical Studies of Combined Oral Contraceptives for Dysmenorrhea in China: A Systematic Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review of controlled clinical trials from China evaluates combined oral contraceptives (COCs) for treating dysmenorrhea, which occurs in two forms: primary dysmenorrhea related to prostaglandin overproduction, and secondary dysmenorrhea caused by endometriosis or adenomyosis. COCs relieve dysmenorrhea through multiple mechanisms including ovulation suppression, inhibition of endometrial proliferation-to-secretion transition, and reduction of endometrial prostaglandin production. For primary dysmenorrhea, COCs are more effective than placebo and comparable to nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, while for secondary dysmenorrhea, continuous dosing regimens show greater efficacy than cyclic regimens, though with higher rates of abnormal vaginal bleeding. GnRH agonists demonstrate superior efficacy compared to cyclic COCs by inducing amenorrhea and achieving complete symptom resolution, while continuous COC regimens offer comparable effectiveness with fewer systemic side effects. The most common adverse effects of COC use include abnormal vaginal bleeding, breast pain, nausea, headache, and mood changes, with continuous administration significantly increasing bleeding complications.

title: Between contraception and hormones: a qualitative analysis of the lived experiences of former contraceptive pill users
summary:
# Summary

This qualitative study examined the lived experiences of former oral contraceptive pill (OCP) users, revealing that while the pill effectively treats menstrual pain, irregular periods, and conditions like polycystic ovary syndrome, nearly all former users reported significant adverse effects including mood swings, depressive symptoms, reduced libido, migraines, headaches, weight gain, and irregular bleeding that ultimately drove discontinuation. The research demonstrates that contraceptive decision-making is a complex, multifactorial process shaped by biopsychological factors, personal values, circumstances, and relationships, with many users experiencing frequent switching, stopping, and restarting of pill use based on accumulated side effects. A critical finding was the disconnect between healthcare providers and patients in contraceptive counseling, combined with growing social media influence promoting "natural" hormone-free alternatives, which contributed to rejection of hormonal contraception despite health benefits. The study emphasizes that quantitative approaches fail to capture embodied experiences and calls for incorporating women's informal knowledge and lived experiences into future contraceptive counseling and reproductive justice frameworks to improve patient-provider relationships and individualized care.

title: The Association Between Medroxyprogesterone Acetate Exposure and Cerebral Meningioma Among a Medicaid Population
summary: This epidemiological study examined the association between depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (dMPA) exposure and cerebral meningioma development in a Medicaid population. Medroxyprogesterone acetate is a synthetic progestin widely used to treat endometriosis, abnormal uterine bleeding, contraception, and endometrial carcinoma, and it works by binding to progesterone receptors in the hypothalamus, pituitary gland, and female reproductive tract to inhibit gonadotropin-releasing hormone and suppress ovulation. The study found a strong association between prolonged dMPA exposure (≥2 years) and cerebral meningioma risk, with odds ratios approximately 1.87–3.80 depending on the comparison group, whereas oral medroxyprogesterone acetate showed no association. This increased risk likely stems from sustained serum concentrations of MPA providing prolonged progesterone receptor stimulation, as meningiomas express high levels of progesterone receptors (33–89%), which are associated with tumor location, grade, and recurrence patterns. Notably, discontinuation of dMPA in some patients resulted in tumor shrinkage, suggesting the association may be reversible. These findings have important clinical implications for women using dMPA for endometriosis management and other indications, warranting careful risk-benefit assessment in this population.

title: Restoring TADS: RIAT reanalysis of the Treatment for Adolescents with Depression Study
journal: The International Journal of Risk & Safety in Medicine
summary: This reanalysis of the Treatment for Adolescents with Depression Study (TADS) examined the efficacy and safety of fluoxetine, cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), and their combination in treating adolescent major depressive disorder. The combination treatment (fluoxetine plus CBT) demonstrated superior efficacy, producing significant symptom reduction on the Children's Depression Rating Scale at 12 weeks and achieving higher Clinical Global Impression-Improvement response rates (73.72%) compared with fluoxetine alone (64.16%) or placebo. However, the reanalysis uncovered substantially higher rates of adverse events with fluoxetine, including 66% of reported adverse events occurring in fluoxetine-treated participants, 22 serious adverse events, and notably, 11 unreported suicide-related adverse events that had not been disclosed in the original publication. Fluoxetine monotherapy showed no significant advantage over placebo for symptom reduction, while CBT alone similarly showed no significant difference from placebo on primary outcome measures. These findings underscore the importance of combining pharmacological and psychosocial interventions for adolescent depression while highlighting previously underreported safety concerns associated with fluoxetine treatment in this population.

title: Contraceptive use in France in 2012 and 2022: a descriptive analysis of two repeated nationwide cross-sectional studies
summary:
# Summary

This study examines contraceptive use patterns in France between 2012 and 2022, with emphasis on the safety profiles and therapeutic applications of different contraceptive methods. Combined oral contraceptives (COCs) are recognized for treating endometriosis and menstrual cycle disorders, as are levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine devices, reflecting established non-contraceptive benefits highlighted in French medical guidelines. However, the choice of COC formulation carries important cardiovascular implications, as third and fourth generation progestogens are associated with elevated cardiovascular risk compared to second generation formulations. Additionally, COCs in general carry an elevated risk of venous thromboembolism and pulmonary embolism, which generated significant public concern during the study period. These findings underscore the need to balance the therapeutic benefits of hormonal contraceptives for conditions like endometriosis against their thromboembolic and cardiovascular risks, with particular attention to progestogen generation when selecting appropriate formulations.

title: Medical treatment of fibroids: FIGO best practice guidance
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary

This FIGO guidance provides comprehensive evidence for medical management of uterine fibroids, which affect approximately 30% of women symptomatically with abnormal uterine bleeding, anemia, pelvic pain, and infertility. Multiple drug classes effectively reduce fibroid-related symptoms through distinct mechanisms: tranexamic acid and oral progestins reduce menstrual blood loss via fibrinolysis inhibition and endometrial stabilization respectively, while combined oral contraceptives reduce abnormal bleeding by 13.4% and fibroid growth by 17%. GnRH agonists achieve approximately 50% fibroid volume reduction and improve preoperative hemoglobin levels and reduce intraoperative bleeding, whereas newer GnRH antagonists like elagolix, linzagolix, and relugolix offer faster symptom relief with better tolerability profiles. Selective progesterone receptor modulators (SPRMs) including ulipristal acetate exploit progesterone's central role in fibroid pathogenesis to reduce both fibroid size and menstrual blood loss while increasing amenorrhea rates. The levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system provides sustained symptom control with 86.8% reduction in menstrual blood loss within one month, offering an effective non-systemic alternative for eligible candidates.

title: Dual-route embedding-aware graph neural networks for drug repositioning
summary:
# Summary

DREAM-GNN is a dual-route embedding-aware graph neural network framework designed to improve drug repositioning and discovery by integrating multiview deep learning with biomedical domain knowledge. The model systematically combines pretrained embeddings from ChemBERTa (drugs), ESM-2 (proteins), and BioBERT (diseases) while employing dual-route encoding that optimizes both topology and similarity relationships—the topology encoder enhances AUROC while the similarity encoder improves AUPRC. Evaluated on benchmark datasets, DREAM-GNN substantially outperforms competing methods including DFDRNN, AdaDR, DRAGNN, and BNNR, achieving 72.20% AUPRC and 97.38% AUROC on standard benchmarks. In endometriosis-relevant applications, the framework identified leuprolide, a GnRH1-targeting GnRH receptor superagonist, as an established therapeutic agent treating endometriosis, prostate cancer, and uterine leiomyomata, while also uncovering novel associations such as calcitriol's relationship to osteogenic sarcoma through vitamin D receptor signaling. The model's robustness derives from key architectural components including pretrained embeddings, data augmentation strategies, and parameter sharing, positioning DREAM-GNN as a generalizable computational framework for precision medicine and drug repurposing applications.

title: Investigation of the Relevance of CYP3A4 Inhibition on the Pharmacokinetics of the Novel P2X3 Antagonist Filapixant: Results of In Vitro Explorations and a Fixed-Sequence Clinical Trial with Itraconazole in Healthy Volunteers
summary:
# Summary

Filapixant is a novel P2X3 receptor antagonist in development for refractory chronic cough and other conditions including endometriosis, osteoarthritis, and bladder disorders. The compound is rapidly absorbed and suitable for once-daily dosing with a terminal half-life of 6-10 hours, but undergoes complex hepatic metabolism involving multiple cytochrome P450 enzymes, with CYP3A4 being the predominant pathway. This clinical pharmacokinetics study demonstrated that the strong CYP3A4 inhibitor itraconazole substantially increased filapixant exposure, elevating both peak plasma concentration and area under the curve by approximately two-fold and prolonging the half-life from 12.1 to 22.8 hours. Importantly, the itraconazole-induced increase in filapixant exposure did not produce taste disturbances—the major adverse effect associated with higher plasma concentrations above 400 µg/L—because co-administration resulted in maximum concentrations around 172 µg/L. The findings support that strong CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein inhibitors require filapixant dose adjustment to maintain safety, though the margin of safety observed in this study was reassuring for clinical use.

title: The Effect of Bazedoxifene and Fulvestrant for Preventing Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome: An Experimental Study
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) is a serious complication of fertility treatment driven by elevated estradiol and human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG), which trigger excessive vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), and interleukin-6 (IL-6) production, leading to ascites, edema, and tissue damage. This experimental study investigated two estrogen-modulating agents—bazedoxifene, a selective estrogen receptor modulator that also inhibits IL-6 signaling through GP130, and fulvestrant, a pure estrogen receptor antagonist that promotes ER degradation and inhibits VEGF expression—as potential preventive therapies in an OHSS rat model. Both drugs significantly reduced OHSS severity by decreasing ascites formation, weight gain, collagen fiber deposition, and stromal estrogen receptor expression compared to untreated OHSS controls. Fulvestrant's mechanism involved direct ER blockade and nuclear translocation inhibition, while bazedoxifene combined tissue-selective estrogen modulation with anti-inflammatory activity targeting the IL-6/GP130/STAT3 pathway. These findings suggest that both pharmacological approaches may offer therapeutic benefit in preventing OHSS, with fulvestrant's pure antiestrogenic effects and bazedoxifene's dual ER-modulatory and anti-inflammatory mechanisms complementing each other in reducing the pathophysiological hallmarks of this condition.

title: Gene Expression Profiling and Pathway Analysis of the Effect of Dienogest on Ovarian Endometriosis: A Comparative Study
summary:
# Summary

This gene expression profiling study demonstrates that dienogest, a selective progesterone receptor agonist, treats endometriosis through multiple complementary mechanisms. The drug alleviates endometriosis-related pain by suppressing ovulation and reducing serum estrogen and plasma estradiol levels, while simultaneously inhibiting angiogenesis and promoting apoptosis of endometrial tissue. Dienogest modulates critical inflammatory and immune pathways in treated endometriosis, notably downregulating genes associated with chronic inflammation (HLA-DOB and ELOVL2) and cell migration (LRRC15) while upregulating detoxification mechanisms (GSTM1). The altered gene expression profile in dienogest-treated ovarian endometriomas reveals enhanced immune regulation through pathways controlling T cell proliferation, leukocyte activation, and cytokine production. These findings suggest that dienogest's therapeutic efficacy extends beyond hormonal suppression to include immune system modulation and potential delay of endometriosis progression toward malignant transformation.

title: Comparing the analgesic efficacy of liposomal bupivacaine versus bupivacaine for postoperative pain management in breast surgery: protocol for a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review protocol evaluates the analgesic efficacy of liposomal bupivacaine compared to standard bupivacaine for postoperative pain management in breast surgery. Liposomal bupivacaine is a sustained-release formulation that encapsulates bupivacaine within lipid bilayers, blocking sodium channels in nerve fibers to produce reversible conduction blockade with an elimination half-life approximately three times longer than standard bupivacaine. Clinical evidence indicates that liposomal bupivacaine reduces acute postoperative pain scores and decreases systemic opioid consumption more effectively than standard formulations. Since inadequate pain control is closely associated with delayed postoperative recovery and prolonged hospital stays, improving analgesia through extended-release local anesthetics could significantly enhance perioperative outcomes. If liposomal bupivacaine demonstrates clear superiority, it may be more widely integrated into enhanced recovery protocols to optimize pain management while reducing perioperative opioid requirements and accelerating postoperative recovery.

title: Dynamic clinical trial success rates for drugs in the 21st century
summary: This analysis of 21st century clinical trial dynamics reveals that vilaprisan has been evaluated as a potential pharmacological treatment for endometriosis, alongside its investigation for uterine leiomyoma. The study demonstrates broader patterns in drug development success rates, showing that overall clinical trial success rates have declined since the early 2000s, though they have recently begun to recover from a plateau. Notably, repurposed drugs and anti-COVID-19 therapeutics exhibited unexpectedly low clinical trial success rates compared to newly developed compounds, suggesting that drug repurposing strategies may face greater efficacy or safety challenges in clinical translation. The research illustrates these trends through multiple examples, including drugs that successfully transitioned across disease indications, such as cladribine and alemtuzumab, which gained approvals for multiple conditions. These findings highlight the complex landscape of contemporary drug development, where traditional single-indication development pathways may offer higher success probabilities than adaptive trial designs or therapeutic repurposing approaches.

title: Ultrasound imaging-guided protocol for monitoring tumor growth in orthotopic rat model of hepatocellular carcinoma
summary: This article describes the development and validation of an ultrasound imaging protocol for monitoring hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) growth in an orthotopic rat model. Hepatocellular carcinoma was established by implanting N1S1 cells into the left liver lobe of Sprague Dawley rats, with ultrasound imaging revealing well-defined hypoechoic nodules by day 7 post-implantation. Ultrasound-derived tumor volume measurements demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity, with robust intra- and interobserver reproducibility and strong correlation to gross caliper measurements, histopathological analysis, and microcomputed tomography imaging. The study validated sorafenib as an effective therapeutic agent, which significantly reduced tumor volume compared to untreated controls. Orthotopic syngeneic rat models offer advantages over xenograft approaches by preserving intact immune function and stromal components, making them valuable for preclinical assessment of anti-HCC therapies. This ultrasound imaging approach enables longitudinal monitoring while reducing animal numbers and providing statistically robust data for future HCC drug development studies.

title: C1 esterase inhibitor (C1–INH) response as a supportive diagnostic criterion for patients with suspected hereditary angioedema with normal C1–INH∗
summary:
# Summary

Hereditary angioedema with normal C1-INH (HAE-nl-C1INH) is a rare disorder characterized by recurrent angioedema episodes that fail to respond to conventional antihistamine and mast cell-targeted therapies. The pathophysiology involves genetic mutations that impair regulation of the kallikrein-kinin system, leading to excessive bradykinin production and resulting angioedema. This study demonstrates that a favorable clinical response to recombinant C1-INH (rhC1-INH) replacement therapy can serve as a useful supportive diagnostic criterion for confirming HAE-nl-C1INH diagnosis in suspected cases. HAE-specific medications including icatibant, ecallantide, and lanadelumab-flyo effectively treat acute attacks by reducing bradykinin levels. The patients studied frequently presented with comorbid autoimmune and allergic conditions including hypothyroidism, rheumatoid arthritis, and Sjögren syndrome, suggesting potential shared immunological mechanisms. These findings support the incorporation of medication responsiveness testing into diagnostic algorithms for HAE-nl-C1INH, particularly when genetic testing yields inconclusive results.

title: Perinatal Outcomes After the Use of Kampo Medicines Containing Ephedra During Pregnancy
summary: This pharmacoepidemiological study examined the safety of Kampo medicines containing ephedra during pregnancy, focusing on perinatal outcomes. Kampo medicines such as kakkonto, shoseiryuto, and maoto—which contain ephedra and its active alkaloids ephedrine and pseudoephedrine—are commonly used in Japan to treat common cold symptoms during pregnancy. Although ephedrine is known to increase blood pressure, heart rate, and uterine muscle contractility while potentially causing hemodynamic disturbances, the study found no statistically significant associations between maternal use of ephedra-containing Kampo medicines and adverse perinatal outcomes including caesarean section, preterm birth, low birth weight, small for gestational age, low Apgar scores, or congenital malformations. These findings provide reassuring evidence regarding the safety profile of ephedra-containing Kampo medicines as cold remedies during pregnancy, though the sympathomimetic properties of ephedrine warrant continued cautious use.

title: Subcapsular Hepatic Hemorrhage: An Uncommon and Catastrophic Manifestation of Metastatic Melanoma
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents an uncommon but catastrophic complication of metastatic melanoma: spontaneous subcapsular hepatic hemorrhage. Metastatic melanoma causes hepatic hemorrhage through tumor invasion that breaches the hepatic capsule and erodes blood vessels, a risk heightened by the hypervascular nature of melanoma metastases. The patient presented with acute epigastric pain radiating to the right shoulder, vomiting, and syncope—clinical manifestations of diaphragmatic irritation and hemodynamic instability caused by the expanding hematoma. Contrast-enhanced CT imaging confirmed the diagnosis and subsequent follow-up imaging documented hematoma expansion. Management involved supportive care with morphine for pain control, ondansetron for nausea, dexamethasone for symptom management, and discontinuation of nephrotoxic medications. Though hepatic metastases occur in 10–20% of advanced melanoma patients, spontaneous hemorrhagic presentations remain exceptionally rare, yet carry a poor prognosis associated with rapid disease progression and unfavorable outcomes.

title: Network pharmacology and experimental validation reveal clotrimazole alleviates dextran sulfate sodium–induced ulcerative colitis by inhibiting intestinal senescence
journal: The Journal of Pharmacology and Experimental Therapeutics
summary: This study demonstrates that clotrimazole alleviates dextran sulfate sodium (DSS)-induced ulcerative colitis by inhibiting intestinal cellular senescence through modulation of key signaling pathways. DSS promotes colitis development by inducing cellular senescence in colonic epithelial tissue, disrupting AMPK signaling while excessively activating MAPK pathways—molecular events that drive chronic inflammation and tissue damage. Clotrimazole, identified through network pharmacology, exhibits potent antisenescence activity by restoring AMPK activity and suppressing MAPK activation in DSS-treated colon mucosa, thereby reducing senescence-associated colonic injury. The anti-inflammatory properties of clotrimazole further contribute to its therapeutic efficacy against ulcerative colitis. These findings establish cellular senescence as a critical mechanism in UC pathogenesis and identify clotrimazole as a promising therapeutic agent that targets this underlying biological process to mitigate disease-related colonic damage.

title: Case Report: Long-term maintenance of GnRH-a combined with dienogest for thoracic endometriosis syndrome
summary: This case report demonstrates that thoracic endometriosis syndrome, a rare manifestation of endometriosis involving ectopic endometrial tissue in the lungs, causes menstrual-related pulmonary symptoms including haemoptysis, pneumothorax, and haemothorax in affected patients. The authors present two cases successfully managed with a combination pharmacological strategy: initial treatment with gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists (GnRH-a) followed by long-term maintenance with dienogest, a selective progesterone receptor modulator. GnRH-a suppresses follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinising hormone, rapidly inhibiting endometrial tissue proliferation, while dienogest provides sustained disease control through its antiproliferative, antiangiogenic, and anti-inflammatory effects on endometriotic implants. This combined approach successfully resolved haemoptysis and reduced lung lesions in both patients while avoiding the significant adverse effects associated with long-term GnRH-a monotherapy, such as bone loss and perimenopausal symptoms. The findings support combined GnRH-a and dienogest maintenance therapy as an effective pharmacological alternative to surgery for managing thoracic endometriosis syndrome.

title: Hysteroscopic suture fixation for levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system in adenomyosis feasibility and therapeutic outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium, causes abnormal uterine bleeding, dysmenorrhea, and infertility through a mechanism involving genetic and epigenetic alterations that increase intracellular aromatase activity, leading to excessive estrogen production and inflammatory fibrotic tissue remodeling. The levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) effectively treats these symptoms by suppressing estrogen receptor expression, inhibiting estrogen-dependent endometrial proliferation, inducing glandular atrophy, and downregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-6 and tumor necrosis factor-alpha. However, adenomyosis-associated uterine enlargement significantly increases LNG-IUS expulsion rates; hysteroscopic suture fixation dramatically reduces expulsion to 2.1% compared to traditional placement rates. In a clinical cohort, hysteroscopic suture fixation of LNG-IUS achieved substantial improvements in dysmenorrhea severity and menstrual flow reduction, with elevated hemoglobin levels and 94% patient satisfaction, though irregular bleeding emerged as a common side effect requiring management with tranexamic acid or other interventions in some cases. These findings establish hysteroscopic suture fixation as a feasible and therapeutically superior approach to LNG-IUS placement in adenomyosis patients, particularly those with risk factors for device expulsion.

title: Potency of oral gonadotropin-releasing hormone antagonist as endometriosis-associated pain novel treatments: An updated meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that oral gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) antagonists—elagolix, linzagolix, and relugolix—effectively treat endometriosis-associated pain by suppressing estrogen through receptor blockade and downstream inhibition of luteinizing hormone and estradiol production. These agents significantly reduced all three primary pain phenotypes: dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and non-menstrual chronic pelvic pain, with symptom relief correlating strongly to consistent estradiol suppression (20–60 pg/mL). Beyond pain management, GnRH antagonists improved overall health status and patient perception of treatment benefit, offering advantages over first-line progestogens (which face ~33% progesterone resistance) and second-line GnRH agonists (limited by flare effects and restricted duration). However, the therapeutic benefits are offset by dose-dependent adverse effects including hot flushes (3.61-fold higher incidence), elevated low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (8.96% increase), and reduced bone mineral density in the spine—metabolic consequences of estrogen suppression that warrant monitoring during long-term treatment. These findings establish oral GnRH antagonists as promising pharmacological alternatives for endometriosis-associated pain management, though careful patient selection and risk stratification remain essential given their metabolic and bone effects.

title: Delphi Consensus on Attenuated Androgen Use for Long‐Term Prophylaxis in Hereditary Angioedema: AURA Project
summary:
# Summary

Hereditary angioedema (HAE) results from C1 inhibitor (C1INH) deficiency or dysfunction, which impairs regulation of the kallikrein-bradykinin cascade, leading to excessive bradykinin production that triggers vasodilation and increased vascular permeability, causing characteristic swelling and inflammation. Danazol and other attenuated androgens have historically served as cornerstones of long-term prophylaxis for HAE; however, danazol use is associated with a substantial burden of adverse effects including virilization, weight gain, dyslipidemia, hepatotoxicity, cardiovascular complications, metabolic disturbances (hyperglycemia, hyperinsulinemia), psychological effects, and increased cancer risk (ovarian, hepatic, and prostate). Newer therapeutic options for HAE long-term prophylaxis, including plasma-derived C1INH, lanadelumab, and berotralstat, offer alternatives that may mitigate these risks. The dose-dependent nature of danazol's adverse effects and evidence that HAE itself is associated with endothelial dysfunction highlight the need for careful therapeutic selection and monitoring. This Delphi consensus emphasizes the importance of individualizing HAE management strategies to balance attack prevention with minimization of treatment-related morbidity.

title: Tirzepatide as a Potential Disease-Modifying Therapy in Lipedema: A Narrative Review on Bridging Metabolism, Inflammation, and Fibrosis
summary:
# Summary

Lipedema is characterized by a complex pathophysiology involving adipocyte hypertrophy, chronic inflammation, extracellular matrix fibrosis, mitochondrial dysfunction, and sex steroid imbalance, with altered estrogen receptor signaling and reduced thermogenic capacity playing central roles. The disease is driven by M1 macrophage infiltration that secretes pro-inflammatory cytokines, combined with elevated intracrine estradiol production and progesterone resistance, which promotes adipocyte proliferation and fibrotic remodeling. Tirzepatide, a dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist approved for obesity and diabetes, demonstrates potential as a disease-modifying therapy by simultaneously addressing multiple lipedema pathways through improved insulin sensitivity, upregulation of uncoupling protein-1, and activation of beige adipocytes to enhance thermogenesis. Additionally, tirzepatide improves macrophage polarization, reduces cytokine signaling, and promotes extracellular matrix turnover, thereby counteracting the chronic inflammatory and fibrotic microenvironment characteristic of lipedema adipose tissue. The mechanistic overlap between lipedema and gynecological conditions such as endometriosis and adenomyosis through progesterone resistance further supports the potential for tirzepatide to address shared underlying pathological mechanisms in these interconnected diseases.

title: Evaluation of the long-term efficacy and safety of dydrogesterone in the treatment of endometriosis/dysmenorrhea
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic disease closely associated with dysmenorrhea, infertility, and chronic pelvic pain that significantly impairs quality of life and sexual function. This study evaluated dydrogesterone, a progestin structurally similar to natural progesterone, as a long-term treatment option. Dydrogesterone effectively reduced endometriotic lesion volumes (from 65.73 to 24.83 cm³), ovarian endometriomas, and adenomyoma lesions while providing symptomatic relief in over 80% of patients after two treatment cycles, with visual analog pain scores decreasing significantly from 4.83 to lower levels. Beyond lesion reduction, dydrogesterone improved multiple quality-of-life dimensions including physical functioning, body pain, social functioning, and general health, while also reducing menstrual volume and improving hemoglobin levels and menstrual cycle regularity. A key advantage over oral contraceptives is that dydrogesterone preserves ovulation, does not affect bone metabolism, and carries no thrombosis risk, making it suitable for women seeking pregnancy. Although minor adverse effects such as breast pain and irregular bleeding occurred in some patients, the study demonstrates dydrogesterone as an effective and safe long-term pharmacological management option for endometriosis-associated dysmenorrhea.

title: Medical cannabis in Israel: a comprehensive review of trends and regulations, 2011–2025
summary:
# Summary

This review examines medical cannabis (MC) trends and regulatory oversight in Israel from 2011 to 2025, documenting a substantial expansion in therapeutic applications and patient licensing. Chronic non-cancer pain emerged as the most common approved indication, increasing from 53% to 63% of licenses, while post-traumatic stress disorder showed the second-highest growth at approximately 89%, expanding from 9% to 17% of approvals.
