The post hoc analysis confirms that E4/DRSP represents an effective and safer pharmacological option for managing adenomyosis in women seeking symptom relief with reduced thrombotic risk.

title: A retrospective audit of the real‐world safety and effectiveness profile of eptinezumab for treatment‐resistant chronic migraine in Australia
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective audit demonstrates that eptinezumab, a monoclonal antibody targeting calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP), is effective for treating treatment-resistant chronic migraine in Australian patients. CGRP plays a critical role in migraine pathophysiology by becoming elevated during migraine attacks, and eptinezumab binds directly to this neuropeptide to prevent its activity. Following three-monthly intravenous infusions, patients experienced significant reductions in monthly migraine days (from 23.0 to 15.4 days at three months, declining further to 11.0 days at six months), monthly headache days, and acute analgesic medication use, with improvements observed even in patients who had previously failed other migraine prophylactics including onabotulinumtoxinA, fremanezumab, and galcanezumab. Notably, eptinezumab remained safe and effective in the substantial subset of patients with concurrent medication-overuse headache (52% of cohort), and may convert chronic migraine into an episodic phenotype while improving overall quality of life. These findings support eptinezumab as a valuable treatment option for chronic migraine when conventional oral preventives and other biologic agents have proven unsuccessful.

title: Venous Thromboembolism Risk Associated With Relugolix-estradiol-norethisterone Acetate Combination Therapy
summary:
# Summary

Relugolix, an oral GnRH receptor antagonist, effectively treats endometriosis and uterine fibroids by suppressing the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and reducing estrogen production to infra-physiological levels, which produces neutral hemostatic effects and lower thrombotic risk compared to traditional combined oral contraceptives. When combined with add-back therapy using low-dose estradiol (E2) and norethisterone acetate (NETA)—formulated as relugolix-CT—the combination achieves systemic E2 levels mimicking the early follicular phase while maintaining bone health and improving quality of life by mitigating hypoestrogenic side effects. Although estroprogestin combinations generally carry prothrombotic risk due to E2's role in increasing procoagulant factors and reducing fibrinolysis, E2-based regimens and NETA-containing therapies demonstrate significantly lower venous thromboembolism (VTE) risk than ethinylestradiol-based alternatives, with relugolix-CT showing a favorable thrombotic profile in clinical trials. However, relugolix-CT remains contraindicated in women with prior thrombotic events, known thrombophilia, or multiple VTE risk factors including age over 35 and smoking, reflecting the need for careful patient selection despite the overall safety advantage of this formulation in reproductive-age women without such contraindications.

title: Effects of silybin on triptorelin-induced bone metabolic abnormalities in prostate cancer revealed based on TMT-based proteomics
summary: This study demonstrates that silybin, a natural compound from milk thistle seeds, effectively mitigates triptorelin-induced bone metabolic abnormalities through modulation of the IL-17 signaling pathway. Using proteomics analysis in prostate cancer cells (LNCaP), silybin and triptorelin both independently inhibited cellular proliferation, migration, and invasion, with their combination producing synergistic effects. The mechanism of silybin's protective action against triptorelin-induced bone loss involves regulation of five key differentially expressed proteins: p-ERK2, RELA, HSP90B1, GNAI1, and GNAI3. Silybin's antioxidant and anticarcinogenic properties enable it to suppress osteoclastogenesis by attenuating RANKL and TNF-α signaling cascades, thereby protecting bone homeostasis. These findings suggest that silybin represents a promising therapeutic adjunct to triptorelin treatment, potentially reducing the significant side effects on bone metabolism that currently limit long-term use of this GnRH agonist in cancer and endometriosis management.

title: Approved and Pipeline Pharmacological Interventions for Eating Disorders (2010–2025): 15 Years of Progress (or Lack Thereof)
summary:
# Summary

This review examines pharmacological interventions for eating disorders from 2010–2025, revealing limited FDA-approved options despite 15 years of research. Only fluoxetine for bulimia nervosa and lisdexamfetamine for binge eating disorder have gained FDA approval, highlighting a significant treatment gap. Several investigational agents show promise through distinct mechanisms: ketamine improves eating disorder symptoms by increasing glutamate release and enhancing synaptic plasticity and neurogenesis, while d-cycloserine facilitates fear extinction and emotional learning through NMDA receptor activation. Emerging agents like psilocybin activate serotonin 5-HT2A receptors and show efficacy for comorbid depression and anxiety, whereas olanzapine modulates dopamine and serotonin signaling but carries substantial adverse effects including extrapyramidal symptoms and weight gain. Metabolic-focused interventions such as metreleptin targeting leptin signaling and relamorelin reducing gastric emptying represent alternative approaches to appetite regulation and energy balance. Despite ongoing investigation, the field has achieved minimal regulatory progress, underscoring the need for more effective, well-tolerated pharmacological treatments for eating disorders.

title: A randomized controlled trial comparing combination of letrozole and clomiphene citrate or letrozole alone for ovarian stimulation in infertile women
journal: Journal of Family Medicine and Primary Care
summary: This randomized controlled trial compared letrozole monotherapy to combination letrozole-clomiphene citrate for ovarian stimulation in infertile women, evaluating both efficacy and safety outcomes. Both treatment regimens were equally effective at inducing ovulation and treating female infertility, with letrozole alone achieving superior outcomes by preserving endometrial thickness compared to the combination approach, which causes endometrial thinning—a known adverse effect of clomiphene citrate. The combination therapy promoted excessive follicular rupture and resulted in higher rates of adverse events including hot flushes, nausea, and ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome, whereas letrozole monotherapy demonstrated fewer side effects and no cases of hyperstimulation. These findings suggest that letrozole alone represents a safer and more physiologically favorable approach for ovarian stimulation, particularly given the critical role of adequate endometrial thickness in embryo implantation and the reduced endocrine burden of avoiding clomiphene citrate's estrogen receptor antagonism.

title: Danazol for unexplained subfertility
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary: Danazol is a synthetic androgen originally developed in the 1970s to treat endometriosis and was subsequently tested for managing unexplained subfertility in women. However, randomized controlled trials showed no statistically significant improvement in live birth rates or clinical pregnancy rates when danazol was compared to placebo, with clinical pregnancies showing only borderline, non-significant effects. Despite its established efficacy in treating endometriosis itself, danazol's use for subfertility was limited by the emergence of multiple adverse effects associated with treatment. This evidence suggests that while danazol remains a therapeutic option for endometriosis, its application for unexplained subfertility lacks adequate efficacy to justify routine clinical use given the burden of side effects.

title: Recent Developments and Future Perspectives in Magnetic Resonance Imaging and Computed Tomography Contrast Media
summary: This review examines recent advances in contrast media for radiological imaging, though the extracted relationships focus primarily on safety profiles and technological innovations rather than endometriosis management. Iodinated contrast media remain widely used but carry risks including hypersensitivity reactions, thyroid dysfunction, and contrast-induced acute kidney injury, with fluid replacement proven effective for prevention in at-risk patients. Gadolinium-based contrast agents (GBCAs) have evolved significantly, with macrocyclic formulations demonstrating lower gadolinium retention and reduced nephrogenic systemic fibrosis risk compared to older linear agents, while newer agents like gadopiclenol show improved T1-relaxivity enabling dose reduction in CNS imaging. Emerging technologies including photon-counting CT and spectral CT allow substantial reductions in contrast media dosing while maintaining diagnostic quality. Contrast-enhanced mammography has gained regulatory approval for multiple iodinated agents and offers faster, less expensive breast cancer detection compared to MRI with improved lesion characterization. The article emphasizes balancing diagnostic efficacy with safety considerations, particularly for vulnerable populations including neonates, children, and patients with renal impairment.

title: Targeting angiogenesis in endometriosis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis of VEGF-directed pharmacotherapies
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and network meta-analysis identifies VEGF-driven angiogenesis as central to endometriosis pathogenesis, where aberrant neovascularization promotes lesion formation, innervation, and pain generation. Among multiple pharmacological approaches targeting VEGF signaling and related pathways, curcumin emerged as the most effective agent, achieving the greatest reduction in lesion size and microvascular density through broad engagement of key signaling nodes including MAPK1/3, AKT1, and apoptotic caspases. Additional efficacious therapies include GnRH antagonists (cetrorelix and ganirelix), anti-VEGF antibodies (bevacizumab), multi-kinase inhibitors (sorafenib), mTOR-pathway modulators (rapamycin and temsirolimus), and dopamine agonists, all converging on shared targets within a 16-gene regulatory hub that controls angiogenesis and cell proliferation. Network analysis revealed that the most potent drugs—curcumin, bentamapimod, and sorafenib—share common molecular targets in MAPK and PI3K-Akt/mTOR signaling cascades, suggesting that combined pathway inhibition may enhance therapeutic efficacy. Despite curcumin's superior preclinical efficacy, its poor oral bioavailability remains a clinical limitation, highlighting the need for improved formulations or complementary agents such as tacrolimus to expand pathway coverage.

title: How We Can Optimize Dysmenorrhea Treatment: Real-World Results from a Cross-Sectional, Multi-Center Study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic, hormone-dependent condition strongly associated with dysmenorrhea and chronic pelvic pain, representing a significant clinical challenge requiring optimized treatment strategies. This cross-sectional, multi-center study demonstrates that endocrine therapies—including combined oral contraceptives, progestin-only preparations, and dienogest—effectively reduce dysmenorrhea symptoms and improve quality of life, with continuous hormone use providing greater pain reduction than cyclical regimens. However, the study reveals substantial patient refusal of endocrine therapy (40.9% cite side effects, 17.0% cite thrombosis concerns), with particular hesitancy among women with ovarian endometriomas and those experiencing migraine with aura due to increased stroke risk with combined oral contraceptives. Notably, patients on pain medication alone report higher pain severity than those on endocrine therapy, suggesting suboptimal symptom control with analgesics alone. The findings indicate that optimizing dysmenorrhea management requires careful patient counseling regarding hormone therapy benefits and risks, individualized treatment selection based on contraindications such as migraine with aura, and consideration of progestin-only options for high-risk patients, as these effectively suppress endometriosis-related pain without significantly increasing thromboembolism risk.

title: Gut microbiota in dysmenorrhea: causal evidence from Mendelian randomization and microbial-targeted intervention validation
summary:
# Summary

This study establishes a causal relationship between dysmenorrhea and gut microbiota dysbiosis using Mendelian randomization and animal model validation. Dysmenorrhea drives alterations in specific bacterial taxa, particularly increased Erysipelotrichaceae and Mollicutes RF9 alongside decreased butyrate-producing Lachnospiraceae members (Eubacterium xylanophilum group, Lachnospiraceae NK4A136 group, and Roseburia), which normally protect against inflammation and pain through short-chain fatty acid production. The study demonstrates that both ibuprofen and the traditional Chinese medicine Wenjing Zhitong Decoction alleviate dysmenorrhea symptoms in a prostaglandin-dependent manner, but they achieve this through distinct microbial signatures—ibuprofen enriches Staphylococcus while Wenjing Zhitong Decoction restores beneficial Bifidobacterium and Blautia populations associated with pain reduction. These findings suggest that dysmenorrhea pathogenesis involves a gut-brain axis mechanism mediated by inflammatory cytokines (IL-6 and TNF-α), and that microbiota-targeted interventions may offer complementary therapeutic approaches alongside conventional NSAIDs and hormonal contraceptives.

title: Aquagenic palmar keratoderma associated with palmoplantar hyperhidrosis: a case report
summary: This case report describes aquagenic palmar keratoderma (APPK), a rare skin condition characterized by water-induced palmoplantar hyperkeratosis associated with palmoplantar hyperhidrosis in a 31-year-old female patient. APPK is strongly associated with cystic fibrosis and CFTR mutations, though most cases remain idiopathic, and can be triggered or exacerbated by COX-2 inhibitors and aspirin, which reduce prostaglandin synthesis and lead to increased sodium retention and abnormal water transport in epidermal cells through altered aquaporin expression. Multiple treatment modalities were evaluated, with topical aluminum hydroxide and aluminum chloride serving as primary therapeutic agents, while keratolytic preparations including salicylic acid and urea, along with botulinum toxin injections, demonstrated efficacy in managing symptoms. The patient's medical history also included endometriosis managed with oral contraceptives (norethindrone and estradiol) and migraines treated with ibuprofen, highlighting the complex clinical presentation. APPK can be associated with serious systemic complications including nephrotic syndrome, cardiac anomalies, and marasmus, underscoring the importance of comprehensive clinical assessment. The case illustrates that drug-induced APPK from agents like celecoxib and rofecoxib requires careful medication review and may be reversible with discontinuation and appropriate topical management.

title: Efficacy and acceptability of dienogest among patients with endometriosis in Thailand
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue that triggers chronic inflammation, neovascularization, and fibrosis, resulting in diverse clinical manifestations including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and impaired fertility. Dienogest, a fourth-generation progestin with high selectivity for progesterone receptors, demonstrated significant efficacy in reducing endometriosis-associated pain through multiple mechanisms including inhibition of gonadotropin secretion, anti-inflammatory effects, and activation of apoptotic pathways. In this Thai population study, dienogest 2 mg daily reduced overall pain scores from 7.5 to 2.8 within 12 weeks and improved dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and sexual function, with efficacy comparable to GnRH agonists and superior to combined oral contraceptives. The most common adverse effects were acne (29%), depressed mood (16.1%), and breast pain (14.5%), while amenorrhea occurred in 45.1% of participants. Overall, dienogest proved to be a well-tolerated, effective first-line hormonal therapy for endometriosis management with substantial improvements in pain symptoms and quality of life.

title: A Longitudinal Assessment of Endometriosis Patients Prescribed Cannabis‐Based Medicinal Products: A Case Series From the UK Medical Cannabis Registry
journal: The Australian & New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics & Gynaecology
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth, causes chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, abdominal cramps, and infertility, substantially impairing quality of life. Cannabis-based medicinal products (CBMPs) improve endometriosis-associated pain through multiple mechanisms: cannabidiol (CBD) reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) and endometriotic implant surface area while inhibiting TRPV1 signaling and anandamide breakdown, while delta-9-THC modulates neurotransmitter release via CB1 receptor agonism. In this UK Medical Cannabis Registry case series, CBMP treatment yielded significant improvements across multiple pain measures (Visual Analogue Scale, Brief Pain Inventory, McGill Pain Score), health-related quality of life, anxiety, and sleep quality over 18 months of follow-up. However, improvements in affective pain dimensions plateaued by 18 months, potentially reflecting pharmacological tolerance, placebo response, or expectancy bias, which may be heightened by CBMPs' distinct sensory properties and positive media coverage. These findings suggest CBMPs offer symptomatic benefit for endometriosis-associated chronic pain, though long-term efficacy and the contribution of non-pharmacological factors warrant further investigation in rigorous controlled trials.

title: A Scoping Systematic Review of Cannabis Use in Endometriosis
journal: The Australian & New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics & Gynaecology
summary: Endometriosis, affecting 6–10% of reproductive-age women, causes chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, infertility, and associated psychiatric complications including depression and anxiety. The underlying mechanisms involve retrograde menstruation and neuroangiogenesis, which drive chronic inflammation and pain pathway activation. While standard treatments include surgical interventions and hormonal therapies (combined oral contraceptives, progestogens, and GnRH analogues), emerging evidence suggests cannabis may offer symptom relief through the endocannabinoid system, which modulates pain and inflammatory activity via CB1 and CB2 receptors. Observational studies report that cannabis, particularly CBD-THC combinations administered topically or orally, reduces endometriosis-associated pain, cramping, and muscle spasms, with many patients able to reduce or discontinue opioids, NSAIDs, and psychiatric medications. However, cannabis carries adverse effects including euphoria, dry mouth, and central nervous system symptoms such as memory problems and confusion, with efficacy influenced by cannabinoid ratios, age, and cannabis legality. The review highlights the need for rigorous clinical trials to establish safety and efficacy given current evidence derives largely from observational studies using illicitly sourced cannabis lacking standardization.

title: Natural products as HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors (statins) for the management of non-communicable diseases
summary:
# Summary

Statins are HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors that reduce LDL cholesterol and treat multiple cardiovascular and metabolic disorders through diverse molecular mechanisms. Beyond their primary lipid-lowering effects, statins inhibit pro-inflammatory pathways by blocking NF-κB signaling and suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines like TNF-α and IL-6, while simultaneously upregulating protective mechanisms such as endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS) to promote vasodilation and reduce oxidative stress. Statins stabilize ion channels dysregulated in arrhythmias, inhibit Rho and Rac GTPases to suppress NADPH oxidase-mediated inflammation, and block profibrotic signaling through TGF-β inhibition and matrix metalloproteinase suppression. In kidney disease, specific statins including simvastatin, pravastatin, and atorvastatin demonstrate renoprotective effects by reducing macrophage infiltration, proteinuria, and fibrosis while preventing lipotoxicity-driven renal damage. These multi-target mechanisms establish statins as cornerstones of therapy for cardiovascular diseases, atherosclerosis, and complications of dyslipidemia and chronic kidney disease.

title: Progestogen use and the risk of intracranial meningioma: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that progesterone plays a critical role in meningioma pathophysiology, as meningioma cells express progesterone receptors in 38–88% of cases, and certain progestogens used for contraception and endometriosis management are associated with substantially elevated intracranial meningioma risk. Cyproterone acetate shows the strongest association with meningioma development, with a pooled odds ratio of 12.36 and a clear dose-response relationship, particularly at cumulative doses exceeding 60 grams, while depot medroxyprogesterone acetate demonstrates a more modest but significant increase in risk (pooled OR 2.68) that correlates with exposure duration. Other progestogens including chlormadinone acetate, nomegestrol acetate, and promegestone show intermediate signals of increased risk, whereas progesterone itself, levonorgestrel, norgestrel, dydrogesterone, and spironolactone do not demonstrate significant meningioma associations. Notably, discontinuation of high-risk progestogens, particularly cyproterone acetate and nomegestrol acetate, results in meningioma regression in reported cases, suggesting the tumors are hormone-dependent. These findings highlight the critical need for careful risk-benefit assessment when prescribing high-dose macroprogestogens for endometriosis and contraception, particularly given that meningiomas present with serious neurological manifestations including seizures, focal neurological deficits, and cognitive disturbances.

title: Comparison of Substances in Combined Oral Contraceptives Used in Acne Vulgaris, Hirsutism, Migraine, and Dysmenorrhea
journal: Medical Science Monitor: International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
summary: Combined oral contraceptives (COCs) represent an effective pharmacological approach for managing multiple menstrual-related conditions through their dual mechanisms of inhibiting ovulation and suppressing androgen synthesis. The estrogen component (typically ethinylestradiol) and progestogens work synergistically to reduce prostaglandin production by the endometrium, thereby alleviating dysmenorrhea, while simultaneously lowering androgen levels to improve acne vulgaris and hirsutism. Among available progestogens, those with stronger anti-androgenic properties—particularly drospirenone, chlormadinone acetate, and dienogest—demonstrate superior efficacy compared to levonorgestrel for acne and hirsutism management. For dysmenorrhea specifically, extended or continuous regimens of ethinylestradiol/drospirenone show enhanced benefit, and drospirenone-containing formulations are preferred due to their analgesic properties. COCs also improve menstrual migraine without aura, though they are contraindicated in migraine with aura due to increased stroke risk. Importantly, combination therapy with metformin enhances outcomes in hyperandrogenic conditions like polycystic ovary syndrome by further reducing insulin resistance and inflammatory markers.

title: The Effect of TISSEEL® on the Healing Process of Uterine Horn Reanastomosis in an Experimental Animal Model
summary:
# Summary

This experimental study evaluated TISSEEL® fibrin sealant as an adjunct to microsurgical suturing in rat uterine horn reanastomosis, a procedure relevant to restoring fertility after tubal ligation. TISSEEL® significantly enhanced neovascularization and collagen deposition at the anastomosis site while substantially reducing severe adhesion formation compared to suture alone, with 77.8–80.0% of TISSEEL® groups showing high-grade neovascularization versus only 11.1% minimal adhesions in the TISSEEL® group compared to 66.7% severe adhesions in suture-only controls. Importantly, TISSEEL® promoted these beneficial healing responses without inducing excessive inflammation or fibrosis, with the combined TISSEEL® plus suture approach producing the most pronounced neovascularization profile. Since improved microvascularization is critical for maintaining tubal viability and supporting ovum fertilization and embryo transport, these findings suggest that TISSEEL® fibrin sealant may be a valuable adjunct in tubal reconstructive surgery to enhance anastomotic healing quality and reduce postoperative adhesions that threaten fertility outcomes.

title: ABSTRACTS FOR E-POSTERS
summary:
# Summary

This collection of clinical case reports examines the psychiatric and neurological manifestations of various medical conditions and their pharmacological management. The cases demonstrate that systemic and neurological diseases frequently present with psychiatric symptoms: pituitary insufficiency from postpartum hemorrhage causes endocrine failure and psychosis treatable with hormone replacement therapy, temporal lobe epilepsy causes memory disturbance and hallucinations, and right frontal lobe infarcts induce apathy and mood changes. Multiple medications show efficacy in treating psychiatric symptoms across conditions, including pimavanserin as a 5-HT2A inverse agonist for psychosis and extrapyramidal symptoms, risperidone for hallucinations in Charles Bonnet syndrome, and combined olanzapine and fluoxetine for anorexia nervosa. The cases also highlight iatrogenic psychiatric complications: fluorouracil chemotherapy induces acute psychosis managed with haloperidol, levodopa use in Parkinson's disease associates with dopamine dysregulation syndrome presenting as hypomania and psychosis, and mirtazapine can trigger hypersensitivity reactions. These reports underscore the critical importance of recognizing medical and iatrogenic etiologies in psychiatric presentations to enable targeted treatment and improve patient outcomes.

title: Risk of Dementia in Patients With Type 2 Diabetes Using SGLT2 Inhibitors Versus DPP‐4 Inhibitors: A Systematic Review and Meta‐Analysis
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 (SGLT2) inhibitors significantly reduce dementia risk in type 2 diabetes patients compared to dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitors, with hazard ratios of 0.74 for all-cause dementia, 0.62 for Alzheimer's disease, and 0.54 for vascular dementia. The neuroprotective mechanisms underlying SGLT2 inhibitor efficacy are multifaceted, including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, induction of mild ketosis with elevation of neuroprotective β-hydroxybutyrate, stabilization of the blood-brain barrier, and modulation of amyloid processing. Within the SGLT2 inhibitor class, dapagliflozin and empagliflozin demonstrated significant dementia risk reduction, whereas canagliflozin showed no significant benefit, likely due to lower selectivity for SGLT2 over SGLT1. In contrast, DPP-4 inhibitors, which primarily enhance GLP-1 signaling, showed neutral effects on cognitive outcomes despite some preclinical evidence for potential neuroprotective effects. The bidirectional association between type 2 diabetes and dementia is mediated by shared pathophysiological mechanisms including insulin resistance, oxidative stress, chronic inflammation, and microvascular dysfunction, all of which SGLT2 inhibitors appear to attenuate through their cardiovascular and metabolic benefits. This evidence suggests SGLT2 inhibitors should be considered as preferred agents for dementia risk reduction in diabetic patients.

title: The letrozole use in reproductive medicine: Beyond aromatase inhibition - a comprehensive review
journal: Turkish Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary: Letrozole is a third-generation aromatase inhibitor that reversibly blocks the cytochrome P450 enzyme responsible for converting androgens to estrogens, thereby reducing peripheral estrogen levels and promoting physiologic rather than supraphysiologic hormonal exposure. By lowering estrogen through selective aromatase inhibition, letrozole enhances endogenous follicle-stimulating hormone secretion, supporting controlled mono- or bi-follicular development and reducing gonadotropin requirements compared to traditional stimulation protocols. Beyond its established role as a potent ovulation inducer particularly effective in polycystic ovary syndrome, letrozole demonstrates therapeutic benefits in managing endometriosis-related pain and infertility through inhibition of estrogen-dependent pathways, and its estrogen-suppressive capacity significantly reduces the risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome. The drug has expanded applications across assisted reproductive technologies, including in vitro fertilization, in vitro maturation, endometrial preparation, and fertility preservation, making it increasingly valuable in complex reproductive scenarios. These characteristics position letrozole as a versatile pharmacological tool that maintains physiologic hormonal conditions while providing superior safety and efficacy profiles compared to conventional approaches in reproductive medicine.

title: Exploring effectiveness of Metronidazole, Bismuth, and Rifaximin in treating small intestinal bacterial overgrowth and irritable bowel syndrome: A systematic review
summary: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO) causes gastrointestinal symptoms including malabsorption, bloating, diarrhea, and abdominal pain through bacterial fermentation of carbohydrates, and shows substantial clinical overlap with irritable bowel syndrome with diarrhea (IBS-D) through shared pathogenic mechanisms including dysbiosis and impaired intestinal motility. Three antibiotics—metronidazole, bismuth subsalicylate, and rifaximin—demonstrate varying efficacy in treating these conditions: metronidazole inhibits bacterial DNA synthesis and provides symptom relief in approximately 70% of SIBO cases but is limited by high rates of gastrointestinal side effects including nausea and vomiting, plus emerging resistance. Rifaximin emerged as the most effective agent, demonstrating superior efficacy across both IBS-D and SIBO with a favorable safety profile due to its gut-selective, nonsystemic activity and minimal absorption. Bismuth subsalicylate offers antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties and enhances antibiotic response when combined with metronidazole, providing notable symptom relief for bloating and diarrhea though with generally lower overall effectiveness. Across all three agents, adverse events were comparable in frequency (16–17%), though metronidazole demonstrated the highest burden of gastrointestinal side effects, supporting rifaximin as the preferred therapeutic option for SIBO and IBS-D management.

title: Characteristics of Menstrual Cycles, Prevalence of Menstrual Disorders in Young Women and Their Relationship to Hormonal Contraceptive Use
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that hormonal contraceptives significantly improve menstrual cycle characteristics in young women by reducing irregular cycles (adjusted odds ratio 0.28), infrequent bleeding (aOR 0.18), and excessive bleeding volume (aOR 0.47) while promoting shorter, more regular cycles. Among 404 young women surveyed, menstrual abnormalities were highly prevalent, with over 50% experiencing irregularity and heavy menstrual bleeding, 25% reporting irregular cycles, and 28% experiencing excessive bleeding. The research identified multiple factors associated with menstrual disturbances including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, high body mass index, eating disorders, and strenuous exercise, while establishing that progestogen-only contraceptives are more likely to cause menstrual disorders compared to combined hormonal formulations. Excessive menstrual bleeding was linked to anemia in 11.9% of participants, and menstrual disorders substantially reduced quality of life, underscoring the clinical importance of hormonal contraceptives as an effective pharmacological management strategy for menstrual dysfunction in this population.

title: Cryoglobulinemia Type II: Sustained Remission After B-Cell–Directed Therapy
journal: The American Journal of Case Reports
summary: Type II cryoglobulinemia is an immune-mediated vasculitis caused by monoclonal immunoglobulin produced by clonal B cells, leading to cryoglobulin precipitation in blood vessels and multi-organ damage including vasculitis, purpura, glomerulonephritis, and peripheral neuropathy. B-cell directed therapy using rituximab (anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody) combined with either fludarabine or bendamustine successfully induced sustained clinical remission in two reported cases, with rituximab binding to CD20 and triggering complement-mediated B-cell depletion. These combination therapies rapidly reduced skin manifestations, monoclonal immunoglobulin levels, and clonal B-cell populations while achieving complete hematological remission. Standard rituximab monotherapy or combination with glucocorticosteroids achieves clinical remission in approximately two-thirds of patients; however, combining rituximab with fludarabine or bendamustine substantially improves response rates by directly targeting the underlying pathogenic B-cell clone. Although rituximab carries risks of delayed neutropenia and hypogammaglobulinemia, B-cell directed combination therapy represents the preferred modern approach for non-infectious type II cryoglobulinemia when conventional immunosuppression fails.

title: Acute recurrent appendicitis in a hemophiliac Nepalese girl: beyond just… avoidance of formidable cuts! – A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes the surgical management of acute recurrent appendicitis in a Nepalese girl with Hemophilia A, a hereditary bleeding disorder caused by Factor VIII deficiency that results in prolonged bleeding and easy bruising. The patient underwent emergency laparoscopic appendectomy with careful perioperative management to minimize hemorrhagic complications, including preoperative Factor VIII transfusion (500 IU) and intraoperative hemostatic techniques using bipolar vessel sealer and hook electrocautery. The surgical approach highlights critical considerations for hemophilic patients undergoing emergency procedures, such as avoiding muscle relaxants like succinylcholine that increase muscle and joint hemorrhage risk, eschewing intramuscular injections, and utilizing minimal-access laparoscopic techniques that reduce overall bleeding. Adjunctive pharmacological management included the use of antifibrinolytic agents like tranexamic acid and desmopressin to upregulate Factor VIII levels, while postoperative analgesia avoided NSAIDs that impair platelet function. The report emphasizes that in female hemophiliacs presenting with acute abdominal pain, early evaluation for gynecological conditions such as endometriosis and Mittelschmerz-related pain is essential to distinguish these mimics of appendicitis, ultimately improving diagnostic accuracy and treatment outcomes.

title: Research Status of Androgen Deprivation Therapy Monotherapy and Combination Therapy for Prostate Cancer: A Scoping Review
summary:
# Summary

Androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) represents the cornerstone of prostate cancer treatment, utilizing multiple drug classes including GnRH agonists (leuprorelin, goserelin, triptorelin), GnRH antagonists (degarelix), and anti-androgen agents (bicalutamide, flutamide) that effectively treat localized, metastatic hormone-sensitive, and castration-resistant disease. Leuprorelin has emerged as the most extensively studied and widely used ADT agent across randomized controlled trials, available in multiple dosage formulations and frequently employed as a control drug in comparative studies. Contemporary treatment approaches increasingly combine traditional ADT with novel androgen receptor pathway inhibitors—including abiraterone, enzalutamide, apalutamide, and darolutamide—which consistently demonstrate prolonged overall survival and radiographic progression-free survival in metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer and castration-resistant disease. While triptorelin and degarelix remain comparatively understudied in combination therapy protocols, both agents show promise as alternatives to first-generation drugs. Emerging evidence supports combining ADT with aerobic and resistance exercise to enhance quality of life during treatment, and ADT continues to serve as adjuvant therapy for prostate cancer with regional lymph node metastasis and in combination with external beam radiation therapy. These findings collectively establish ADT monotherapy and combination therapy as evidence-based approaches that substantially improve clinical outcomes across multiple prostate cancer disease states.

title: Progestins - a review of clinical application in gynecology
summary:
# Summary

Progestins represent a diverse class of synthetic progesterone compounds with broad therapeutic applications beyond contraception, particularly in managing gynecological conditions including endometriosis, abnormal uterine bleeding, endometrial hyperplasia, and secondary amenorrhea. Natural progesterone, produced by the adrenal cortex, ovaries, corpus luteum, and placenta, exerts its effects through multiple mechanisms including suppression of inflammatory reactions, reduction of uterine contractility, and regulation of endometrial proliferation and vascularization. Specific progestin formulations demonstrate distinct clinical advantages: medroxyprogesterone acetate effectively treats endometrial hyperplasia and prevents ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome, norethindrone manages endometriosis-related dysmenorrhea, and relugolix combination therapy significantly reduces menstrual blood loss in both endometriosis and uterine fibroids while improving quality of life. At the molecular level, progestogens counteract estrogen-driven endometrial proliferation by decreasing estrogen receptor expression and suppressing luteinizing hormone, thereby preventing endometrial cancer—a critical protective mechanism in women receiving hormone therapy. These findings underscore progestins' essential role in treating estrogen-dependent gynecological pathologies through coordinated effects on immune regulation, endometrial remodeling, and reproductive hormone signaling.

title: The therapeutic pipeline in migraine: What does the future hold?
summary:
# Summary

This article reviews emerging therapeutic targets in migraine pathophysiology, highlighting multiple ion channel-based mechanisms beyond traditional triptan therapy. Established treatments like sumatriptan target serotonin 5-HT1B/1D receptors, while newer monoclonal antibodies such as erenumab block the calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) receptor, which plays a central role in migraine pathogenesis. The article emphasizes potassium and calcium-activated potassium channels (KATP and BKCa) as critical mediators of migraine, as their synthetic openers trigger headache and vasodilation in migraine-relevant structures including the trigeminovascular system and meningeal arteries, while channel blockers like glibenclamide reduce CGRP release and migraine-related hypersensitivity. Additionally, TRPM8, a temperature-sensing ion channel associated with migraine susceptibility through genetic variants, represents another promising therapeutic target, with TRPM8 antagonists preventing migraine-like pain and modulating CGRP release in preclinical models. These findings collectively suggest that ion channel dysfunction underlies migraine mechanisms, offering multiple pharmacological avenues for future therapeutic development beyond current standard-of-care approaches.

title: Cardiotoxicity associated with anticancer therapies for gynecological tumors
summary:
# Summary

Anticancer therapies used for gynecological tumors, while effective at treating ovarian, endometrial, and cervical cancers, carry significant cardiotoxic risks through diverse mechanisms. Anthracycline agents (doxorubicin, epirubicin) cause tumor cell apoptosis but induce permanent heart muscle damage, with risk escalating at higher doses or with concurrent radiation, though pegylated liposomal formulations reduce this toxicity. Platinum-based agents (cisplatin, carboplatin) and alkylating agents (cyclophosphamide) damage vascular endothelium and activate coagulation pathways, leading to thromboembolism, atherosclerosis, myocarditis, and arrhythmias. Additional chemotherapeutic classes including 5-fluorouracil, taxanes, and gemcitabine cause myocardial ischemia, coronary artery spasms, and acute coronary syndrome. Targeted therapies such as bevacizumab, pazopanib, lenvatinib, and PARP inhibitors predominantly cause hypertension and heart failure through VEGF pathway inhibition or DNA repair blockade, while anti-HER2 therapy (trastuzumab) causes left ventricular dysfunction. Notably, even subthreshold doses of some agents may cause subclinical cardiac injury manifesting years after treatment, necessitating careful cardiotoxicity monitoring throughout and after gynecological cancer therapy.

title: Safety and efficacy of apixaban versus enoxaparin in gynecologic cancer patients undergoing open surgery: an open-label randomized controlled trial
summary: This randomized controlled trial evaluated postoperative venous thromboembolism (VTE) prophylaxis in gynecologic cancer patients undergoing open surgery, comparing the oral factor Xa inhibitor apixaban with the standard subcutaneous enoxaparin. Gynecologic cancer patients, particularly those with ovarian cancer, face substantially elevated VTE risk following open surgery, necessitating effective anticoagulation strategies. Apixaban demonstrated superior safety compared to enoxaparin, with major bleeding events occurring in only 1.4% of the apixaban group versus 9.0% in the enoxaparin group, driven largely by gastrointestinal bleeding complications in the enoxaparin arm. Both agents achieved comparable efficacy for VTE prevention, with incidence rates of 2.0% and 3.0% respectively, supporting apixaban as a viable alternative to conventional low-molecular-weight heparin therapy. Beyond safety and efficacy, apixaban offered additional advantages including improved patient compliance, higher patient satisfaction, and lower out-of-pocket costs due to its oral formulation. These findings align with recent clinical guideline updates endorsing direct oral anticoagulants for VTE prophylaxis in cancer patients, establishing apixaban as a preferable option for postoperative thromboprophylaxis in gynecologic cancer surgery.

title: Association of GnRH agonists with depression and suicide/self-injury: a FAERS pharmacovigilance study
summary:
# Summary

This pharmacovigilance study using FDA Adverse Event Reporting System data examined associations between gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists (GnRH-as) and depression and suicide/self-injury (DASSI) outcomes across multiple hormone-sensitive conditions including endometriosis, uterine leiomyoma, breast cancer, and prostate cancer. GnRH-as work by initially activating GnRH receptors to stimulate luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone release, but continuous administration leads to feedback inhibition that markedly reduces these hormones and downstream sex steroids, thereby treating hormone-dependent diseases. The analysis revealed significant disproportionate reporting signals for DASSI associated with specific GnRH-as, particularly triptorelin and goserelin for suicide/self-injury, and leuprolide, triptorelin, histrelin, and nafarelin for depression in female patients across age groups. Importantly, DASSI symptoms emerged earlier in females, children, and adults aged 18–65 years compared to males and elderly patients, and no such associations were detected in endometriosis patients specifically. The reduced sex hormone synthesis induced by continuous GnRH-a administration likely contributes to psychiatric adverse events through disruption of hormonal regulation of mood and behavior.

title: The gap between prevalence of primary dysmenorrhea and available treatment strategies
summary:
# Summary

Primary dysmenorrhea arises from multiple interconnected physiological mechanisms centered on excessive uterine contractility and reduced blood flow during menstruation. Elevated levels of prostaglandin F2α (PGF2α) and vasopressin stimulate uterine smooth muscle contractions and cause vasoconstriction, leading to increased intrauterine pressure that restricts blood flow and causes tissue ischemia and pain. Current pharmacological management relies primarily on nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen and naproxen, which inhibit prostaglandin production through cyclooxygenase (COX) inhibition, alongside selective COX-2 inhibitors like celecoxib that provide comparable pain relief with potentially fewer side effects. Hormonal approaches including combined oral contraceptive pills and levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine devices reduce dysmenorrhea by suppressing ovulation, thinning the endometrium, and decreasing prostaglandin production. Emerging complementary strategies show promise, including vitamin D and vitamin E supplementation, herbal preparations such as Ge Gen Decoction, and plant-derived extracts like Tabebuia avellanedae that target both pain and uterine inflammation, though these require further clinical validation to establish their role in standard treatment protocols.

title: Persistent menstruation in transgender people using testosterone gender-affirming hormone therapy
summary:
# Summary

This study examines why some transgender people undergoing testosterone gender-affirming hormone therapy experience persistent menstruation despite the expected suppressive effects. Testosterone normally causes menstrual cessation through negative feedback inhibition of luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone, and it effectively treats gender dysphoria associated with menstruation. However, the administration route significantly impacts efficacy: testosterone injections achieve menstrual cessation more reliably than transdermal gels and creams, likely because injections produce higher serum testosterone levels. Body mass index also plays a crucial role, with individuals having BMI ≥30 kg/m² showing lower odds of persistent menstruation, potentially due to underlying secondary hypogonadism or concurrent polycystic ovary syndrome. When testosterone fails to achieve menstrual suppression after twelve months or when abnormal bleeding occurs, clinicians should investigate for underlying endometrial pathology including hyperplasia, fibroids, and adenomyosis, and may consider alternative interventions such as progesterone, endometrial ablation, or hysterectomy.

title: Chinese herbal formula Xuefu Zhuyu oral liquid for primary dysmenorrhea: a multicenter randomized controlled trial
summary: Primary dysmenorrhea, a condition affecting up to 15% of women severely and associated with depression, anxiety, and reduced quality of life, is driven by elevated prostaglandin secretion, particularly increased PGF2α and PGE2 levels through the cyclooxygenase pathway. While nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs remain standard treatment, their significant side effects including gastrointestinal toxicity, nephrotoxicity, cardiovascular complications, and thrombotic events necessitate alternative approaches. This multicenter randomized controlled trial evaluated Xuefu Zhuyu oral liquid, a traditional Chinese herbal formula containing Persicae semen, Carthami flos, and Rehmanniae radix, which exerts its therapeutic effects by reducing PGF2α levels while raising PGE2, and by inhibiting inflammation and oxidative stress. Xuefu Zhuyu demonstrated efficacy comparable to placebo with significantly reduced painkiller use and consumption compared to placebo, suggesting its potential as a safer alternative analgesic. The formula's active constituents, including paeoniflorin, naringin, glycyrrhizic acid, ferulic acid, and hydroxysafflor yellow A, work through inhibition of cyclooxygenase-2, reduction of inflammatory mediators and intracellular calcium, and activation of antioxidant pathways. These findings support herbal medicine as a viable treatment strategy for primary dysmenorrhea with improved safety profiles over conventional NSAIDs.

title: Medical Treatment for Endometriosis: One Size Does Not Fit All
summary: Endometriosis causes nociceptive pain through peripheral and central sensitization mechanisms driven primarily by elevated estrogen, which sensitizes nociceptors, promotes neuroinflammation, and facilitates macrophage-nerve interactions. Multiple hormonal therapies effectively treat endometriosis-associated pain, with progestogens and estrogen-progestogen combinations as first-line options, while GnRH agonists and antagonists serve as effective escalation therapies; notably, body-identical transdermal estradiol combined with progestogens or GnRH analogues reduces thrombotic risk compared to ethinyl-estradiol-containing regimens. Dienogest, a selective progestogen, significantly reduces dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and lesion dimensions, though GnRH agonists demonstrate superior efficacy for nonmenstrual pelvic pain relief. The pathophysiology involves repetitive menstruation triggering tissue remodeling and fibrosis, leading to adenomyosis and deep infiltrating lesions, while anovulation and amenorrhea counteract both endocrine and inflammatory pain mechanisms. Treatment must be individualized, as irregular bleeding—a major adverse effect of first-line medications—substantially impacts quality of life, and emerging evidence supports neuromodulatory drugs for managing neuropathic and nociplastic pain components in select patients.

title: Association of hormone therapy with spheno-orbital meningiomas: bridging evidence and unknowns
summary:
# Summary

Long-term hormone therapy, particularly progestin-based treatments used in endometriosis management, is associated with increased risk of spheno-orbital meningiomas (SOMs), especially cyproterone acetate and nomegestrol acetate. These tumors exhibit elevated progesterone receptor expression and belong to the TRAF7/PIK3CA molecular subtype, with progesterone promoting growth through receptor activation and steroid coactivators (SRC-1, SRC-2, SRC-3) that enhance transcriptional activity. Clinical evidence demonstrates that cessation of hormone therapy can result in partial tumor regression, particularly in soft tissue components, though surgical resection—particularly total resection—remains the primary definitive treatment to reduce recurrence risk. SOMs predominantly affect women and present with visual symptoms, proptosis, and orbital compression; a hybrid management approach combining hormone therapy discontinuation with surgical intervention and radiologic surveillance may benefit selected cases.

title: Single‐Dose Bioequivalence Study of Elagolix Tablets 200 mg (Test Product) and Elagolix Tablets 200 mg (Reference Product) in Healthy Non‐Pregnant, Non‐Lactating Premenopausal Female Subjects Under Fasting Conditions
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, characterized by dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and psychological complications including anxiety and depression. The disease is driven by hormonal imbalances, particularly elevated estrogen and low progesterone levels, compounded by progesterone resistance due to reduced progesterone receptor-B expression in endometriotic lesions. Elagolix, a short-acting oral gonadotropin-releasing hormone antagonist, treats endometriosis-associated pain by inhibiting gonadotropins and estradiol while providing more precise modulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis compared to long-acting alternatives. A bioequivalence study demonstrated that the test formulation (Egolix™ 200 mg) achieved comparable pharmacokinetic properties to the reference product (Orilissa™ 200 mg), with rapid absorption (median Tmax 1.2-1.5 hours), mean half-life of 2.1-2.2 hours, and excellent tolerability with only mild, resolvable adverse events. Clinical evidence from landmark trials showed Elagolix 200 mg effectively treats dyspareunia and moderate-to-severe endometriosis-related pain, with superior safety profiles and favorable pharmacokinetic properties positioning it as a pivotal therapeutic option for endometriosis management.

title: Evaluation of adverse event profiles for leuprolide and goserelin: Insights from the FDA adverse event reporting system following STROBE guidelines
summary:
# Summary

This FDA adverse event reporting analysis compared the safety profiles of leuprolide and goserelin, two GnRH agonists widely used to treat endometriosis, prostate cancer, breast cancer, and uterine fibroids. Both drugs function through the same mechanism: binding to GnRH receptors in the anterior pituitary gland, initially stimulating gonadotropin secretion before causing receptor desensitization that ultimately suppresses estrogen and testosterone production. While both medications share common adverse effects including hot flashes, sexual dysfunction, and reduced bone density, leuprolide demonstrated stronger associations with reproductive and breast disorders, including pituitary apoplexy, injection site complications, and testosterone abnormalities, whereas goserelin showed higher reporting frequencies for endocrine disorders and device-related complications. The study revealed that leuprolide was particularly linked to metastatic disease outcomes across multiple sites, while both drugs exhibited strong safety signals for increased prostate-specific antigen and testosterone elevations despite their intended hormonal suppression effects.

title: Cannabidiol and ∆9-Tetrahydrocannabinol in Endometriosis: A Literature Review on Therapeutic Applications and Mechanisms
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition characterized by chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, with estradiol driving both inflammation and pain pathways. The endocannabinoid system, mediated through CB1 and CB2 receptors, represents a promising therapeutic target, as both receptors are significantly elevated in endometriotic lesions compared to healthy tissue. Cannabidiol (CBD) and tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), the primary phytocannabinoids, exert distinct anti-endometriotic effects: THC acts as a partial agonist at CB1 and CB2 receptors to reduce hypersensitivity and inhibit cyst development, while CBD acts as an antagonist to these receptors and interacts with TRPV1/TRPV2 channels to reduce inflammation, oxidative stress, and pain. At 5 mg/kg concentration, CBD significantly reduces endometriotic lesion size and peritoneal cytokine levels (IL-6 and TNF-α) while enhancing total antioxidant status, demonstrating anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and analgesic properties. These findings suggest that cannabinoid-based therapies offer a mechanistically distinct alternative to current pharmaceutical approaches such as progestogens and NSAIDs for managing endometriosis-related symptoms.

title: Comparison of the Effect of Ketofol and Ketodex as Procedural Sedation and Analgesia for Oocyte Retrieval during In vitro Fertilisation Procedures: A Randomised Controlled Pilot Study
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled pilot study compared Ketodex (ketamine-dexmedetomidine) and Ketofol (ketamine-propofol) for procedural sedation during oocyte retrieval in IVF procedures. Ketodex demonstrated superior analgesic efficacy with significantly lower Visual Analog Scale pain scores at all post-operative time points (10 minutes through 2 hours) and achieved complete opioid-sparing sedation without requiring intraoperative fentanyl, whereas Ketofol patients required a median fentanyl dose of 30 μg. Critically, Ketodex eliminated respiratory complications, with apnea occurring in 53.3% of Ketofol patients but none in the Ketodex group, addressing a major safety concern with propofol-based sedation. The pharmacodynamic complementarity of ketamine's NMDA receptor inhibition and dexmedetomidine's α₂-adrenergic agonism produced stable hemodynamics by mutually counteracting each agent's cardiovascular effects—dexmedetomidine attenuated ketamine-induced tachycardia while ketamine prevented dexmedetomidine-related bradycardia. Despite superior sedation and analgesia outcomes with Ketodex, fertilization rates and oocyte quality remained comparable between groups, confirming that procedural sedation choice does not compromise reproductive outcomes. These findings establish Ketodex as a safer and more effective sedation regimen for oocyte retrieval with improved patient safety profiles.

title: Prevalence, predictors, and clinical relevance of drug–drug interactions in outpatient prescribing: A national cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This national cross-sectional study demonstrates that drug-drug interactions (DDIs) represent a major preventable cause of medication-related harm in outpatient prescribing, with polypharmacy and multimorbidity significantly amplifying risk. Acetylsalicylic acid (ASA) and NSAIDs emerged as dominant contributors to high-risk interactions, including the contraindicated NSAID duplication pairs naproxen-ketorolac that cause acute kidney injury and gastrointestinal perforation, and the major ASA-clopidogrel interaction associated with major hemorrhage. DDI exposure correlated with serious adverse outcomes including cardiac arrhythmias, seizures, respiratory depression, kidney injury, and liver toxicity, with psychiatry and cardiology prescriptions showing the highest DDI prevalence at over 70 percent in cardiology. Age-stratified analysis revealed progressive DDI prevalence from children (24.92%) to older adults (59.62%), where complex regimens substantially increased hospitalizations and healthcare costs. Discovery and management of DDIs can substantially reduce healthcare utilization, underscoring the clinical importance of pharmacovigilance in polypharmacy settings.

title: A detailed review of pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics of progestogens in oral contraception
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive pharmacological review elucidates how progestogens used in oral contraceptives achieve their contraceptive and therapeutic effects through multiple mechanisms of action. Progestogens potently inhibit gonadotropin secretion and ovarian function while suppressing ovulation and altering cervical mucus, endometrial receptivity, and tubal motility to enhance contraceptive efficacy. Beyond contraception, specific progestin formulations offer distinct clinical benefits: anti-androgenic progestins treat hyperandrogenism and polycystic ovarian syndrome, while anti-mineralocorticoid progestins alleviate premenstrual dysphoric disorder and blood pressure dysregulation. Structurally diverse progestogens bind to multiple receptor classes beyond the progesterone receptor, including estrogen, androgen, glucocorticoid, and mineralocorticoid receptors, conferring their varied pharmacological profiles. Bioavailability varies significantly among compounds, with levonorgestrel and cyproterone acetate achieving near-complete absorption while others like norethisterone show lower bioavailability. Contemporary formulations combining natural estrogens with specific progestins (such as estradiol valerate with dienogest or estetrol with drospirenone) demonstrate reduced thrombotic risks compared to traditional ethinyl estradiol-containing combinations, though combined oral contraceptives generally carry inherent associations with venous and arterial thromboembolism.

title: Multifocal Rosai Dorfman disease and simultaneous endometrioid ovarian cancer: a case report
summary: This case report presents a rare simultaneous diagnosis of multifocal Rosai Dorfman disease (RDD) and endometrioid ovarian cancer in a single patient. RDD is a non-Langerhans-cell histiocytosis characterized by histiocyte accumulation and emperipolesis, with S100 protein positivity on pathological examination, and approximately one-third of patients harbor mutations in the KRAS or MAP2K1 genes of the MAPK signaling pathway that drive multifocal disease. The patient's multifocal RDD involved multiple anatomical sites including lymph nodes, head and neck structures, lacrimal glands, orbital tissue, thyroid, and nasal cavity. Treatment was initiated with high-dose prednisolone followed by methotrexate, with the MEK inhibitor Cobimetinib considered as a targeted therapy option given the detected KRAS mutation and its demonstrated efficacy in inducing remission in histiocytotic disorders. The concurrent presentation with endometrioid ovarian cancer, which is typically associated with endometriosis and ARID1A mutations, highlights the complex clinical picture managed in this case.

title: The role of fomepizole in acetaminophen-related poisoning: a narrative review
summary: This narrative review examines fomepizole as an adjunctive treatment for acetaminophen (APAP) poisoning by elucidating its hepatoprotective mechanisms. Fomepizole protects the liver through dual inhibition of cytochrome P450 2E1 (CYP2E1) and c-Jun N-terminal kinase (JNK) activation, thereby reducing the formation of N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI), the toxic metabolite responsible for acetaminophen-induced hepatotoxicity. At toxic APAP doses exceeding 4 g/day in adults, normal metabolic pathways become saturated, forcing excessive CYP2E1-mediated metabolism that generates NAPQI and depletes hepatic glutathione, triggering oxidative liver damage. When fomepizole was administered alongside the standard antidote N-acetylcysteine (NAC), patients demonstrated significantly lower peak aspartate aminotransferase levels, reduced need for liver transplantation, and lower near-term mortality compared to NAC alone. Early initiation of fomepizole within 4–8 hours of overdose showed no serious adverse events in the reviewed case series and resulted in favorable laboratory and clinical outcomes. These findings suggest fomepizole represents a promising adjunctive therapeutic option for severe acetaminophen poisoning when used in combination with NAC to enhance hepatoprotection.

title: Clinical Efficacy of Dienogest for Symptomatic Adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis is a benign gynecologic disorder characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue within the myometrium, causing dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, and chronic pelvic pain while contributing to infertility and adverse obstetric outcomes. The pathophysiology of adenomyosis is driven by estrogen dependence, chronic inflammation, and abnormal uterine peristalsis, making it amenable to progestin-based therapies. Dienogest, a selective progesterone receptor agonist, exerts antiproliferative, anti-inflammatory, and anti-angiogenic effects on ectopic endometrial tissue, leading to suppression of lesion growth and reduction of inflammatory mediators. Clinical evidence demonstrates that dienogest effectively improves dysmenorrhea and heavy menstrual bleeding in approximately 80% and 88% of patients respectively, while significantly reducing uterine volume and normalizing serum CA-125 levels by twelve months of treatment. Dienogest provides equal or greater symptomatic relief compared to other hormonal therapies and represents an important medical alternative to hysterectomy, though symptom improvement does not necessarily correlate with imaging-based improvements in uterine structure.

title: An Overview of Endometriosis and Potential Pharmacogenetic Targets
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease presenting with multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility, which arise from the presence of endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus. The condition develops through several proposed mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia of peritoneal cells, and benign metastasis of endometrial cells via lymphatic vessels involving vascular growth factors VEGF-C and VEGF-D. The disease pathophysiology is driven by a persistent inflammatory microenvironment characterized by elevated peritoneal levels of activated macrophages, interleukins (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8), and nerve growth factor, which are activated by elevated estrogen levels. Multiple therapeutic approaches target endometriosis through distinct mechanisms, including progestogens and selective progesterone receptor modulators that reduce symptoms, GnRH agonists and antagonists (elagolix, relugolix, linzagolix) that suppress gonadotropins, aromatase inhibitors that reduce estrogen synthesis, and NSAIDs that reduce inflammation. Pharmacogenetic variations in cytochrome P450 enzymes—particularly CYP3A4, CYP3A5, CYP2C9, and CYP2C19 polymorphisms—significantly influence the metabolism and efficacy of endometriosis treatments, suggesting that personalized pharmacogenetic profiling could optimize therapeutic outcomes for individual patients.

title: Clinical and endocrine effects of pharmacological therapy in endometriosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic estrogen-dependent disorder characterized by severe pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, with underlying pathophysiology involving progesterone resistance that perpetuates chronic inflammation and impaired decidualization. This meta-analysis demonstrates that multiple pharmacological approaches effectively reduce endometriosis-related symptoms through distinct mechanisms: combined oral contraceptives and progestins inhibit estrogen production and endometrial proliferation while reducing pain and menstrual irregularities; GnRH agonists and antagonists (leuprolide, nafarelin, elagolix, linzagolix, and relugolix) suppress ovarian steroidogenesis to create a hypoestrogenic state that causes ectopic endometrial atrophy; and dienogest provides particularly effective dysmenorrhea relief with lesion size reduction. Adjunctive therapies including levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems, antioxidants, melatonin, and digital therapeutics provide additional pain relief and help prevent disease recurrence following surgery. Emerging agents such as estetrol and relugolix represent newer therapeutic options with favorable efficacy profiles for managing endometriosis-associated pain.

title: Gathering insights about sexual health promotion from adolescents’ adoption of COVID-19-protective behaviors
journal: Health education & behavior : the official publication of the Society for Public Health Education
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how social norms influence adolescent health behaviors by analyzing the relationship between COVID-19-protective behaviors and sexual health practices. Friend and family norms supporting COVID-19 precautions were strongly associated with increased masking and vaccine uptake among adolescents. Notably, both COVID-19 vaccination and masking were positively associated with condom use at last sexual encounter, with vaccinated participants showing over twice the odds of condom use compared to unvaccinated peers. However, neither COVID-protective behaviors nor vaccine uptake showed significant associations with hormonal contraceptive use, despite hormonal contraceptives being commonly prescribed for conditions including endometriosis, irregular bleeding, dysmenorrhea, and acne. These findings underscore that social norms are powerful drivers of adolescent health behaviors across multiple domains, though the mechanisms linking pandemic precautions to barrier contraceptive use versus hormonal contraceptive adoption appear distinct.
